title: Chapter 2: The Rub
content:
Chapter 2
The Rub Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
The Rub
Bob once told a personal story that mocks the fickle footing of the art world and at the same time his own novitiate efforts. I’ll share it with you…
…“Antique …Tonight at ,” read a discrete little sign outside the Christopher Centre. Christine, my wife and I wandered into the large hall. To our bewilderment the auctioneer was in a heated argument with a short heavy-set fellow. Gerry Niessen, the auctioneer was well known to me, but not his pugnacious assailant—a man in his sixties with a flushed round face. His grave concerns were made even more dramatic by his broken English and strong German accent.
After skirting Gerry’s conflict, it became clear that the whole setup was unusual. The discrete venue, the seeming lack of publicity for the extraordinary items on view, and the raucous banter in the entryway; put together it all made for a surreal moment. To our left was a case filled with expensive-looking gold jewelry set proudly atop a black lacquered and heavily carved oriental table. Above the table was an outstanding impressionist painting of cattle. Those gentle creatures condensed from broken brushwork lazily observed us from their mountainous isolation – probably somewhere in .
The argument at the entrance door seemed to be heating up, and becoming progressively one-sided. The mystery man was insisting that the sale be cancelled. Gerry was trying to temper the argument by standing taller, emphasizing further his normally stiff demeanor, and possibly to get enough altitude to remove himself from the embarrassment of the moment. This ploy was effective. The elderly aggressor soon wandered off arm in arm with what looked like his attractive daughter of about thirty.
Other items scattered about the auction room included medieval arms and armor, eighteenth-century German ceramics and pewter, pictures, rare oriental rugs, fifteenth and sixteenth-century religious carvings, very fine quality miscellaneous household items, and a small robot that looked like a miniature version of Expo Ernie from Vancouver’s 1986 World’s Fair.
Analyzing estate auction items can give interesting clues to the background of the former owner, but here the objects were so rare and unusual that I was dumbfounded. To add to my confusion, most of these items would have been at home in a centuries-old European castle and did not seem to mesh with Expo Ernie—the leading edge of technology at the time. What also seemed curiously out of place was a set of contemporary glassware engraved with Hermani-Benz.
Later that evening before the auction began I was able to learn from the auctioneer that the collection was part of an estate that belonged to Rudolph Hermani, a wealthy German who had lived out the last years of his life on an orchard five kilometers from town. As I settled in for the sale, warming the seat in front of me was the stout German who had given the auctioneer such a hard time earlier. I was still perplexed about his female companion—possibly she was his private nurse. The riddle was solved when he leaned over and nuzzled her neck. The auction sale began with a few inconsequential household bits and pieces. As the evening progressed interesting treasures came and went. Three quarters through the sale, my stout neighbor had collapsed into a child-like slumber and his companion was in the process of reviving him. Before long, away they went without offering so much as a single bid on anything. But why had he antagonized Gerry about choosing as the venue for the sale? He had even charged that the sale was too important to be held in , and that it should go back to . Why was he so adamant?
Next on the auction block was an ancient looking panel painting, the size of a book page (see image No. 1). I had viewed it during the preview period but was at a loss to its significance. The back of the piece looked new but the front had the stress of great age. The subject included a strong looking peasant carrying a small naked child on his shoulders. The child lay in an odd horizontal repose and the peasant had the physiognomy not unlike that of my German friend who had just been escorted home to bed. Loose in its frame, it embarrassed its handler by nearly tumbling to the floor. Once everyone regained composure the bidding began. As the porter thrust it towards my corner of the room I was spontaneously drawn to bid, especially since the trifling opening-bid amount was motivation enough.
Soon the little panel painting was knocked down for the price of its broken frame, and I was the new owner. This strange little curiosity sat without judgment for several months in a prominent corner of our front room. On occasion I would bring it to the attention of a visitor, but to no avail. The character of the work was familiar, but as is often the case, images seen years earlier can remain trapped in the memory and influence behavior, but not break consciousness. There it remained, an odd yet remarkable little curiosity waiting for proper recognition.
It turned out that the subject of the painting was the least difficult part of the puzzle; I had seen similar images on pendants growing up and when reviewing art books on Gothic art, my memory was refreshed. It was the burly St. Christopher transporting the baby Jesus on his shoulders. In the biblical story the weight of the child during the river crossing became crushing for the Saint. The incredulity of the Saint about the Godly importance of his charge was soon erased by this profound miracle. It was a popular Christian image north of the before Martin Luther’s Reformation of the early sixteenth century. In more recent times, images of this patron Saint have been carried as a talisman to provide safe passage. Was it providence that St. Christopher was found at the , a hall that was named in the Saint’s honour? After the sale I learned more about Rudolph Hermani; he had been associated with Mercedes Benz and his estranged wife was of Benz lineage. Thus the mystery of the glass tableware etched with Hermani-Benz was solved. This former business mogul, I was in due course apprised, had descended from the Krupp industrialists of . He had lived in his safe haven on for a number of years with little fanfare. His new home was likely a necessary getaway from the former responsibilities and pressures of the old country.
Strange renovations included: a complicated flight path down to his fortified boat launch, massive oil storage bunkers, and double locks on every door—even the interior ones. This German gentleman must have imported some of the fears prevalent in at the time. It was long after WWII, but activists were still terrorizing high-ranking aristocrats and industrialists. In contrast, the history and lifestyle of the quiet Okanagan valley should have been far removed from such fear.
Shortly after he passed away, the Hermani orchard was put up for sale. The wayward treasures had been waiting in to make their trans-Atlantic voyage (a voyage that never took place). In a mysterious fire that blazed endlessly with the fury of Hades, the house, bowling alley, and bunkers of oil burned, and burned. Christine and I recall pondering over the inferno and enduring trail of black smoke that took everything with it. It was somewhat of a miracle that the antiques had been taken out beforehand.
The St. Christopher painting had suffered much. There was considerable over-painting which gave the hulking figure in the foreground a more ominous look than his bulk had intended. The eyes were distorted and mask-like; and in a piteous attempt by some unknown restorer to strengthen areas such as the hands, the image looked as though a less than talented amateur had painted it. The baby perched atop the rounded shoulders of the Saint was the only visual relief. It offset the dark drama of the image—lying in a horizontal position of questionable stability as the Saint negotiated the turbulent water. To add to the sublime image, ominous clouds boiled overhead while a jagged escarpment seemed to close in on the figures—its overhang covered with root-bare vegetation.
Occasionally I would pick it up and scour it visually. One day it was almost like a Zen awakening as my eyes focused on an understated mark in the lower left-hand corner. Tangled in the dark undergrowth at the edge of the river was a capital A surmounting a capital D. One visitor jokingly suggested the monogram AD might be that of Albrecht Dürer—considered by most to be the greatest of German painters! The fellow that made the comment was from and took his own suggestion lightly; a painting by Dürer away from the wall of a great museum would be impossible in modern times. But considering its provenance—anything was possible. Rudolph Hermani’s family had direct links to Nuremberg, Dürer’s hometown, and because he had other priceless treasures—why not a Dürer!
Bound
Within the month, I was bound with my tiny panel dressed with its few grams of priceless oil paint. I had made arrangements to meet Gregory Martin of Christie's, then one of the most experienced auction house representatives in the field of old master paintings. Heathrow airport was busy as the passengers awkwardly made their way through customs. After declaring my prize, St. Christopher, I was shunted into a waiting area where the customs officials had me unpack my treasure. It was declared to be worth a baronet’s ransom. A number of them wandered around the Saint; this was clearly out of curiosity more than official duty. After several minutes of muffled mirth implying disbelief, they sent me on my way buried with luggage and clutching my panel.
This is an excerpt. For the full chapter, read Art World’s Dirty Little Secret.

title: Chapter 3: Frosty's Nemesis
content:
Chapter 3
Frosty’s Nemesis Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
Frosty’s Nemesis
Bob my present owner started keeping a journal of his odd and not so wonderful art world experiences. He’s not the type one might expect to take on such a task. In fact, he’s a solitary sort of guy living in the middle of what seemed like nowhere when I first arrived. In any case here are a few of his notes about how he learned of me. One last thing! Don’t mind the bits of scribble in the margin, he fancies himself a seer.
Journal … It was late morning and time for a break from tending the gallery [art and antique]. I put my book down to pop outside for some fresh air. During the winter months’ business was slow and reading had become the biggest part of my day. An acquaintance that knew of my interest in old paintings wandered up and started telling of his “great find.” Although he was reservedly enthusiastic, it was obvious he was trying to find a buyer. He said it was a painting by Turner and that it was presently being used as a study piece for the art students at the in . My first question was, “Which Turner?” When told it was John Turner, I returned the volley, “Which John Turner?”
My enthusiasm was at low ebb. When he articulated, J.M.W, my whole perspective changed. Joseph Mallord William Turner (1775-1851). Frosty’s J.M.W. pronouncement seemed unlikely. As this somewhat disheveled looking fellow continued, my imagination was trying to make a connection between his appearance and what might be the owner of a precious work of art. In all fairness, Frosty came by his look honestly. He had just returned from retrieving a new addition for his auto wrecking yard. Later I learned of his ability to maneuver a wreck onto the back of a flatbed truck with little more than brute physical force—his own.
Frosty gave a short description of the painting, “It’s eight feet long. A lifeboat full of people, beside a long pier, is just about to be swamped. There are people in the water and a ship sinking in the distance.” He then gave a scant explanation of where it came from; something to do with the law courts, but the details proved somewhat mysterious and I got the feeling that he wanted to keep it that way.
Depending on the true author, the price he wanted for the picture was either far too low or far too high. Frosty was asking a knight’s ransom for a pauper—100,000 dollars. It is an uphill struggle to gain support for a moderately important painting that has inadequate documentation, and as for a major piece by one of the world’s greatest masters, forget it. At eight feet wide, it was not a picture that could be lost in an attic for a century only to be found during a search for Halloween costumes.
Whatever its history, it had to be an unusual one, and although Frosty told me that he had commissioned a professional appraisal a few years earlier, which claimed it was worth three times his asking price, he still could not tell me anything of the painting’s origin. If painted by J.M.W. Turner, why was this seemingly dispossessed painting, this “orphan”, not catalogued as one of the artist’s accepted works? Was it the object of professional condemnation or even more problematic— fake, or stolen? This worry faded as Frosty wandered off. The painting was probably bogus anyway, and in my mind the matter was closed. Back to more mundane concerns… End of Journal entry…
By the bye, I should formally introduce you to my new caretaker. His name is Robert but most people call him Bob. Why he’s been mucking around with pictures that are out of his league is beyond me.
He’s intercepted a number of fallen orphans (as he names us) on our way to purgatory (as I call it). As a result of an opinion given in haste, inaccurate interpretation by a trusted potentate or for other reasons—an owner can understandably lose faith in his or her painting. Peter Watson in his book, Manet to Manhattan confirms that great paintings are always sneaking through salerooms masquerading as something else. For many dealers this is part of the excitement of the art trade.
By highlighting one particular case of mistaken identity, Watson disparages the highest echelon at Christie’s… “The Veronese-El Greco, however, was more than just another lost Old Master,” he wrote. “The fact that it had been misattributed by the chairman of the company was symptomatic of the low point to which Christie’s had sunk…” I didn’t actually read this. I overheard Bob talking about it. But from what I’ve seen and heard in the past, more insidious reasons beyond incompetence and apathy for making a bad assessment of a painting can include: bias, self-interest, jealousy, legal fears, corruption, or just plain greed. Some of these I’ve witnessed firsthand. What about the “quick draw” art evaluation, the one that most non-confidants receive when searching for a knowledgeable opinion? If one sends a
photograph of the object, and a photo is the only thing available for the expert to interpret, his or her task is nay on impossible. An improper interpretation might be as much a product of inadequate information as anything else. Even with the item in hand the challenge often goes beyond the time available for a proper appraisal. Bob recently sent a professional large-format black and white photograph, plus a colour transparency of a grey colour-wash drawing to the Wildenstein Institute in Paris. He assumed from the images that it would be easily recognized as an aqueous-based medium. Hah! It was not until the picture was sent to Guy Wildenstein in for final assessment that they realized it was not an oil painting at all. From the photo they thought it was grisaille oil on paper. This is the “long lost painting” or prototype of the catalogued print. The exact rendition of the print attests to this conclusion. It would be impossible to do a colour wash drawing (watercolour), precisely, using a print to copy from… but it is quite possible to do an etching or engraving copying a colour wash drawing. Walter Bayes, the professional watercolorist cum Turner biographer: “With water colour you never quite foresee the result, it is a matter of accident… it snaps a decision long before most painters have really done what they meant.”
François Boucher “Le moineau apprivoisé” engraved by René Gaillard The shortage of time, and the trouble involved in seriously analyzing a ‘piece’ discourages many experts from giving a proper analysis in the first place. It is also good to remember that in many cases an expert’s rank or post might substantially exceed his or her abilities. I once heard Bob blurt out in frustration. It was a grandiose statement just after he’d received a letter back from the Tate Gallery. “Connoisseurship cannot be granted by appointment, taken by political influence, or usurped by the power of wealth. Connoisseurship must be earned.”
The next time Bob heard from Frosty he had just returned from with me. “Frosty’s eight-foot would-be Turner,” is how Bob phrased it. Nonetheless Frosty persuaded him to visit his home where I was safely tucked away in a shipping crate. Frosty propped my reinforced plywood container against the side of his house and busily started undoing the large latches that held things together. At the same time, he was making intermittent sweeps with his foot in order to put the run on a persistent little dog. Fido was set on marking me as his own. Soon the cover was down and plastic vapor-barrier drawn back. The moderating sun of an otherwise cold day lit up my surface with an alarming glare. It was almost painful for Bob as he squinted with a grimace. I felt great! He adjusted my position trying to make out details. Here, I’ll let Bob tell it in his own words. The way he wrote it in his journal…
…“It is a dark picture of what looks like survivors in a rescue boat being tossed around by violent waves—all crammed together were sailors, mothers and children; some huddled, others braced against the deadly elements. This first impression was not great. The darkness of the pigments, lack of detail, tremulous subject matter—and the glare! With poor coordination and a couple of expletives, we got the painting turned stretcher-side up, while much to his wife’s dismay, Frosty finally made contact with the busy little dog. She removed this leaking, and now yelping distraction to the safety of her kitchen while Frosty and I continued our assault on the picture.
I found the back was more interesting than the front; this is a terrible thing to admit, and falls into the same disrespectful realm as discussing the quality of a frame while neglecting the painting. I was only somewhat familiar with Turner’s more popular works, and the painting now before me did not have a characteristic Turner feel. On the stretcher there were a few old auction numbers, a label with an inscription, a tag with a number, and a confusion of other marks. These at least indicated that others had previously taken the work seriously.
I was not yet proselytized and ready to sing its praise but was certainly more interested after seeing it in the flesh as it were. The old inscription quilled on the vintage label read: “Shipwreck, the Rescue, J.M.W. Turner R.A.,” and the faded ink stencil mark, 73 OAS was typical of that used by a leading British auction firm. The natural shrinkage in the wood of the stretcher frame had caused the label to bulge in places. This, along with its patina led me to believe that the label was as old as the nineteenth century stretcher.
Shipwreck, the Rescue, label on stretcher This is an excerpt. For the full chapter, read Art World’s Dirty Little Secret.

title: Chapter 4: Other End of the Paintbrush
content:
Chapter 4
Other End of the Paintbrush Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
Other End of the Paintbrush
Bob’s Journal… Looking back previous to the Aldergrove auction sale, a newspaper had written much about Shipwreck, the Rescue, and most of it was controversial. One of the articles quoted a well-known art dealer from Vancouver who disparaged the work on grounds that an important painting such as this was unlikely to be discovered locally. “I just despair” he said, “of people going to auction, with everyone thinking they are going to get a deal. I strongly advise people, ‘Buyer beware.’ I’m going out to see the painting myself tomorrow, but I can tell you that there is about as much chance of it being a genuine Turner as there is of it being a Leonardo da Vinci.”
Skeptical indeed! To take the liberty and paraphrase this prominent dealer: why would a rare painting be here, in this part of the world and unclaimed? The answer is simple: artwork can act as a tangible asset for those fleeing political strife, or those just wanting to move wealth to different parts of the world. Calamity in and of itself can dispossess just about anything. In addition, information about the history of artwork is often lost or distorted over generations of single-family ownership, and estate sales frequently catalogue such items poorly when they eventually come onto the market. It is not difficult to understand why an important lost painting would end up in a cosmopolitan centre like .
Even before the turn of the century, many of ’s finest art treasures made their way across the . They had been delivered to this ‘unruly’ land by the most famous art dealers that ever lived—dealers who were reacting to huge fortunes being amassed by the industrial moguls of the day. These billionaires (by present standards) wanted to gather some of the old world culture under their wing and they made purchases from connoisseurs such as Duveen, Wildenstein, Seligmann, Gimple, Knoedler, and Durand-Ruel. An enormous number of European cultural treasures started moving west across the by the third quarter of the nineteenth century. Frank Herrmann commented on how effectively “the antique exporters from in particular, and in general, had done their job in the era between 1870 and 1930, when had become wealthy and had become poor. Only the best had been good enough.” End journal…
The morning after I had been securely deposited in dark solitude (which turned out to be Bob’s garage), he was back in the gallery, reading another biography about my creator, J.M.W. Turner R.A. Much of what I tell you now I learned later… As he sat trying to identify with the fury of Turner’s Snow Storm, he was distracted twice more by the news media. Many of their questions echoed those of the previous day, "What are you planning to do next and how will you determine if it is truly by the famous Turner?" They wanted something newsworthy so he played along as best he could. He parroted his previous remarks from the evening before. About the importance of determining scientifically if the painting came from Turner’s time period, etcetera, etcetera, blab, blab. His truer plan for the immediate future was to become completely familiar with Turner’s work. Then he could confidently go ahead with those same expensive tests he was promising the media.
To complete the necessary scientific tests a few problems had to be resolved anyway. The most frustrating was the lack of facilities available that could do the job. At the time it was Bob’s understanding that the only place in capable of accomplishing the full spectrum of tests, The Canadian Conservation Institute (CCI), limited itself mainly to institutional work. The cost of roughly $20,000 for a comprehensive analysis seemed like a foolish expenditure unless he was absolutely confident about the painting’s authorship.
Page after page, Bob lived Turner’s life vicariously—what a terribly committed artist! “He feared that social entanglements would divert him from the business of painting.” His level of commitment as an artist was too great to allow for marriage; and even though he had a couple of serious relationships, little is known of these romances. I could add plenty, but it might not be proper to discuss here. All I can say is that I lived under the sly old wizard’s roof for half a century and plenty of torrid stuff went on within ear shot. He was a most proper gent but JMW liked the ladies. What intrigue might I have witnessed if it weren’t for being stacked behind so many early starts and unsold canvases? I’m sure you already know about the disheveled state of his studio. And the cats, my god! It’s not much fun being close to the floor around a bunch of toms. Well, I shouldn’t bore you with complaints.
JMW’s tours at home, in , and abroad were frequent and extensive. I remember him being gone for weeks and sometimes even months at a stretch. Unfortunately, few itineraries survive. The best records are his sketches en route and the limited number of his letters that are still around. Turner’s first two biographers, Thornbury and Hamerton both confirm the dearth of Turner correspondence. On the basis of rarity alone, Thornbury assigned his letters great value. Hamerton, along with many subsequent biographers, mocked Turner’s literacy: “Like most uneducated men, he disliked letter-writing, and he carried this dislike to a degree involving positive discourtesy to others.”
How could Hamerton so confidently declare Turner discourteous? A bit harsh and arrogant wouldn’t you say? There might have been the odd recorded faux pas made by the preoccupied and possibly forgetful artist, but many letters are known to have been lost or destroyed. Rev. Trimmer, Turner’s close friend and one with whom he often corresponded, unwarily incinerated many. There was also Turner’s eccentric attitude toward the multitude of formal invitations he received. Letters remained unopened on Turner’s table for months. “They only want my autograph,” he used to say.
Turner’s father was pleased with the “edycation” he was able to afford for his son in an age when few but the elite were formally educated. Turner certainly had a comfortable measure of humanity and his attendant modesty and introspection is well illustrated in an 1845 letter to Charles Robert Cockerell. He begs pardon from Cockerell’s wife: “I beg her pardon for being so disorderly and deserve expulsion.”
Much of Turner’s graphic note-taking from his travels stayed in his gallery on Queen Anne Street along with the rest of us pictures. Eventually most of us “deadwood” made it to the Tate. The Turner Bequest they call it, hah. I’m sure glad I was eventually sold off. At least I had a bit of a life. Nonetheless it’s interesting to hear what those little colour-notations are now bringing in the marketplace. Many of them were only a few strokes of watercolour over pencil scribbles. The price has sky rocketed beyond reason. In 2002, Sotheby’s gave an estimate on a wistful and simple one, barely a picture, but 150,000-200,000 pounds made it a very expensive stain indeed. And as for those finished watercolours by Turner that never made it to the Tate; they have changed hands for as much as 2.04 million pounds (2001)—not a sum that would facilitate forming a scrapbook full.
They say that Turner was a good money manager and became wealthy in his own lifetime. His wealth did come largely from picture sales. Funny thing is, at the end of it all when the old wizard passed away most of his great artworks (including me) were not included in his Bequest to the Nation. They’d been sold throughout his long active career.
It’s the Clore Gallery that holds the Turner Bequest as part of the Tate Gallery holdings. The Tate officials understandably want their institution to be seen as the greatest repository of Turner treasure. In the process they’ve managed to dominate the world of Turner, the staff and devotees almost exclusively referee any potential new discovery. According to Bob, herein lays a conflict of interest and the first thread in the complex veil of deception inherent in the art authentication process.
Turner was a solitary worker, and while he was creating large masterpieces, he never enjoyed the labour of student helpers, as did many of his colleagues. This accounts for the uniqueness of his work, and removes him from the common argument that haunts many other artists—whether a picture was entirely by the artist’s hand or done as a collaboration with students. Those who would like to limit the oeuvre of certain artists would argue that a questionable work was done entirely by a student or follower. When an artist has a stable of underlings it can become difficult to differentiate the work of a talented student from that of his or her master. Thus the influential skeptic for whatever furtive reason can exclude and condemn such a work offhand. The solitary and secretive Turner has spared the Turner-scholar this particular challenge. However, there is one other creative ploy available for those with the desire to mask the truth of new Turner art discoveries—the invention of hitherto unknown artists. This practice has had a long history that goes back at least to Bernard Berenson and The Italian Painters of the Renaissance. Berenson’s creations were not meant to muddle the truth though, but only to establish repositories for stylistic similarities. The artists he was “creating” were from a time that few art-biographical records were kept, and therefore, out of necessity much of his classifying included invention. We will soon be introduced to a raft of mystery-artists invented in order to double for Turner.
Exceptions to Turner’s secretive working method came later in his career, on what were called varnishing days. These days came just prior to the exhibition and were meant to allow time for final touchups. Turner, who had been

title: Chapter 5: Visnei, Provenance or...
content:
Chapter 5
Visnei, Provenance or… Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
Visnei, Provenance or
Bob’s been scribbling again… In business parlance, rarity equates to increased monetary value; in a museum context it equates to greater prestige. Stealthy management of art resources elevates the influence of expert opinion and those involved in giving it. For example, when much of an artist’s ‘body of work’ is either ignored or condemned surreptitiously… Enough of that! This quasi-conspiratorial tone is just like Bob. I suggest instead that much of this dark imagery can be laid to rest by proposing something much less sinister. It’s a paradigm shift, in a way. With my model most participants play out their roles without fully understanding the implications of their actions. Most are just dumb followers of what is going on, of course some have vested interests but most are just plain oblivious!
“ is three time zones away. Let’s see, that would make it shortly after …” Bob sat muttering to himself attempting to get his thoughts together. He wanted to field a few questions with a conservation scientist who had worked for the Canadian Conservation Institute (CCI) in for a number of years. She was now operating a related business in Toronto, Marilyn Laver, P. Eng.
Bob was stunned to learn that back in 1982, she had worked as the chief conservation scientist charged with heading up scientific testing on the very painting he had phoned about—me! I could have told him that. The Mounties (RCMP) and Interpol had sent me to her for scientific analysis in the course of a previous investigation.
This bizarre discovery left Bob with many unanswered questions. Rather than searching only for art provenance, he now felt part of a covert scheme. One nagging question he’d been haunted with was now partially answered. It was to do with a strange call he received shortly after buying me, and just after talking to Mr. Dowding, the lawyer. An assertive voice demanded, “Be careful what you do with the painting you bought on Sunday; I’m friends with the RCMP member, Montague; the one who did a criminal investigation involving it twenty years ago. The owner went to prison!” Bob shook his head. He later confided to a friend, “I don’t know who it was but by the intonation of her voice she could have been teamed-up quite comfortably with Maxwell Smart of 1960s TV fame.” Dumbfounded at the harebrained provocation, and with confidence fortified by his recent discussion with Mr. Dowding, he boldly rebuked her, “That’s not true. Nobody went to jail.” The phone went dead!
What was the reason for trying to confuse and intimidate Bob? Not only had this farce raised his curiosity, it had also raised his ire. He chastised himself on subsequent occasions for not getting the caller’s number. “I could have demanded a curtain call,” he lamented.
In short order, Marilyn Laver sent Bob a cost estimate to cover her time and expenses for gathering fragments of data from the previous tests done on me. The sum was minor compared to what an analysis from scratch would have cost. For this he thanked “the people of .” It was their tax dollars that paid for the work done on me two decades earlier.
In 1982, the investigation of a Mr. Visnei was wrapped up instantly after he fled the country. I, the subject of the investigation, was somehow being used as bail collateral at the time, and after the collapse of the proceedings I was hurriedly packed in my crate and shipped back to . Consequently, the Canadian Conservation Institute (CCI) never completed a full report on me, and because the defendant had skipped bail and disappeared, all forty square feet of me was liquidated at one of those police auctions in . It was an attempt to recover some of the trial expenses.
The months wore on and Marilyn Laver went about her task, traveling back and forth between and collating her files from the former investigation. Because of her earlier tenure with the CCI, she was considered a colleague and had a comfortable working relationship with them. At times she would send a photograph of me: one time, black and white, another ultraviolet, then infrared, and eventually a very professional normal colour photograph. They were all in large format and were copies of those taken at the time of the investigation.
Ultra violet photograph of Shipwreck, the Rescue It was comforting that the process was moving along at a steady pace. Bob took great interest in the scientific techniques used in gathering the necessary data for the investigation, partly because of his vested interests, but also because of his own degree in science. had a sophisticated research capability even back then when scientific studies on works of art were at a formative stage internationally. The CCI was first established in 1972.
Marilyn sent Bob the odd pamphlet and academic paper relating to Turner’s standard working methods. These were published from studies done subsequent to the 1982 investigation, and ironically, they were published by the Tate Gallery in , which at the time of the investigation had little to share with the CCI.
Meantime I stood like an encapsulated fossil at the back of Bob’s garage. Packed tightly in my sarcophagus of plywood and Styrofoam—alone. Those were trying times. After about six months of entombment I received an unlikely visitor. An out-of-town friend stopped for an overnight visit with Bob and after dinner he insisted on seeing the “would-be Turner.” Because it took two people to wrestle me from my crate and into the light, Bob was more than willing to take him up on his offer.
His helper was G. T. W. Edwards, an anthropologist by dedication and a pharmacist by profession. William’s keen sense of observation came from years of studying and cataloguing Native American herbal remedies, customs, and artifacts at the remote Bella Coola Reservation, British Columbia. The native studies had led him towards a D. Phil. in community medicine at . They cracked my crate, just a hair. “Ahh,” the fresh breeze was intoxicating. Like a methodical automaton moving with stout linear ease, William gradually made his way around my shipping crate. He was a middle-aged graceful man of manners and culture, but seemed somewhat slowed by his gentleness. Dry as parchment, his keen wit bubbled up and broke the surface only on occasion.
They dragged me and my crate from the garage and into the sunlight of mid-afternoon. The day was as bright as it was at Frosty’s, about a year ago when Bob first saw me, but it was considerably warmer. Behind my back William paused—silently. Soon he started snapping pictures of my stretcher, including a new discovery. His keen eye had registered a tiny two-centimetre fragment of paper on the top left corner of my stretcher. Darkened with age, the “air-burn” as it is often called had camouflaged it during previous scrutiny. On the corner that remained of this unpretentious scrap was printed, “THOS. (?)…” It was not until later that a possible connection to a previous owner would be suspected. This added one more interesting piece to the growing puzzle. Uncovering this artifact made William’s day, and as Bob wrenched the last clasp closed and the two of them struggled my crate back into the garage, William hummed with self-satisfied contentment.
Bob finally got motivated to track down the court documents promised by Mr. Dowding, the lawyer. He had given him his home phone number in Maple Ridge, just outside . His phone rang many times and just as Bob was about to give up, a vaporous voice answered. After several clues, Mr. Dowding finally remembered Bob. Frightening indeed! He always believed that when the time was right, he could approach Mr. Dowding and prod him for the key to unlock his part of my mysterious history. By now, it seemed as though he was having difficulty getting the key out of his pocket, never mind into the lock. He was getting older and the provocation required to prompt his recollection of their previous conversation assured Bob that his call was none too soon.
Alternatively, it was assumed that if unable to get the necessary information from Mr. Dowding, the Freedom of Information Act would ensure access to the necessary documents. But this reasoning appeared flawed. According to a local crown prosecutor court records are periodically destroyed—purged every three years! Mr. Dowding was all Bob had! To discover what happened in court twenty years ago must become his main focus and it needed doing before the inevitable loss of his only source of information.
During his first conversation with Mr. Dowding, Bob was assured that there were pertinent documents somewhere in his files. It gave comfort hearing him repeat this same claim. Bob called back several times hoping to hear good news, but ultimately, his records were not to be found. Bob’s sinking feeling was buoyed slightly when he volunteered to go down to the courthouse and look for documents. The journey never took place! The only thing Bob accomplished with his persistent calling was to aggravate the tired old gent.
Two months later and much to Bob’s surprise he received a call. Mr. Dowding had been clearing clutter from his home office and came across the court document for the examination and cross-examination of Montague, the agent provocateur. He gave another elevating and crucial snippet of information during the short talk; information that dealt with a subpoenaed auction house representative who stated during the trial that, “Shipwreck, the Rescue was sold in their sales room.”
According to Mr. Dowding other information was given at the time confirming that I had first come from the estate of one of Turner’s executors. He did not have any supporting papers, but assured Bob that to the best of his recollections it was true. With renewed enthusiasm Bob opted to overlook the lawyer’s previous failure of memory, and accepted this as a turning

title: Chapter 6: Interpol, Mounties, and the Hapless Sting
content:
Chapter 6
Interpol, Mounties, and the Hapless Sting Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
Interpol, Mounties, and the Hapless Sting
Bob spent the next two days reading and re-reading the section of the trial documents Dowding rescued from his cluttered office. It was the segment where the undercover agent was examined and cross-examined. This was also the same RCMP member about whom Bob received that mysterious and menacing phone call just after buying me.
The questions were repetitive and dry, but by the end he had a clear image of what happened over the duration of the investigation. My unfortunate owner at the time, on thin suspicion of wrongdoing had been subjected to the full force of police high jinks, until finally—or so it seemed—he was coaxed to commit an offence. In a scattergun approach at gathering evidence, the Mounties commissioned tests on five of Visnei’s paintings, including myself. We were all labeled as potential forgeries. The other accused included a Gainsborough, a Picasso, and two Collins’. Alas, Visnei, worried over the outcome of these bizarre machinations, skipped the country.
The problems for Visnei started when he introduced yours truly to a doctor in . Having recently been disheartened by an unsatisfactory deal with an acquaintance of Visnei, the incredulous doctor instigated the investigation by reporting misgivings about Visnei and me to the RCMP. Once the locomotive was stoked and Visnei was bound to the tracks, the police made their run.
To negotiate my sale Visnei met the agent for the first time at Doctor Crow’s suite. The second confab culminated with Montague, Crow, and Visnei heading out of town to a large bungalow where I was stored. The cloak and dagger mischief reached its zenith as the agent’s provocative banter stimulated Visnei to expand the program. Their negotiations advanced to include another painting that was being held as collateral for a 600,000 dollar loan at a Zurich bank—The Last Supper by El Greco. Visnei wanted to retire his loan on it by bumping the deal to include both me and El Greco for a cool two million dollars.
During this international-flavoured negotiation, inaccurate currency conversions took place and the interpretation of documents in foreign languages became a real problem. In fact, according to the accused, some very relevant documents eventually disappeared from police custody. There were still two credentials; the El Greco ones in Spanish, and my document in German. Neither had been translated yet, or if so, it was certainly denied under oath:
Dowding (D) questioned the RCMP agent, Cpl. Montague (M):
So, as far as the El Greco is concerned, have you any information on it at all, as to its validity or otherwise?
M. No, Your Honour, I don’t. D. But you did have documents you could have had translated which are exhibits here that appear to authenticate an El Greco, [the] Last Supper.
M. Well –
D. Did you have it translated?
M. I never had a translator.
D. Has anybody else done so?
M. Not to my knowledge, no.
D. Has anyone ever given them to a Spanish speaking person to interpret?
M. I can’t answer that, Your Honour, I don’t know.
D. And same with the German documents of authenticity; did anyone translate those, and do you have copies of any translations?
M. No, Your Honour, I can’t answer that question; I just don’t have that knowledge.
It might have been wise before proceeding with a full-blown sting and prosecution, for the agents to get these relevant documents translated; they were crucial papers and the foundation on which Visnei was supporting the claims of authenticity. It is not as though translation was out of the RCMP’s scope; John Sawatshy’s bestseller Men in the Shadows describes their facility as more than adequate. “The functions of E Section (Electronic Surveillance) proper are routine enough, it has responsibility for transcribing wiretapped and bugged conversations for investigators and analysts to study. Many of the targets are foreigners and often the conversations need translation, so E section is filled with translators and stenographers.” By the way, paintings like me can’t actually read, but Bob has a tendency to read aloud on occasion and I have big ears. This is an excerpt. For the full chapter, read Art World’s Dirty Little Secret.

title: Chapter 7: The Implacable Judgment
content:
Chapter 7
The Implacable Judgment Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
The Implacable Judgment
Visnei had skipped the country to parts unknown…
It was fortunate that Mr. Dowding had the foresight to save what trial information he did in the summer of 1982. It had been so long since the trial that all other copies were lost to the shredder! Another positive discovery that Graham made with the help of Professor Bruce McDougal from the UBC Faculty of Law was the file number and thus the judgment transcript from 1982.
In the County Court of regarding Her Majesty The Queen Against Franz Visnei, His Honour gave his reasons for judgment (excerpt):
“Franz Visnei is charged with two counts of attempted fraud, one count involving a painting said to be by Turner and the other count involving a painting said to be by Picasso. I find Visnei guilty on both counts.
“The chronology of events giving rise to these charges has been outlined by a number of crown witnesses. During 1980, Dr. T. N. Crow had purchased a painting said to be by Gainsborough from one Siegfried Norton, a rather shadowy character who features in the evidence of a number of Crown witnesses. He changed his name to Roger Norten and some time before the denouncement of the circumstances alleged by the Crown he made his departure from to take up residence in . In December 1981, Norton [sic] was in touch with Dr. Crow offering to sell another painting, this one by Turner. Then in February 1982, Dr. Crow received a telephone call from Visnei who introduced himself as a friend of Norten and who expressed an interest in purchasing the Gainsborough. Again, in April 1981[2], Visnei telephoned Dr. Crow this time offering to sell a painting by Turner. By now, however, Dr. Crow had become suspicious and called the R.C.M.P. As a result, it was arranged that Corporal Peter Montague of the commercial crime division should pose as a wealthy friend of Dr. Crow who was seeking a type of investment through which he could hide his earnings of several millions of dollars from the income tax authorities…”
“The various references made by Visnei to the Turner may be summarized in very short form as follows: that he purchased the Turner from Sotheby’s in London in 1979 for £475,000 (about $2,500,000 in Canadian funds); that he was prepared to sell for $2 million as he was in need of funds to clear the title to property in Langley he had recently purchased from Roger Norten. And as to the Picasso, Visnei stated that it was owned by Baroness Jane von Einem of a very old German family who was prepared to sell the Picasso at a very reasonable price, as she was moving. There was, in addition, a very curious telephone call made by Visnei to Mrs. von Einem from which it appeared that she was prepared to accept $150,000 for the Picasso. “It is on these representations that the Crown bases its case. Simply put, the Crown says that these representations were false; that Visnei knew they were false; that he deliberately made these representations to create a false impression as to the value of the two paintings; and that he did so in an attempt to dishonestly deprive Corporal Montague. “In support of its allegations, the Crown tendered a formidable body of evidence from persons who had dealings with the accused during the period alleged in the indictment…” “Louis Peter Nuber, an art dealer of considerable international experience and a resident of Vancouver since 1971, told how he had been introduced to Visnei in the fall of 1981 by Norten as a man who was said to own or have access to sources of art worth some $50 million. Visnei showed Nuber photographs of a number of paintings including the Picasso mentioned in the indictment. As a result of this introduction and several subsequent meetings with Visnei, Nuber was persuaded to go to in the hope that he might be able to purchase some of the works of art, which Visnei was offering for sale. In November 1981, Nuber and his partner made a fruitless trip to virtually chasing Visnei and searching him out in , , , , and . Back in , Nuber accepted an invitation from Norten to view the Turner. Here he met Visnei again who stated he had purchased the Turner and that it was worth $2 or $3 million. In view of his European experience, however, Nuber was not interested in the Turner. “Jane von Einem a writer and entrepreneur, living in and having business interests in was called as a witness. She stated, incidentally, that she had married Baron von Einem but did not consider herself a baroness and did not use the title. More significantly, she stated that she had been introduced to Visnei in the fall of 1981 by Roger Norten. Initially her discussions with Visnei were in connection with a process for the liquefaction of coal developed by an Italian scientist who Visnei claimed to represent…” “Several months later Visnei arrived in her office with the Picasso and asked to leave it there, as he had to leave the city. She agreed. And then, several weeks later Visnei sent two more paintings to be left in her office. In due course, Visnei returned and in later discussions he asked if she knew anyone who could give him a loan on the security of the Picasso so he could invest in her mining business. She referred him to Robert Kemeny. Then one Saturday morning, several weeks later, Visnei came to her office and picked up the several paintings and she was surprised that Visnei had telephoned her later in the day asking about the price of the Picasso when he always had said it was worth $200,000. On the following Monday, he told Mrs. Von Einem that he had been in touch with a millionaire who had bought the Turner for $2 million and that he had to go out to make a “statement” in connection with the sale. Mrs. Von Einem further testified that she never felt that Picasso was hers to deal with. “Robert Kemeny also described the meeting with Visnei at Norten’s farm when Visnei showed him the Turner he was offering for sale. Visnei also produced a number of papers and a photocopy of a portion of a Sotheby’s catalogue describing the painting. Visnei did not indicate who the owner was, or the price expected, but he did say that a painting by Turner would be worth between $1 and $2 million. Visnei talked to Kemeny some four or five times up until April asking him to make an offer on the Turner but Kemeny declined. Kemeny also said that after the trip to Norten’s farm he saw the Picasso in Mrs. von Einem’s office and when talking with Visnei he said he would expect an offer of about $500,000 for the Picasso. “The last witness called by the Crown was Herbert Mostyn Richard Jones, for the last eight years the security manager for Sotheby Parke Bernet & Co., , and before that an officer of the Metropolitan Police. In this position he testified that he had access to, and was familiar with the records kept by Sotheby’s in the regular course of their business. Indeed, he brought a prodigious number of these records to court. He explained in detail the procedure of Sotheby’s from the receipt of goods through to sale and shipment to the purchaser… the Turner was sold to Dr. Horst Rudloff of … The records further indicate that the painting was shipped to Dr. Rudloff on … “Visnei gave evidence on his own behalf. The principle matters he dealt with may be summarized as follows: From the very beginning of his dealings with Montague, Visnei said that he requested an interpreter be present as his command of the English language was not adequate. However, he says this request was denied by Montague who did not wish any additional persons to know of his attempt to hide his “black” money from the tax authorities. The tape recordings of his conversations with Crow and Montague may indicate that his English was far from perfect but it was certainly adequate for meaningful conversation…
It is Visnei’s evidence that the tapes have been altered, tampered with or edited or are incomplete and as a result do not accurately record his dealings with Montague and Crow. It was his evidence that the tapes had been deliberately tampered with to manufacture the lie that he stated that he had purchased the Turner for £475,000. Both Montague and Crow, however, stated that the tape accurately recorded the conversation…
(c) There is also a suggestion in Visnei’s defense that the search warrant held by the police when they seized the paintings in question and the other material from the Langley residence on Monday, May 4, 1982, was somehow deficient. Whether or not that may have been the case, however is of little consequence as the essence of the case against the accused is his deceitful intent.
Visnei stated that amongst the papers he had at his residence was a colour photograph of the Turner painting to which was attached an opinion of a German professor as to its value. These papers were not among those introduced into evidence by the prosecution and have never been found since. I gather that it is Visnei’s position that these papers, if produced, would be evidence of the value and authenticity of the Turner and they have been deliberately withheld by the police. Corporal Roberts, who took possession of all the articles seized, denied that any such papers were included…
(e) Visnei gives a curious account of how he came into possession of the Turner.
This is an excerpt. For the full chapter, read Art World’s Dirty Little Secret.

title: Chapter 8: Art-Science Reverie
content:
Chapter 8
Art-Science Reverie Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
Art-Science Reverie
Now what’s Bob scribbling?
“Connoisseurship takes experience and education and is necessary to differentiate genuine original paintings from later versions; including collaborative efforts between master and pupil, copies done by studio assistants, contemporary followers, late followers, or even forgers. The false premise that the archetype is immeasurably more important has of course led to a greater reliance on the ‘art expert.’ This is regardless of the expert’s connoisseurship. Scholarship must necessarily be combined with proper science or whatever additional corroborative evidence to ensure that the art collector/consumer is protected from error in judgment or outright dishonesty by crook or courtier…” How in the world does that tie in with My story? I’m sure it must or else it wouldn’t be on his mind right now…We’ll see.
Turner, the old wizard! He was affected as much as anyone by advances in science and technology. A real Renaissance man he was… a zealous and intelligent student of the movement towards exact knowledge. I wondered at times if he wasn’t a member of one of those esoteric societies, like Francis Bacon started. The irony is; today there are fears about combining science with art, it’s been a step backwards for certain. Could the following 1887 diatribe by nineteenth-century Turner expert, James Orrock be the first challenge by connoisseurship against science invading the purview of the art establishment?
The chemist, in his analysis and experiments with pigments… has never so much as touched the practice of the watercolour painter and its results, and it is confidently submitted by Mr. Orrock that the chemist never can. What has the exposure under known test conditions of a simple colour to do with the exposure of infinite mixtures and manipulations of semi-tones and infinities of compound colours, which express the genius of the magician of the Art, as Turner expressed it in one of his wondrous aerial and brilliant Landscapes? James was one of my favourite owners. At the time, this argument against science’s “interference” was won by force of rhetoric. The actual truth turned out to be contrary to Orrock’s argument. It was eventually accepted that watercolour pigment is in fact damaged by exposure to light. Rhetoricians are still the winners of most modern debates over such issues, and to go one step further, such arguments are usually made to support special interests. There was an interesting contest at a 2002 IFAR conference. A query was lobbed at one of the team who had just completed the catalogue raisonné on Georgia O’Keeffe. The banter took place during the question and answer period after the cataloguers made their presentation. It was recorded in the IFAR journal as follows:
Question: In addition to your examination of all the sheets of paper, did you do technical analysis of the media, for example the pigments?
Answer: We didn’t. It wasn’t practical to do it. The problem with any kind of analysis such as you describe is that it relies very much on the scientists doing it. Technical analysis is not magically correct because it’s done with a machine. And so the scientist who does the work has a lot of subjective control over what he or she finds…”
As science is the least subjective of any method of analysis, it is possible that the point the cataloguer followed with had more bearing on the matter: “Yes, but it is incredibly time consuming.” In the world of art, scientific solutions are not always as clear-cut as one would expect. Nonetheless, Marilyn Laver, Bob’s champion in this realm was to try her best to analyze me—Shipwreck, the Rescue. Much to his relief, she was able to gather all the available records from her previous analysis in 1982. It took several months to draw together the results and provide a synopsis. The package Bob was waiting for with such anticipation finally arrived but unfortunately it didn’t prove anything about who painted me. The paradox of scientific study is that although seeking to be absolutely conclusive in its findings, it can do little to prove art authorship conclusively. It is more effectively exploited by law enforcement to prove fraud than by art historians and collectors to prove if an object is genuine. From paint samples for example, “pigment identification can deny but rarely confirm a painting’s age.”
If any miniscule bit of data places a painting out of context (as the agents in the Visnei case were hoping would happen) the entire artwork can be rejected. If certain pigments, canvas, paper, wooden supports, or working techniques were present on a picture but were never used prior to a certain date; then a painting purporting to be earlier would automatically be condemned unless there was an explanation for said incongruity. Yet, if innumerable similarities are found that connect favorably to an artist, such as art materials, subject matter, composition, format, brush techniques, palette of colours, and so on, but only a sliver of this information is atypical—what then?
What if the preeminent authority on a particular artist disregards the whole raft of information gathered because of the errant albeit inconclusive bits of datum and takes a position more favourable to his or her more narrowly defined agenda? This does happen, and when it does, one can only speculate whether the reason goes beyond integrity. There can be solid reasons for dismissal, such as those relating to overall style, but there are also many rejections that are largely ungrounded and illogical. In 1938, Frank Jewett Mather, Jr. argued: “It seems to me…that most of our art historians are entirely untrained in historical method; hence have no objective check upon their subjective, stylistic intuitions.”
Because of its importance as an aesthetic tool and the foundation on which true art passions owe homage, I hate to appear hostile toward connoisseurship, but in order to get at the truth and away from the politics, this derision is necessary in hopes of encouraging scientists to take a more confident role. They must be encouraged to state their conclusions with authority rather than compliance.
To exemplify the profound affect a small group, or for that matter, an individual can have on the world of art, we need look no further than the renowned Wildenstein dynasty. Established in the 1870s, it has grown more influential over the ensuing three generations by virtue of erudition, stealth, accumulation of wealth and influence, and above all, the production of catalogues. “Being the publishers of such books confers enormous power – [and among other things] the ability to authenticate paintings…as a publisher of catalogue raisonnés, the Wildensteins virtually control the scholarship on many artists from Boucher to Monet.”
This frustrated one art historian, and with regard to the Wildenstein Gauguin catalogue, rightly or wrongly, he felt that “pedigrees and quotations had been juggled to suit private needs, and that Gauguin’s oeuvre has been shorn of some authentic works and adulterated with others that did not belong.” What about Rembrandt; and those who have been charged since 1968 to write a comprehensive catalogue of his paintings and “especially to free his oeuvre of the accretions that were thought to have disfigured the image of Rembrandt the painter.” One must contemplate whether implicit in this manifesto is the need to preserve only what the Rembrandt Research Project (RRP) considers to be Rembrandt’s better works? In her role as an analytical statistician, Geraldine Keen gives pause for thought over what it means to control any market:
At any one time there is usually one particular scholar whose opinion is considered the most authoritative about the work of a particular artist, though sometimes several scholars may contend for this distinction. In expressing their opinions they may wield tremendous financial power. For instance, no definitive catalogue of Rembrandt’s drawings existed until the 1950s when Otto Benesch published the results of his long years of research. Around 1950 three drawings catalogued as ’’ were sold at Christie’s for about $280; another, attributed by Sotheby’s to one of Rembrandt’s contemporaries, fetched only $25. By the time Benesch’s catalogue was published, its inclusion of these drawings had raised their value to 28,000 - 42,000 each.
What about Turner, and me—Shipwreck, the Rescue? Bob disparaged, in a reply letter to Marilyn Laver: “a conclusion as to authorship in all cases would be based on probability unless an honest J.M.W. Turner was to come back to life. It seems almost futile to approach authentication of a picture by scientific analysis when critical authorities, even within the scientific camp, attempt to discredit a painting based on a meager number of anomalies.” Why the frustration? It was because of the response forwarded to him by Marilyn. It was from one of her colleagues in , Dr. Joyce H. Townsend. This Senior Conservation Scientist at the Tate Gallery had responded to her inquiry over the painting (from her letter dated October, 1997): “The join in the canvas [my canvas] is not typical, as Turner could evidently obtain coarse hessian supports as wide as he required them. I know of only one which has a joined canvas, and it is larger than this one: The Garden of the Hesperides at the Tate Gallery with a horizontal join almost in the centre.”
This is an excerpt. For the full chapter, read Art World’s Dirty Little Secret.

title: Chapter 9: The Impenetrable Shield
content:
Chapter 9
The Impenetrable Shield Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
The Impenetrable Shield
The art world has hesitantly gone along with the world’s greater reliance on science and technology. Watching from the sidelines I’ve wondered if this creeping empiricism hasn’t hampered people’s intuitive and emotional abilities. I like what Bob once wrote…
The need for a diverse outlook is vital when moving among grander ideas and philosophies. When talking about glowing sunsets based on glazing techniques, motherly love based on the Christian Madonna, or movement in a picture based on geometric orientation, one is left empty. We must be able to mate illusion with reality, imagination with knowledge and spirituality with the mundane in order to fully appreciate the world around us, particularly art.
Imagine yourself a technician in a laboratory, denuding a work of art by exposing its hidden secrets—like dissecting an angel. It is sacrilege unless done by conservation scientists with at least the will to turn their temporal deed into something less carnal. Should they confess a deeper emotion than mere curiosity? Some do, some do not, and some pretend. Those that do are enlightened, while those that do not will find only lead white in the fragile wings of Murillo’s angel or bitumen in the haloed grottos of el Greco.
JMW himself made a great synthesis of seemingly diverse interests during his own lifetime, as would be expected of a “genius.” While somewhat abandoning his poetry and allegorical paintings, he looked toward Natural sciences for inspiration with “some of his works even imitating the iron filings of Faraday’s 1851 experiments with magnetism.” Turner’s colleague, John Constable, proclaimed that in such an age as this, painting should be understood not as a poetic aspiration, but as a pursuit, legitimate, scientific, and mechanical. Alas, within the context of the same era and pressed by the hard edge of trade psychology, Morris Eaves advanced that “if art can claim science and mechanics as it once claimed poetry, in a country whose scientific and technological strengths are boosting the well-established strength of commerce, then painting may participate in the English success.”
When my scientific documents arrived from Marilyn Laver, they looked great. They came in a tidy three-ring binder and had a dramatic black and white photograph of me on the cover. There were several large format photographs: full colour, infrared, ultraviolet, electron-microscopic. Detailed photographs were taken of things like my title label and stretcher, and there were several x-radiographic images as well. It had page after page of test results, with pigment names broken down into linguistically impossible contrivances. There were even photographs that made me look like a ghost. In the introduction, Marilyn described the genesis of her report as follows: In 1982, a scientific examination study was undertaken at the Canadian Conservation Institute (Ottawa) on a painting on canvas entitled “Shipwreck – The Rescue” in order to determine whether the materials and technique of the work were consistent with those of J. M. W. Turner. The painting was part of a group of works; which included others in the style of Picasso, Collins and Gainsborough. The request for the study came from the Royal Canadian Mounted Police (Cpl. B. Roberts, Vancouver) and Interpol (John Lyons, ). Time for the investigation was limited as the information was required for a trial in in 1982. A verbal report was made to Crown Prosecutor Powell in by Marilyn Laver; however, expert testimony was not required in court. As the case was settled on the basis of other evidence, further analytical work was not pursued; no formal report was required and none was written. In 1997, Bob Setters, the current private owner of the painting requested an evaluation of the data from the earlier study, resulting in this report, prepared in cooperation with CCI. Once the police had seized me there was great care taken to guarantee all evidence would be of forensic nature. My shipping crate was sealed with a multitude of official RCMP stickers, I even had my own Mountie as escort (nice kid), and once I arrived in much care was taken to secure the results of testing and preserve the chain of custody. My stretcher was marked ‘ML’ by Marilyn Laver on entry into the building and all samples were kept in a locked vault or secure laboratory while under analysis. In the report Marilyn’s observations and conclusions were interspersed with guarded comments trying to explain the enigmatic. At the same time she candidly relied on the 1997 reply from Townsend. It was clear this text would take Bob much energy to digest. He began with Marilyn’s carefully worded conclusions: The painting shows evidence of age which is consistent with a work from the period around 1800. It has undergone several conservation treatments, including retouching and attachment of three lining canvases. It bears a varnish layer with the milky fluorescence which often results from aging. The use of lead antimonite yellow [ yellow] also suggests a 1750 -1850 date range, the period when the pigment was most popular.
Many of the stylistic features observed suggest that this is an unfinished work; for example, the lack of detail in many areas of the composition, and the thinness of the paint with the ground layer and pentimenti visible.
The materials from this painting which were analyzed were available to J.M.W. Turner in his lifetime. The pigments identified suggest the more restricted palette characteristic of his early years (before about 1805), prior to his well-known experimentation with new materials. Relative to published analyses of Turner works, some of the pigment usage is uncommon: ultramarine in a green mixture, lead antimonate yellow [ yellow], and barium sulfate in the ground layer… It can be concluded that physical evidence suggests that the painting originates from the time period of J. M. W. Turner and comprises material available to him… The question of attribution to any particular artist should be addressed by a curatorial expert on Turner’s work based on content and style.
A really important point here: Sir Richard Westmacott (one of a party about to start for ) asked Turner if he could do anything for him, “No,” said Turner, “Unless you will bring me some yellow.” Hamerton, one of JMW’s first biographers also supports Marilyn’s statement about Turner’s experimental nature: “He used any new colour that the experimentalizing ingenuity of modern chemistry could invent for the temptation of an artist.”
…The stretcher in “Shipwreck, the Rescue” consisted of four vertical members and three horizontal ones, keyed at the corners and at each join with the perimeter. By comparison, Townsend mentions that in the early years, many of Turner’s paintings had homemade stretchers with the diagonal cross-members generally found in the eighteenth century. However, this stretcher may have been built later…
The label on the stretcher reading “Shipwreck – The Rescue J.M.W. Turner R.A.” may also have been affixed at this time. The paper of the label was not a wood pulp or Japanese paper; hemp or bast (linen) were possibilities (i.e. rag paper), neither of which are unique to Turner’s period.
If I remember rightly my present stretcher is from Brown of High Holborn, a firm that JMW was patronizing circa 1830-1850. I’m pretty sure it was about that time… He was refurbishing some of us early works trying to find buyers—about the time Ruskin was coming around. In any case it has the proper configuration and construction, including beveled inner edges of the stretcher bars. It’s the kind JMW was buying after daddy passed-on in 1829. It was a relief to be getting a little TLC after fifty years of neglect. I hate recalling that damp moldy environment. Under normal circumstances there would have been no need by this point for such a dramatic restoration on such a young painting. Oh yeah, this is when they slapped the label on my stretcher. Finally I’d been given a proper name—Shipwreck, the Rescue! In keeping with this timing, which followed the 1843 publication of the first volume of Ruskin’s Modern Painters (with its spirited championship of JMW’s art) “his pictures were again in keen demand, and he was encouraged to put some earlier canvases into repair to tempt the new purchasers who now came to the gallery.”
It’s funny that as yet no body has noticed my title label and how it adheres to the wood of my stretcher. Even the seasoned wood of quality furniture, and in this case my stretcher, naturally shrinks given enough time. The top on a small Georgian tea table can shrink as much as three-quarter inch across one plane, leaving the top slightly oval. This happens with stretcher wood also; the wood shrinks in one direction—across the grain. This is relevant here because while my stretcher wood has shrunk over one and a half centuries, my paper label has not. The result is that my label has wrinkled, and in places has separated from the wood, and chipped. This is significant because it guarantees that the label is vintage and that both it and my stretcher are from Turner’s era (see image No. 4). I’m adding some of this detail for the doubting Thomases out there, the ones that don’t recognize me for what I am.
This is an excerpt. For the full chapter, read Art World’s Dirty Little Secret.
