title: Chapter 10: Advocate, Home and Abroad
content:
Chapter 10
Advocate, Home and Abroad Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
Advocate: Home and Abroad
Bob’s Journal entry …
Graham tunneled through security at the airport and made his way down the long corridor to the waiting jet that was ready to thrust him to another continent. Once off the ground, within moments, he would appear like a speck on the horizon. Bob’s sentimental thoughts were brought to an abrupt halt when he realized Graham’s laptop was still at the security-check—forgotten and alone, except for his passport and cash which was tucked in the carrying case.
As Bob anxiously tried to explain the dilemma to a very suspicious and dumbfounded security guard, he noticed arms and legs flailing in the passageway as Graham attempted a new sprint record. Minutes later, cool and composed, he was over the horizon, with laptop, passport, and a pounding spirit for adventure.
Graham’s invitation to study abroad at the International Study Centre (ISC)— Queen’s University’s Herstmonceux Castle was going to leave things pretty quiet around home…
Not all was gloom for Bob, as one of his first projects abroad Graham wrote a bloody good paper on me. His Art History professor, Daphne Lawson was quite remarkable, allowing him to choose his own study projects throughout the year. In my opinion his best was, Analysis of J.M.W. Turner’s Shipwreck, the Rescue. Among the galleries and museums he haunted while searching out JMW’s artwork, were the National Gallery, the , the Tate Gallery, the Courtauld Institute and the & . His resulting paper shows an interesting synthesis of antiquarian interests and social interpretation. At the time, Bob wasn’t too keen on the latter. You’ll see later.
Except from: Analysis of J.M.W. Turner’s “Shipwreck, the Rescue”
The determination of the closest possible dates for artwork is an ongoing challenge for art historians. They appeal to a variety of sources: primary, a bill of sale; and secondary, discoveries about artwork written at a later date. Usually more in-depth research is required, in which case the analysis of both the physical and stylistic characteristics of the work is conducted. An art connoisseur undertakes the examination of stylistic characteristics whereas scientific analysis is required for the examination of physical characteristics. For Shipwreck, the Rescue, considering both physical and stylistic criteria, a date of c1802 is assigned.
Turner, like many of his contemporaries, was forced to look back at the theory and colour practices of past great masters due to a lack of technical teachings at the . This working method is one that Turner retained throughout his life. The informal training of the Romantic period made the apprentice system obsolete and in some cases artists had to rely on technical handbooks for direction. During Turner’s formative years he “felt his way slowly, and invented his procedures as he went.” From his earliest development, he was coached with the idea that an artist should find another artist that is comparable in talent and imitate his or her style with the intent of surpassing their skill. It was thought that if an artist could do this he would achieve a higher position within an artistic hierarchy. Turner had the unique opportunity to put himself ahead of Willem Van de Velde the younger (1633-1707) in this artistic hierarchy when in 1800 he was commissioned to paint, Dutch Boats in a Gale: Fishermen endeavouring to put their Fish on Board (Bridgewater Sea piece). The Duke of Bridgewater wanted Turner to paint it as a companion for his sea piece, A Rising Gale by Willem Van de Velde after which these two sea pieces would hang side by side. Even though Turner’s end result was a significantly different sea piece, it was unanimously well received in the press. This work helped to ensconce him as a great artist after both these works were exhibited together by Lord Francis Egerton in 1837. It was at this point that Turner gained Old Master status. Turner’s successful and well-received effort of 1801 encouraged similar projects: Calais Pier, with French Poissards preparing for Sea: an English Packet arriving; Shipwreck, and most certainly Shipwreck, the Rescue The ‘ piece’ was an important start to a powerful series of sea pieces; however, Turner’s voyage to was a distinctive turning point in the way he would portray the sea. Surely Turner was excited about sailing to because of his fascination with the sea. This voyage changed his outlook forever. The weather during his journey was rough and Turner described his landing at Pier as, ‘Nearly Swampt.’ After this harrowing experience Turner’s sea pieces such as, Calais Pier and Shipwreck took a markedly turbulent shift in style. Turner’s style was also branded by his mother’s prolonged illness and wretched death in 1804. The Shipwreck and The Wreck of a Transport Ship both embody the emotional turbulence that was part of Turner’s new fabric “…the tumult of a catastrophe.” The new intensity with which Turner painted The Shipwreck has been described as an overwhelming impression of realism and terror. The dark tonality of Turner’s early oils is magnificently justified by the subject. Shipwreck, the Rescue and Calais Pier were done prior to the death of Turner’s mother in 1804, which surely accounts for a lesser sense of ‘terror’ and ‘catastrophe’, but undeniably, they still reflect deep anxiety. This might suggest that Shipwreck, the Rescue and Calais Pier are works executed by Turner between 1802 and 1804. The presence of a pier in both, Calais Pier and Shipwreck, the Rescue gives a sense of optimism for the passengers because it provides for them a chance for survival. In Turner’s sea pieces subsequent to his mother’s death, as in The Shipwreck and The Wreck of a Transport Ship this symbol of hope is completely obliterated and replaced with increased furor. To determine the date of a painting from within the body of an artist’s work it is important to compare changes in technique. The somewhat a-typical use of brown ground in Turner’s Shipwreck, the Rescue helps in determining its date. In a sketchbook, dated approximately 1798, at a point when he was beginning to experiment with oils, Turner reversed his watercolour technique. Instead of the traditional white ground Turner began working from a dark ground in order to achieve a stronger range of tones. ...Turner eventually brightened his paintings, giving the desired light to his work by means of a white ground, but the dilemma arose for him later on when he tried to reverse the impact of the predominantly white ground by using bituminous glazes to darken the tone of such paintings as Rembrandt’s Daughter Reading a Letter, 1827. Of course, this work inspired by one of Rembrandt’s own works would need a much darker overall tone, and when Turner darkened it, disaster ensued. Glazing with a bituminous colorant in an attempt to impart the appropriate chiaroscuro proved a tragic exercise. One only needs to see the disastrous results of such an effort by observing the ugly degradation of the surface that resulted over time. Clearly in this case Turner should have started with a dark ground and worked his illumination with lighter pigments and finally heavily applied white impasto as Rembrandt did or he himself had done during the earlier years of the century. On February 13, 1802, Turner was described by a fellow Academician as painting “…on an absorbing ground prepared by Grandi…It absorbs the oil even at the fourth time of painting over…by this process he thinks he gets air and avoids any horny appearance.” Grandi prepared these grounds for Turner starting early in 1802. They would have been a darkish colour similar to that found on Shipwreck, the Rescue. After 1800, Turner was already interested in the effects of a dark ground for his oil paintings because of the elusive “Venetian Secret.” It was thought by many members in the that the Venetian painters, such as Titian, used glazed transparent colours over a “dark absorbent ground.” Turner’s pilgrimage to the Louvre in 1802 gave him a first-hand experience with Venetian masters such as Titian. Turner sketched and made notes on over thirty paintings during his visit there. He paid particularly close attention to Titian’s Entombment. In his words: This picture may be ranked among the first of Titian’s pictures as to colour and pathos of effect, for by casting a brilliant light on the Holy Mother and Martha, the figures of Josephe [sic] and the Body has a Sepulceral [sic] effect…The Flesh is thinly painted, first by a cold colors over a Brown ground, so that it is neither purple or green, some red is used in the extremities, and the lights are warm…his brown figures lose in great measure the gray colour which in fact is produced by the ground…
Turner’s thorough analysis of this painting shows his interest in the Venetian master’s technique for achieving effect. He emphasizes the point that Titian used a brown ground and glazing to achieve the ‘brilliant light.’ The techniques used for the Entombment and many other paintings left a considerable impression on Turner. Like his contemporaries, he might not have realized that on some of the worked up areas, Titian’s ground had been a white gesso over a dark brown ground and consequently he did not achieve the desired “Titianesque” effect. The result was too dark. Because of these somewhat unsuccessful results (which many of his contemporaries criticized), Turner abandoned the brown ground used in Shipwreck, the Rescue and used white grounds for Calais Pier, and Shipwreck. This is another point that helps to establish a date of c1802 for Shipwreck, the Rescue. Over the next decade, he continued to use dark coloured grounds for non-aquatic genera. The style and technique are paramount when dating artwork, however, the scientific analysis of physical characteristics is also highly significant. Turner was

title: Chapter 11: Orrock Esq.
content:
Chapter 11
Orrock Esq. Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
730 AS, the Property of James Orrock Esq.
“Hello, this is Jeremy Rex-Parkes, I am away from my desk at the moment, but if you would like to leave your name and number I will get back to you as soon as possible.” Just bad timing! Each time Bob phoned the archivist at Christie’s in he was away. Because of the eight-hour time difference he was calling at then again at in the morning. He left his name and number once, but to no avail. Bob mumbled, “The repetitive nature of the job must have driven poor Jeremy to distraction.” Out of frustration Bob was about to give up. “One last try,” he grumbled.
“Oh yes, I’m sorry,” Jeremy apologized. “I was not able to return your call yesterday, the message was cut off before I got your number …” Bob interrupted – “Oh, that’s fine, I’m happy enough just to make contact.” The formal impersonal voice of Jeremy’s answering machine had become one of gentle conciliatory charm. Bob’s mental image was of a middle aged docent with a scholar’s demeanor; weathered, slight, and gray. It is amazing how a strange voice automatically conjures up an image, probably an image of someone better than real. Or maybe not.
Before phoning that morning, Bob had been reviewing his progress. Since the beginning of the rescue efforts (in deference to my noble provenance) he’d been aware of a stock number stenciled on my stretcher. He rightly assumed that during the struggle by scientists and the courts to find answers this was a stone already turned. It was an indication that I had sold at public auction, and because Mr. Dowding the defense lawyer, told him I had sold through Sotheby’s and it was a Sotheby’s representative that was called in as an expert witness, he assumed it was their number. The court case took place almost twenty years ago. It was understandable that much of the information Dowding gave him embodied only dim recollection. I remember Bob mentioning off hand that the number looked like Christie’s. He took the suspicion no further.
Volumes had accumulated about me since the west-coast auction almost three years earlier and only a short list of challenges remained. One was linking the number on my stretcher with a Sotheby’s catalogue entry. As always, provenance is a key factor. Bob decided to track down information about the stencil number, while Graham was still trying to get the balance of elusive court documents in . From Sotheby’s, , Bob was assured by a young voice that it could very well be a Sotheby’s number from long ago. He was disappointed to learn that because of my age a manual search of old catalogues was required. To facilitate this, the girl requested a photograph and description of me. It was at this point that Bob decided to contact Christie’s just on the off-chance the number was theirs.
In the meantime, the task of tracking down the remaining court documents was beginning to look futile. Graham was told that criminal case documents can only be released to the parties who had been directly involved in proceedings. The only person who could help was Dowding, and he seemed too tired.
Again, as with Sotheby’s Christie’s needed time to search archives. One relief was that it could all be done online. Bob sent quality images of the sales number and label along with the following note, “We were more than happy when you confirmed this might be a Christie’s number. There has been information suggesting the painting came from the estate of one of J.M.W. Turner’s executors. The vintage label reads, Shipwreck, the Rescue J.M.W. Turner RA. The tattoo number is 73 OAS …”
The next morning, William, Bob’s pharmacist buddy from woke him bright and early. “Happy Birthday,” he drawled. After an amiable chat, Bob checked his e-mail. Mr. Rex-Parkes had already replied. It had taken him less than a day.
Good Morning Mr. Setters; It was 73OAS and the sale was 4th June 1904, ‘The Highly Important Collection of Pictures and Watercolour Drawings,’ the property of James Orrock, owing to ill health and is ordered away from London, Lot 140, JMW Turner, RA, The Shipwreck 63 X 87 inches.
Presented by the artist to his executor, Mr. Griffith. From the collection of Sir J. C. Robinson. I hope the information is what you were looking for, and if you have other stock numbers, do not hesitate to contact me. Regards, jrp.
Stunning! Bob had to read these few words several times before they sunk in and the implications began to jell. “What a great birthday surprise,” he chuckled. Not only was this substantial provenance, it took my history directly back to JMW himself. Bob didn’t realize it but this exciting news would generate a research project in and of its self. Colin B. Bailey, at the time Chief Curator at the National Gallery of had dropped in for a visit shortly after this revelation. He was touring and lecturing about a National Gallery exhibition he was promoting, Renoir’s Portraits: Impressions of an Age. He authored the accompanying catalogue. He and Bob discussed a number of the “orphans” around home, including me. Without prompting, and on his return to , Dr. Bailey sent the entire catalogue from the Christie’s 1904 Orrock . The same catalogue from whence Jeremy Rex-Parkes quoted.
Who were all these people mentioned in the sale’s listing? Bob started his search. He was like a kid with a new toy. Several journal entries followed… …James Orrock R.I., R.O.I. (1829-1913) the Victorian/Edwardian gentleman from whose collection Shipwreck, the Rescue sold at Christie’s in 1904, was a renowned collector of both paintings and Oriental ceramics and was himself a widely exhibited artist
James Orrock R.I., R.O.I., print after an oil painting by Orrock In fact, some of what has come down to us about Turner’s secreted working methods has done so by virtue of Orrock quoting his own painting master, W.L. Leitch (who knew Turner personally).
Orrock had been twenty-one years old on Turner’s passing, and was only ten years younger than Turner’s champion—Ruskin. He knew Ruskin well. [Bob didn’t know yet but there was an oversize two-volume biography written about Orrock by Byron Webber: published in , Chatto and Windus 1903: James , Painter, Connoisseur, Collector.] Some of the artfully produced books Orrock had personally illustrated during his lifetime, in the style of Turner, included: Mary Queen of Scots; Old England - her story mirrored in her scenes, and In the Border Country. References to Orrock are found in several other publications about Turner including: The Studio; The Genius of J.M.W. Turner R.A, and Turner and Ruskin. As an artist, renowned collector, and foremost advocate of Turner and British art, Orrock must be considered a premier Turner connoisseur and expert of his time.
…Orrock acquired Shipwreck, the Rescue from Sir John Charles Robinson R.E. (1824-1913) the landscape painter and etcher. From 1879 until 1901, Sir Charles held the venerable post of Surveyor of Her Majesty Queen Victoria’s Pictures, and was highly respected for his understanding and appreciation of art at an exemplary level. He served as the first Superintendent of Collections at the (1852-1869), later to be called the and . When the rooms in House occupied by Turner’s pictures and drawings were required for other purposes, the Government decided to build a series of temporary galleries in connection with the Museum at . This 1858 establishment of what amounted to a makeshift Turner Gallery was in lieu of a proper facility as prescribed by Turner in his Bequest. It was under Robinson’s aegis that this extensive collection of Turner’s work was housed, protected, and displayed, while plans were still underway for a permanent and exclusive facility to house the trove—those hundreds of paintings and thousands of works on paper. At the midterm of his tenure Robinson would have been the conductor of this extensive project, and by association he would have been a leading connoisseur of Turner’s work.
This is an excerpt. For the full chapter, read Art World’s Dirty Little Secret.

title: Chapter 12: The Jolly Art Sleuth
content:
Chapter 12
The Jolly Art Sleuth Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
The Jolly Art Sleuth
Shortly after Bob contacted the previously mentioned scientist, Marilyn Laver, and the gathering of material from her 1982 CCI report on yours truly had begun, she sent him a small news clipping. It didn’t seem relevant. It had something to do with a Canadian fellow discovering JMW’s fingerprints on an oil painting. He had bought it from a picker who found it mixed together with a jumble of old and broken frames. The artist was unknown. Surprisingly, the soiled landscape which was later purchased by the Biró family produced the proverbial rags to riches story!
It was not long after this family started to restore it that they realized it was somehow special. According to the news article, they noticed fingerprints on the upper left edge of the stretcher. A remnant of an illegible label was found. They subjected it to intense scrutiny; the latent ink had bled through the obliterated piece of paper and the stain appeared to read: “J.M. William Turner.” With hopes of having an original JMW Turner their inquiry intensified. At the end of their efforts and ten years later their 1,000 dollar chance purchase returned nearly 200,000 dollars by way of a Phillips auction in .
As published in the Philip’s sales catalogue of , Early British and Victorian Paintings, the painting was first reported by David Hill in the Turner Society News #66, March 1994. The discovery attracted world-wide media interest. Coverage focused on fingerprints found in the paint at the top left of the foreground tree. This had been positively cross-matched with fingerprints on samples in the Turner Bequest at the Tate Gallery. As Bob reread the catalogue entry and the clipping I knew something was stating to percolate.
Clearly, the acceptance of the painting was based on fingerprint evidence, at which point other scientific and curatorial opinion was brought in for additional support. While the considerable publicity that followed changed the minds of several disbelievers, it was the print match that set everything in motion. Tate authorities had previously condemned it as being by “someone painting in the spirit of Turner.”
Even the slightest hint of dissention can send a painting to its death. From the Tate there had been more than a hint! J.H. Duveen gave insight about how an authentic work can be “spoiled by enemies who by an untraceable word here and there spread the idea that it is not exactly right! That kind of malevolence is often to be found, no matter how august the art circle concerned.” Without the fingerprint, the Birós would have been out ranked and without hope.
It is known that Turner used his thumbprint in trees for a rapid effect
Vale of Avoca detail, greatly enlarged fingerprint and that he would also “make the texture of a stone with a single pressure of his thumb.” “Turner had formed the habit of working the paint with his fingers: thumbprints are to be found on many early drawings. They are nearly always present somewhere in his finished watercolours. This technique was particularly effective for building up his trees; he blurred the more distant leaves by dabbing his finger in the damp paint, then he articulated the forward leaves with brushwork. This gave his trees volume in space, significant form, and the illusion of motion.
There had already been academic discussions about the possibilities of using fingerprint evidence to help identify an artist’s work. In 1948 Otto Kurz in his book called Fakes acknowledged that the “study of the imprint of the artist’s fingers on pictures has been much advertised as a means of identification, but nothing has ever come of it.”
The Birós ultimate success was due to their inventive genius and tenacity, they triumphed over almost insurmountable odds. The painting was apparently unsigned and had absolutely no traceable provenance, but as leading edge technology would prove—the little guy was right—the Birós made history.
The coincidence is uncanny that this painting was the same one referred to by the local art dealer from ; the one, who in the The Vancouver Sun had disparaged me prior to the Aldergrove auction in 1997. “I just despair,” he was quoted, “of people going to auctions and getting carried away by the event. It’s really a feeding frenzy at auctions, with everyone thinking they’re going to get a deal. I strongly advise people: ‘Buyer beware’… the dealer, who is a student of Turner’s work, quotes last year’s international sales records for authentic Turner paintings: ‘the highest price paid at auction in 1996 was 180,000 dollars for a large landscape with rainbow, a work that Turner actually signed and then recorded in his own journal.” The painting referred to, is the same painting found mixed in with a bunch of old frames in . In fact, it did not have provenance or a signature. This was Biró’s painting! And as for its reception by the Turner art establishment, Landscape with Rainbow had barely been dusted off.
The Biró family is indeed a gifted father and two-son team. I shouldn’t have made them sound like novices. They’ve juggled art world vagaries for two generations already. Leading up to their fingerprint line-of-attack, the Birós had developed formative techniques to investigate such minutiae. Paul, the younger son had previous experience in photography and computer enhancement.
Bob grumbled assertively as he made his daily journal entry, “Oftentimes the presence of a fingerprint is the only evidence drawing the executioner to the guillotine.” He then carried on with his entry: “A student of Turner’s work, David Hill, eventually gained sufficient confidence to give “stylistic” support for the painting. This was after Scotland Yard corroborated the fingerprint. Within the conservative art world it seems that new methods of art study are looked on with distrust lest they upset the status quo.”
I don’t want to get ahead of myself here, but there are important Turner experts that still refuse to fully accept Landscape with Rainbow. There are qualms within the curatorial community regarding any new research tool and/or major discovery. New information can contradict standard scholarship and potentially compromise reputations. And legal fears over changing the status of an artwork appear pandemic. A sale of this painting at a different time and place and supported by additional expertise would surely have brought a multiple of what the Biró’s realized—several million I’m sure.
When given the Biró news clipping by Marilyn Laver, Bob had no idea of its significance and that the Biró story would soon connect personally. At the time, Bob ignored the crumpled scrap and tucked it away at the back of his “Turner file.” Three years later, that same art-sleuth was to spend nearly a week in , nose to my pigments, spotlight beaming relentlessly.
What brought their name to Bob a second time was the Internet. The ethereal Landscape with Rainbow led him to their Art Restoration and Forensic Studies in Art website affectionately called Biró the Art Sleuth. Their laboratory had expertise in everything from restoration to forensic studies using fingerprint imaging technology. The FBI had even shown an interest in their novel fingerprint-imaging techniques.
Bob’s short e-mail inquiry evolved quickly into a commitment to hire Peter Paul Biró, the sleuth member of the Biró family. Art sleuth par excellence, a man of many talents! He had moved to with his parents and older brother at the age of fourteen. They were refugees from where his ‘papa’ had worked for many years as an art conservator at the Museum of Fine Arts.
In getting credentials for their own Turner the Birós dealt with their frustrations differently than Bob. They went right for the jugular, forcing their painting through auction, and likely in defiance of important curatorial opinion. Bob and Graham thought they could accomplish their own goal through art-historical study of JMW and his contemporaries. In other words, I was being treated like one of the family, another one of their “orphans” per se. Little did they realize that there were political problems that out flanked the simple task of dredging up the truth. There were scoundrels lurking beneath the bleachers. Untangling how they were connected to influential dealers, scholars, auction houses, galleries and museums was going to be more difficult than he realized. It soon became clear that status within the art community was based as much on longevity and power of wealth as it was upon altruism and erudite credentials.
Reading many tomes about fakes and forgeries opened Bob’s eyes somewhat but the puzzle pieces did not fit easily. Combined with collecting and reading a hundred-plus books on Turner, he became more than confident that there was no deception – I was the real thing! With such irreproachable provenance, and powerful scientific proof there should be no problem getting me accepted. In all my monumental grandeur I should have been considered the Turner find of the century. But rather than an explosion of excitement, the indifference became stifling for poor Bob—I was slowly becoming his nemesis.
This is an excerpt. For the full chapter, read Art World’s Dirty Little Secret.

title: Chapter 13: Clairvoyants and Necromancers
content:
Chapter 13
Clairvoyants and Necromancers Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
Clairvoyants and Necromancers
Bob’s Journal entries, Sept 2000…
…The accepted “expert” today needs mainly to be a good cataloguer, and largely exists apart from “old fashioned” connoisseurship. If someone is able to collect into one catalogue, the entire body of an artist’s accepted work, the same diligent actuary is given authority to be an expert. The weakness inherent in this process is that the expert is not always a connoisseur or able to think outside the limited scope of standard literature on the artist. More important, he or she is unable to recognize un-catalogued new discoveries. Here one must be able to go beyond mere recognition and into the realm of synthesis.
The process of cataloguing in itself might be viewed as an attempt to squelch unfettered research on a particular artist. To “close off” the number of accepted works by an artist assures an orderly maintenance of the marketplace. The person who has arduously carried out the task might also be expected to defend their “definitive” catalogue listings against potential new intruders in order to avoid expensive revisions or to have their research considered lacking. Nobody wants a defective brainchild. Does it not seem a bit presumptuous in the first place that anyone would proclaim their catalogue complete?
Zealous antagonism towards new art discoveries is more explicable if one accepts carte blanche Bernard Berenson’s statement, “By bestowing a name on a work of art we ignoble that work in all the love that it arouses in us and at the same time shut our eyes to the elements which, seen dispassionately, make it wrong. One easily tends to exclude adverse factors.” The problem with extrapolating this much-adopted attitude is that Berenson was working ostensibly with artists that had no name to begin with, and much of his life’s work was based on invention; whereas, in an era after that of the Renaissance, when the name of an artist, in itself, might connote fame, there is always the likelihood of having stylistic comparables to pacify the incredulous art historian and expert alike.
Skepticism is not an uncommon frame of mind for many within the curatorial realm. What are acceptable limits though? Are attempts by individual owners of un-catalogued paintings to correlate and match information about a particular artist, only a sinister scheme to deceive? Of course not! They are the normal first steps in trying to find solutions.
Justice Herman Black’s closing remarks came after a famous debate between experts over the authenticity of La Belle Ferronnière believed to be by Leonardo da Vinci. These experts included: Berenson, Roger Fry, Sir Charles Holmes, then director of ’s National Gallery, and Duveen. Justice Black made pointed reference to experts who offer “nothing better than their own sixth sense as evidence for a picture’s genuineness.” The judge also criticized that it required “a good deal of mental agility to follow some of the experts from their positive evidence on the stand, to the diametrically opposite views they had expressed in their books long before.”
There was apparently a complete flip-flop of curatorial opinion. Duveen’s own cousin James Henry Duveen described this same sensational lawsuit but with more zest: “The reports of the interminable proceedings prove the truth of an English judge’s well-known dictum that there are three kinds of liars: liars, damned liars, and expert witnesses.”
After Duveen died the owner of La Belle Ferronnière published The Rape of La Belle. The book was a vicious attack on Duveen, who, Hahn said, “headed an international plot by art dealers to control the market, and Duveen’s array of experts, no better than fortune-tellers, clairvoyants and two-bit prophets.” Berenson himself eventually expressed this same sentiment in shared self-criticism using the terms, “clairvoyants and necromancers.”
From the other side of the authenticity debate and in sympathy with the curatorial position—the calamity that would result from a mistake in judgment could be fatal for one’s career. As one British art critic observed: “people love to see authority figures slipping on a banana skin.” The mystery and high prices associated with this type of fraud, makes forgery attention grabbing. This is not only for those deeply involved in the industry, but also for the art-illiterate. To the dismay of many in the art world, and particularly museum and gallery officials, the stir of publicity created by scandals over art fraud usually overshadows other momentous events. The distorted view left in most people’s mind, including the minds of many sophisticated collectors, is that much out there is “fake.” This is untrue. Luke Herrmann worries us similarly in relation to Turner, but fails to substantiate it: “As a painter in oils Turner attracted few followers, though there were numerous efforts to produce fake Turners in the years immediately before and after the artist’s death.” Who were they? Other than the three nefarious, albeit hard to define shadowy spirits, “Hands A, B, and C” of Martin Butlin (discussed later), no one seems able to finger these mysterious fakers. End Journal…
After breakfast on the fourth day, Paul and Bob sat at length enjoying the fresh morning air. He reviewed Marilyn Laver’s report, the one that had been done quickly to facilitate the Crown’s need for evidence in support of its case against Visnei. Paul disagreed with the background information that had drawn suspicion to the painting in the first place. Likely some characteristics of the painting that Joyce Townsend considered unusual for Turner were a result of incomplete data. The significance of the canvas thread count, colour of the ground, the canvas size, number of relinings, and the canvas being joined in the centre—these were all questioned by Paul.
Again there was banter about the way many experts such as art scholars and curators were so hesitant to give well thought-out opinions on works owned by private individuals, even when powerful scientific evidence was present. Paul agreed that in some ways there appeared to be animosity toward private art scholarship generally. Bob had already concluded that much indifference shown the private sector had to do with fear over litigation. Dr. Maryan W. Ainsworth alluded to this in the 2001 IFAR journal when she wrote that “Max Friedlander never calls anything a ‘fake.’ It is always a ‘replica’ or a ‘copy.’ I don’t think he ever even says ‘later version.’ It’s interesting that he, by choice, didn’t include fakes in his volumes… So he wasn’t talking either, for some reason. Probably for the same reason we don’t.” It might be noted here that for the sake of Friedlander’s discretion, the substitute terms: “copy” and “replica” have taken on a much more derogatory connotation than they deserve. Confucius knew the importance of “the rectification of names.” Did Friedlander?
This same trepidation was expressed by Kenneth Clark over what should have been considered fair art criticism. In his 1975, The Romantic Rebellion, he condemns trite mimicry by contemporary artists in light of some of Fuseli’s emotional and distorted artwork, but fails to name names. He wrote: “The laws of libel prevent me from naming several contemporary examples.” If the fear of litigation, even over art criticism, immobilized such a monarch of critical thinking, it is no wonder that infective paranoia has made much of the art world dumb to the needs of a collector like Bob.
Another salient reason for lack of support by the curatorial community for the private sector might be the personal frustration over the miniscule benefits they receive from giving an attribution. If a student’s work was upgraded to that of a great master such as Raphael, the painting could become a hundred times, or even a thousand times more valuable, and therefore provide its owner with a huge benefit. Could resentment at times, be at the root of feigned indifference?
There should be little argument that the monetary value of art has much to do with altering attitudes and stimulating activity in and around academic endeavours, including those at the elementary level. Aristotle Contemplating the Bust of Homer was bought at auction by the for the record price of 2.3 million dollars in 1961. An art history professor in the area recalled that the attendance in his introductory art appreciation doubled shortly after the Rembrandt sale – “most of the new students cheerfully admitted that their interest in art had been provoked by the press accounts of the Rembrandt Aristotle.” The energy that lights the flame of curiosity and eventually that of erudition is often sparked by the clatter of coins. From his College Window A. C. Benson contemplated—“to what extent it is permissible to use motives that are lower than the highest, because they may possess a greater effectiveness in the case of immature minds.”
“Banana-phobia” is probably one of the most unnerving perils for the curator/expert who works under public and administrative scrutiny. While most of them are certainly trying to perform their duties as well as possible, those often-antagonistic external forces that are outside their direct control, very much govern their lives. Public interest in scandal is one of those things. Intrigue over art forgery has been popularized by several contemporary writers in their attempt to titillate the fancy of the uninformed public. This exercise indubitably makes any bureaucrat cringe. Art critic Geraldine Howard, having worked to expose forgers, was intrigued with the public’s fascination with them. The experts don’t have all that much faith in themselves to be absolutely sure it’s a fake as opposed to van Gogh on a bad day. They’re not certain… is how she expressed it.
Taking all the pitfalls into account, ‘curators’ may have a right to feel

title: Chapter 14: The Unknown Turner
content:
Chapter 14
The Unknown Turner Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
The Unknown Turner
Bob’s Journal Dec 2000…
Was a madman? John E. Anderson. Jr. had a thesis, several in fact. These were so far off the mark relative to traditional Turner scholarship that there could be no question. According to the way history has treated him, if not mad, he must have at least been a fool.
As fate would have it, I was able to acquire, not one, but three copies of his rare book published in 1926, The Unknown Turner. The first was somewhat musty and looked as though it was rescued from a shipwreck, but the contents and images were intact. It is a large-format artful publication treading a discordant path between prose, art scholarship, and science. Unfortunately, in his enthusiasm to reinforce his thesis, the author’s tone is quite defensive. One is left with more than a suspicion that he was challenged considerably about his ideas prior to the book’s publication.
For several decades was an avid Turner researcher, and even became a connoisseur, but where did he go wrong? His plausible arguments focus on two main points: that all Turner drawings and watercolours, at least the ones in his own collection were secretly signed; and only half of Turner’s pictures worldwide are accepted as authentic. Of the other half he claimed to have the lion’s share. He reasoned conservatively (in the case of Turner who was wildly prolific) that with two drawings a day for 300 days of the year, and over an active period of sixty years he likely did 36,000 or more drawings and sketches. This included everything from summary sketches to finished masterpieces. Anderson’s number might be as reasonable as the commonly believed number—22,500. In 1820, “Turner traveled to for the first time, producing over 2,000 sketches in two months…” Averaging almost thirty-five a day.
Even accounting for a lifetime obsession, for to accumulate 15,000 drawings in “thirty years of steady, persistent search,” would require a greater acquisition rate than one per day. One would have to be as committed to collecting Turner’s work as the artist himself was to producing it. This passion took back and forth between and numerous times. His sources ranged from naïve dealers to top-end aristocratic collectors. From some of the most respected patrician families of the day, he was even able to buy collated collections of drawings.
Let us assume for a moment that this huge body of work was authentic. What impact would it have, and who might not want to acknowledge its legitimacy? Imagine the monumental task that would be needed to catalogue it! It would then have to be conjoined with the rest of the artist’s accepted work. Turner’s life history would need rewriting, and in doing so there would be a tremendous jolt to the status quo. This of course would debase much Turner scholarship to the chagrin of modern Turner experts. And for those Brits with a financial vested interest—it could certainly drive down the market value of all Turner’s artwork.
According to Eric Shanes, the Turner Bequest alone has always presented the nation with display problems due to its size. In 1960, Sir John Rothenstein admitted that adequate cataloguing had not yet been completed and in all its range the collection was inadequately known. Ironically, at the time he was speaking only about the Tate holdings of Turner’s work. Surely, to ’s detriment, there were no more Turner pictures welcome!
was on the perimeter of art scholarship. Reviewing the times before and after his collecting spree might help to understand an important evolution that effectively sealed his fate. By 1926, when his book was published, seventy-five years after Turner’s death, was just barely getting a handle on the enormous Turner Bequest. Modern-day art scholarship had yet to fully emerge with its fundamental documentary needs. Before this, the gentlemanly activity of art-connoisseurship was private, derived from family wealth, and carried on by people with aristocratic means.
In more recent history and particularly in , industrial fortunes from the nineteenth and early twentieth century became the foundation for acquiring great art treasures from abroad. These new “squillionaires,” as Bernard Berenson referred to them, realized early on that a broader appreciation of culture was needed to make them fully-fledged nobility in their fresh new land. This along with new tax benefits for gifting art to public collections enticed a deluge of art from abroad, which in turn made fortunes for several European dealers. During this upheaval of shifting art fortunes and because of distrust created by voracious competition between dealers and collectors, instability was soon met with demands for greater guarantees. There was apparently decreasing confidence in the old courtly way of doing business, and as chivalrous attitudes waned, the need for documentation became more important. Duveen confirmed that:
The multi-millionaires became much more prudent. They bought from reputable dealers, or employed them to collect for them; but then the professional expert began to assert himself. There were many of these, from the late Doctor Wilhelm von Bode downwards. Many of these experts charged fees for their opinion or their ‘certificates,’ and some even expected a percentage of the value. It cannot surprise anyone that with such a fertile field to exploit, some of these experts became “industrialized.” Soon, work by certain masters had to be “certified” by one or more of the particular specialists in the various schools. What had begun as a most honourable occupation became, in many cases, a furious race for gain, frequently degenerating into the behaviour of a pack of hungry wolves. Those experts who did not accept fees were so jealous that it was often sufficient for one deus ex machina to pronounce a judgment to move another one of the same elevated standing to contradict it. During the last half century, the law courts had to deal with many cases resulting from these internecine wars.
According to Geraldine Keen… support by a recognized expert for a painting can put the monetary value over the top. The power that scholars wield in this way can have at least two unfortunate effects. The more honest would avoid publicly expressing doubts about paintings on the market or in private hands that may pass or have passed for years as definitely the work of the artist in question. The less honest may be prepared to exchange their confirmation of a painting’s authenticity for cash remuneration; there are many certificates of this sort in circulation, some signed by scholars of undoubted eminence.
The cozy payment-for-services relationship between dealers and experts apparently included not only individuals, but also institutions. “Joseph Duveen should have died a baronet, for he was offered a baronetcy in reward of his gift of the Turner wing to the Tate Gallery.” It is difficult to say which is more insidious and which could more likely lead to corruption: the outright payment for services as with Bode, or the giving of high recommendations by noble institutions such as the Tate Gallery. Social advantage bestowed by the latter is of course cyclical and begs mutually supporting patronage. Particularly, in England from ancient times, patronage has been effectively folded into the social fabric under the guise of duty.
After being gifted great collections and large endowments, private or public institutions overgrew private interests and struck a death knell to effective individual involvement. Art history students began to flood out of art schools. This flood was particularly apparent in where new policies required art history departments to be connected to each art school. These were of course not seasoned collectors, but instead university graduates looking for employment and a secure future in a position.
Beyond this apparent dimming of Renaissance thought, there seemed to be a flickering light. From his parents Paul Mellon inherited, in his words, “A desire to own, to enjoy, to savour, and to conserve rare and beautiful things, a desire which must infuse all collectors. Anyone who has felt the impulse to conserve rare and beautiful things has been infected by the virus of collecting. This is one reason the world has its great museums.” Unfortunately, Mellon presents us with an oxymoron as he takes the individual joy of collecting and almost as an afterthought gives it over to the institutional museum.
What was the context within which was struggling? As was mentioned, there were a number of highly successful dealers operating at that time who were instrumental in gathering foreign treasures for those wealthy American collectors. The most notable players at the time were Bernard Berenson and Joseph Duveen. This was a collaborative effort between private scholar and gentleman dealer. Berenson was a young émigré from and eventual Harvard graduate (1887). By studying and carefully cataloguing Italian Renaissance art, his erudite labours formed the model for modern art scholarship. “B. B.’ as he was affectionately called, had read Morelli, Crowe and Cavalcaselle, as well as the new school of art historians who were more scientific and less rhapsodic than their predecessors.”
There was nothing random about Berenson’s approach, and because one of his desires was financial independence, it was this element of his success that came back to haunt him. The suspicious observers, who wanted to believe he was Duveen’s puppet, sullied his reputation by saying he stretched authentications to favour Duveen and Duveen’s clients. This damaging critique may have gone well beyond hurt feelings. When contemplating the donation of his famous home called I Tatti (near ) to Harvard as a post-doctoral study centre, Berenson in late life lamented the path he himself had taken. “He wanted the student body of his new school to

title: Chapter 15: Cool Britannia
content:
Chapter 15
Cool Britannia Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
Cool Britannia
A finely-finished topographical watercolour in Bob’s collection was framed and lettered by hand: “J.M.W. Turner R.A.” by Thos. Agnew and Son, years ago (see image No. 14). It has the best of provenances; it came from the estate of Turner’s friend, Rev. William Kingsley. Giving John Anderson’s theory about hidden signatures the benefit of the doubt, Bob searched long and hard for the mysterious cipher, and although his search was in vain he still refused to ignore the thirty-five years of dedicated effort Anderson applied to his study of JMW.
In his book, Anderson presented a list of 100 London views from his collection, and stated that everyone bears a mark indicating where Turner’s hidden signature and date were to be found, and where it may be found by “any person possessing ordinary eyesight, a fair amount of patience and perseverance, and an open mind.”
Another problem for was his assertion that he had 100 watercolours of . Public institutions in claim a monopoly on this important part of Turner’s production. For to have a monopoly would be, for them, the ideal of course. Sir Edward T. Cook announced that “To many of our foreign visitors, Turner is one of the principal attractions of .”
Yes, when one considers tourism—a monopoly of anything featured in those eminent British institutions would be preferred. Today over two-and-one-half million tourists visit annually, and upwards of three million a year visit certain individual sites. With meals, accommodation and travel this equates to many billions of pounds for the British economy—tourism is clearly an important issue for “Cool Britannia.”
Fate or
As fate would have it, it was Bob’s dubious good fortune to acquire one of those same watercolour drawings from the John Anderson Jr. collection: Off Dover by J.M.W. Turner. It came from the estate of a wealthy North American antiques and art collector who had collected during the 1930s and 1940s, some of the leanest years in the art market, and some of the best years for buying. (this watercolour was subsequently given to The Turner Museum in Florida USA under the care of founder Douglass Montrose-Graem)
Off Dover is an atmospheric little study of sailboats afore the cliffs. This peaceful seascape is pencil drawn and gently washed-over in various tones of blue and grey. Ruskin had one such drawing described as, “a delicate drawing in blue and grey washes.” It was one of the few drawings from the Dr. Monro school c1794-98 that can be firmly attributed to Turner working independently.
Swinburne more accurately dates these earliest blue and grey tinted drawings done while Turner traveled around with “Tom” Girtin. He wrote that they were done in 1795-7, when both Turner and Girtin did much work traveling together in the , the Southern counties, and . Graham Reynolds concurs. During the closing years of the 18th century, early in Turner’s career, the colours in which he composed watercolours were the “low-toned blues and blue-greys which had their own emotional melancholic.” This was a time that Turner visited the southern coast region to enjoy the picturesque qualities of the sea. He likely produced many such drawings. Off Dover has summary notations written directly onto the image, the way Turner often did in order to better recall the occasion. For such sketches, Monro of Novar wrote that “he generally preferred the pencil-point, writing in here and there the colours and effects.”
“George F. Muller, art appraiser, [dies at age] 92,” was the headline of an obituary. After a painting career abroad, Mr. Muller was an independent art appraiser in from 1915 to 1951. He was a thirty-six-year veteran and expert in his field. His other art-related qualifications were impressive, including the exhibition of his own work early in life at the Salon. Muller authenticated Off Dover while it was still with . With all this great provenance and professional opinion, has the art scholarship today changed so much that Sotheby’s should write Bob saying:
My colleague Martin Gallon has passed on your letter and photographs of your watercolour (Off Dover) attributed to J M W Turner. I regret that after careful examination of your photographs, I am unable to support the attribution to Turner. During the past few years considerable research has been done on Turner watercolours and a number of works previously thought to be by him have since been turned down by the leading experts on the artist. This letter from Sotheby’s British paintings and watercolour specialist, Henry Wemys is polite but bereft of substance. It is certain that if all 15,000 works owned by have been circulating in the marketplace for the last fifty years, those same experts have likely been plagued with enquiries. Also they have probably been advised by their predecessors and present overseers to beware of the collection. If these indoctrinates could be advised to produce a form letter in defense of all such North American enquiries, it would certainly streamline tradition.
There are those who might like to see all Turner’s work of lesser importance or dubious provenance, done away with or at least condemned, particularly in light of the glut of his production. Even those with blinders must realize that painting is a learning process that took, even Turner, a lifetime. If he thought any of his own works unworthy, or not necessary to maintain context for the whole lot, he himself would have done the job. He would have destroyed them.
Is it that ‘Turner’ has become more than an artist; perhaps he has become a British institution, and as such must be cosseted to protect his posthumous reputation from the vagaries of the art world? C. Lewis Hind gives an early example how even one of Turner’s major works was exiled: “impatient of The Golden Bough… the trustees of the National Gallery banished it to .”
Ruskin’s ‘take’ on this purging process was to swiftly destroy the nude drawings in the late Turner’s studio. Jack Lindsay claims this was done “on the request of the National Gallery trustees in 1858.” In Ruskin’s own words: “Yes, I burned them all! I’m proud, very proud to have done it.” Ruskin was a religious man and likely assumed that such an act would not only be the moral thing to do but also protect Turner’s fame. It has taken another 150 years of insular faux-erudition to hack away much more of the artist’s oeuvre. In 1975, while reviewing the catalogue of Frans Hals’s work, Christopher White wrote:
“An initial period of discovery is accompanied by a spirit of optimistic expansionism, this is followed eventually by a mood of deep retrenchment in which it almost becomes a matter of honour to reach negative conclusions, so that much of the baby as well as the bathwater tends to be thrown out.”
It is likely that the standard of quality is an issue being debated as much as anything by members of such core groups. And if so, there is the danger that Rembrandt’s baroque visions, or Turner’s Romantic ones are being defined by modern values. This perfidy has undoubtedly resulted in authentic works being lost altogether. Any necessary culling of poor craftsmanship might be left to the master and the master alone, and not to his biased admirers—admirers holding nationalist interests and their own introspective visions in the balance.
There is another point to consider in this equation. Who would know better than those same well-educated and often dreadfully experienced groups, that the public is keen on rarity? Exclusivity is important. Poignant evidence here is the number of people who make an art pilgrimage to the Louvre to see one of the few Leonardos in existence—The Mona Lisa. What better way to make something rare than to eliminate its competitors? What national museum would not enjoy complete control over such a carefully selected trove, particularly if it were by a famous native son like Turner?
Curiously, while most Turner biographers treat Thornbury dismissively, and much of what he recorded as hearsay or invention, they trivialize or fail to mention the significant damage done to Turner by the illustrious John Ruskin. Ruskin had been Turner’s most important early promoter. After becoming disgruntled with much of Turner’s late painterly “inventions,” he sought to somewhat ignore his former idol. His insular religious convictions and desire to maintain Turner’s stellar reputation saw him destroy a highly intimate chapter of Turner’s life. “The prudery of this Victorian has prevented our ever having full knowledge of Turner’s genius as a draftsman of the human figure.”
This is an excerpt. For the full chapter, read Art World’s Dirty Little Secret.

title: Chapter 16: Noblesse Oblige, or Not!
content:
Chapter 16
Noblesse Oblige, or Not! Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
Noblesse Oblige, or Not!
In young democratic , the work ethic was ablaze for . Things were evolving at a revolutionary pace, and it was not so much who you knew, but what you were able to accomplish by due diligence. People in were confident in their ability to succeed without the need to be connected or ennobled, and to progress at a pace commensurate with ability and effort. So entered the fray as an innocent. It is apparent from his actions that he mistakenly felt that after applying conscientious effort, he was able to blaze into the obdurate British art world and make his mark. Ah, but became a victim. As in Bob’s case, what doused his flame was the title-gazing inattention that often accompanies privilege? Fortunately for me, Bob was still smoldering.
’s plans were inarguably altruistic. He wished to have his collection go to the Tate. After reading much about JMW, he had become a Turnerphile. It is clear by his own declaration that he was compelled to act accordingly. Aware of JMW’s ardent love of his native country, endeavoured to find a way that his collection of Turner’s drawings and sketches could become the property of the English nation. It was in deference to what he believed Turner might want that in late life attempted the generous offer of his collection to the Tate Gallery:
I was prepared to make a great monetary sacrifice in order to bring this about… A visit was made to my home, a small portion of my collection was examined, and the receipt of the letter, from which an extract is given, followed: “I have to inform you that your collection is of no interest to this gallery… J. B. Manson, Assistant Director, Tate Gallery, .”
Almost every drawing and sketch shown to the representatives of the Tate Gallery came from either the Landseers (father and son), John Henderson, Lady Leicester, Sir Charles Fellows, or Sir Wilfred Lawson, although no mention of this was made at the time.
Instead of being welcomed “as a possible possessor of valuable material which might prove serviceable in throwing additional light upon the remarkable career of one of the world’s greatest artists,” Anderson was cast off as if he were a peddler of third rate forgeries. Even to the casual observer, a person would have to be at best a lunatic to try and load the Tate full of fake Turners. Dubious works would not stand the test of even a cursory review.
And what about the Tate collection that already existed at the time? Ruskin classified the Turner Bequest of Drawings into three classes according to quality: in the first were Rivers of France (45), Roger’s Poems (57), Rivers and Harbours of (23), and a few others, 135 in all. There were 1757 studies in the second class, and in the third were the drawings in black and white…The third class evidently comprised nine-tenths of the entire collection. Ninety percent of the Bequest is effectively third rate and not worthy of cataloguing. Even most of the second tier fails the test. It has been argued that much of what Turner did not sell was waste, except for a number of key pieces fated for the Nation by the artist early on. At his death, much was discovered haphazardly jammed into drawers. A. J. Finberg believed the fact that Turner, “the water-colour painter is represented at the National Gallery [now in the Tate Gallery] is purely an accident. The bulk of his water colours are in private collections.” The Nation had received some 19,000 works on paper; it becomes evident that most were of curatorial interest only. Plainly, the main catalogue of Turner watercolours produced by Andrew Wilton supports Finberg’s theory since most of his listings are not from the Turner Bequest. The number of major untraced works is also noteworthy. There are many with no history subsequent to the time of ’s pillage. If Turner sold most of the better works in his lifetime, then those available to while sleuthing the English countryside would have been the better pieces.
A group of eighteen Turners recently came up for sale in America. They had been ’s and were being sold with letters written by Anderson himself. According to the vendor, the sale also included a collection of large gilt-bound books: Turner’s Rivers of France, Harbours of England by Turner & Ruskin, Turner Gallery, Turner by Sir Walter Armstrong, and engraved plates for Armstrong’s Turner. They had a recent on-line appraisal valuing them at 50,000 dollars each. There were no takers at an opening required bid of 40,000 dollars for the whole lot. Bob wrote the owner and informed her of the tenuous nature of an provenance: The John Anderson Jr. collection is something I have been researching for a while now, and have talked at length about it to the leading Turner expert, Dr. Martin Butlin. This is the same expert that was co-author of the Oxford Companion to J.M.W. Turner published in 2001, as well as the complete catalogue of Turner’s paintings. I in fact have a watercolour from the collection. Butlin has informed me that he believes all the works from that collection are dubious at best and has described them under the Fakes section in the Oxford Companion. I do not personally believe this and have been working to try and prove that much of ’s collection was okay. The unfortunate thing is that without Butlin’s support, the auction houses and dealers would have a hard time selling them and might even risk legal problems. Is there any way that you could get copies of the letters to me? This might prove helpful to both of us.
Bob has not been able to maintain contact with Nancy from Hotchkiss, Co., . The lack of cooperation is understandable considering that a stranger—Bob, had just given wings to her anticipated fortune.
The question still remained, if those missing watercolours listed in Turner’s catalogue raisonné, had not been banished to obscurity in a foreign land, then why are so many of them untraceable? What other suitable explanation could there be for so many important works becoming lost? Many of them had clear provenance as indicated in ’s catalogue. Agreed, there are likely many of them hidden away, passing from generation to generation without the necessary death duties being levied, but by conservative calculations, Turner produced an enormous number of images that are not presently accounted for. It is often described how Turner’s need to create was compulsive, he was continuously sketching and often in the most unlikely of circumstances.
To inject another dark thought into the mix, one that is more often skirted than debated: the probability that certain works and sketchbooks were stolen from Turner’s studio during his late years. There was a lengthy stretch that he was cohabitating with Mrs. Booth and away from his studio. It is well known that during his feeble years, JMW became unwary and less concerned over his studio.
Human nature being what it is, the lack of security, and the market value of Turner’s work makes it likely that at least some skullduggery took place. Could this be the reason the secretive and suspicious Turner appended so many codicils to his will? Was he angry for some reason? The changes were seemingly meant to exclude people that were formerly near and dear to him, people who would have at least had limited access to the studio during his absence. “One codicil from , revoked the bequests to close relations and his housekeeper.”
“I have to inform you that your collection is of no interest to this gallery….” Clearly it would have been valuable to see the complete letter that received from the Tate Gallery eighty years ago. The ostracized with his narrow concept of the world, blamed his situation on individuals making insular decisions, and although the abuse he suffered had come from a long-established authority, the Tate, he did not believe their verdict for a minute. This connoisseur had come too far in the study of Turner to be misled, but he was ultimately unable, or unwilling, to do much more than express his opinion in a book. The tome was certainly produced for the benefit of posterity with hopes that eventually his unjust treatment would be revisited.
Even a slightly suspicious person struggling to make sense of what happened to might believe there was a plot, one that may carry on today. The only mention warranted in the Oxford Companion to J.M.W. Turner is a couple of lines under the section dealing with fakes and copies. Does this mean that he has been swept away again without adequate appraisal? To be generous, even if one could prove furtive intentions by those making the decisions, those intentions might not be clandestine in the normal sense. They might merely be part of doing one’s job within a milieu of dutiful obligation—something so familiar to the noble system of patronage.
Bob is now at variance with Anderson in his belief that individuals alone are making those obstructive and self-serving decisions, preferring to believe that it is a mob of interconnected self-interests, and although not likely planned as a conspiracy, the consequence adds up to deception of equal significance. It is not only ‘human nature’, as believed, with individuals being vindictive; but instead, human nature on a macro-level. Institutions, governments, art organizations, dealer cartels, expert coalitions, etcetera; all have a vested interest in whatever happens in the art world. In working synergistically, they are naturally subject to being in each other’s pockets. It takes a very special member of any established group to break ranks. It takes integrity on the part of any individual to make judgments for altruistic reasons alone, and unimpeachable honesty to make a judgment contrary to self-interests.
An unfair review of a painting on the part of an individual, group, or institution can happen for a variety of reasons. Selfishness, bad judgment,

title: Chapter 17: Back to Christie's
content:
Chapter 17
Back to Christie’s Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
Back to Christie’s
After several months without contact with Christie’s, Bob finally decided to phone Jeremy Rex-Parkes to determine why his seemingly smooth correspondence had been cut off so abruptly. Jeremy’s cheerful greeting betrayed his own deception. As it turned out he had been forwarding Bob’s e-mails to Mr. Nicholas White of the picture department expecting him to respond to Bob, but for some unknown reason they went no further. According to Jeremy, Mr. White said he answered the inquiries; but the truth of the matter was Bob heard nothing from anyone. After a brooding wait, he could only assume he’d been brushed off.
Now that they were back in touch, he told Jeremy about JMW’s fingerprint. The one discovered by Paul Biró. He was quite intrigued. Bob also prodded him trying to find out why the three-month interruption of communications. It was no secret that I had been shopped-around by many a previous owner, but once Christie’s was shown my persuasive provenance—what was the problem? Was it some message from the Tate that muddied the waters?
The Tate people had been contacted about me many times previously and must have been aware of my sordid legal problems, problems stemming from Visnei’s criminal prosecution of 1982. Was the staff intimidated by such a high level fraud investigation by international law enforcement? It is understandable that an institution such as the Tate would shy from bad publicity.
True, many people for many years tried to flog my not-so-decorative canvas at varying venues without success. It is an axiom of the art world: if a painting is not by a famous artist, and does not have strong art historical value, it had better be “pretty” and have decorative value. The Tate officials were probably weary seeing those wretched victims in my “rescue boat.” There had been three instances alone, where requests were directed to Martin Butlin, the chief for Historic British Art at the Tate.
The somewhat dumbfounded Jeremy again relayed a message from Bob to Nicholas White. By this time Bob was certain he was being evaded, but to his surprise Mr. White called back within ten minutes. He acknowledged that he had previously contacted Martin Butlin and that he was skeptical. White seemed enthusiastic about the new fingerprint evidence though, and assured Bob that he would contact Butlin again. In addition, he promised to contact Evelyn Joll. He explained that Butlin and Joll were the authors of the large two-volume catalogue raisonné of JMW’s work. He said that they had come across the listing of me in the Christie’s catalogue of 1904 (Orrock sale), but at that time were cynical.
This was at a time before such catalogues had photographs and there was only a scant description. They must have assumed I was “in the style of,” or by a “follower of” Turner. One can understand their reticence. Quite fairly, the question could have been posed: “If Shipwreck, the Rescue was right, why had it not at some point been recorded, or been published alongside the artist’s other great early sea pieces.” Both cataloguers where likely at a loss without an image and not knowing my whereabouts. 1904 was well before works were systematically relegated to catalogue raisonnés, and after 1904, I disappeared. Unfortunately, I was unavailable for proper assessment at the time Butlin and Joll had a crack at producing their definitive catalogue raisonné.
When Mr. White consulted the two experts, they must have felt shadowed by a nemesis. The thought must have crossed their minds that I could truly be a great discovery, and they would be the discoverers. At least one of their regrets was that they had expended much effort compiling the most comprehensive listing of the JMW’s paintings, and now after already revising it once, their brainchild was again looking deficient. It was published first in 1977 and again in 1984.
Nicholas White, with renewed enthusiasm requested professional photographs. It had been a year since Jeremy Rex-Parkes had identified the stock number and Bob was only now being asked for photographs. He assembled a complex package of photographs and text, twenty pages in total, including: my front and back, close-up details, images in colour, black and white, and ultraviolet. There were essays about previous ownership, stylistic references, images of the fingerprint comparisons, and a compact disc with some high-resolution details. Bob was uncertain if including the compact disc would prove futile, at the time Martin Butlin was without a computer.
In her report, Marilyn Laver stated that the question of attribution to any particular artist should be addressed by a curatorial expert on Turner’s work based on content and style. In the letter to Christie’s and Butlin, the physical characteristics were emphasized, leaving the visual and style comparisons to Butlin, who hypothetically was the curatorial expert. This broad trust in Butlin’s connoisseurship was an uncomfortable surrender for Bob. And especially after he had just heard a discourse by Professor Susan Greenfield: “Vision is not about simply soaking up the outside world, instead, it is an active process which invents, ignores, and distorts what is entering through the eyes.”
A century ago, Robert de la Sizeranne described Turner’s own special facility for mingling perception and invention with the natural. There are two modes of being naturalistic: “doing what Nature has realized or doing what Nature can realize; copying her results, or inspiring oneself by her laws. Turner did not perhaps paint any one of his pictures ‘after’ Nature, and some hundreds he certainly painted without having Nature before his eyes. Is such a thing legitimate? Is it absurd? May it not be necessary sometimes?”
In light of the mind’s capacity to autonomously invent, one must be left with uncertainty over connoisseurship generally. The hypothesis of Professor Rudolfo Llinas from New York University is that “seeing is just another form of dreaming; dreaming and being awake are next of kin if not exactly the same thing… the brain is about making images.” Conversely, if the visual can be explained sensibly using content and style arguments, then surely, connoisseurship deserves due regard despite the potential pitfalls, or delusions. There was little choice under the circumstances anyway, Christie’s held complete sway over the situation and Butlin’s opinion was to be their final word.
Included in the same letter was an extensive interpretation of the letter to Thomas Griffith dated . It was the possible reference by Turner to Shipwreck, the Rescue calling it a Stormy Picture. The word ‘Hero’ in Turner’s letter could have led to the assumption that the “stormy” painting under discussion was Hero and Leander, but this idea was dismissed by John Gage in his book on Turner’s correspondence. Not impossible, but it would be somewhat difficult to believe that a painting that was exhibited at the R.A. in 1837 (Hero and Leander) would so desperately need dragging from its dark abode for lining and cleaning seven years later.
The condition had suffered according to Walter Thornbury, but even if it were bad enough to need such radical treatment, the image of Hero and Leander, although ominous, does not necessarily bring to mind a full-blown storm. Luke Herrmann calls it “turbulent splendour.” In 1807, when negotiating a trade of paintings to regain ownership of Shipwreck (My younger brother), JMW again used the title, Storm to describe the painting. What “stormy picture” was he describing in his letter to ? I’m certain it was me.
In the above letter, the discussion centered on the more vulnerable “early large works” and their state of dirty neglect. You should have seen me back then, I was a mess! I was painted early in 1802. Yeah, that would be about it, 1802. JMW wrote Griffith that the “large Pictures I am rather fond of tho it is a pity they are subject to neglect and dirt… if I could find a young man acquainted with picture cleaning and would help me to clean accidental stains away, would be a happiness to drag them from their dark abode. The Stormy Picture you saw in the Parlour for Mr. Foord’s Hero to advise with about cleaning and lining…” Yep, now I remember.
The noteworthy interest that Griffith showed for that neglected “stormy picture” in 1844 was clearly the inducement for JMW, in the autumn of his life, to refurbish me for presentation to his dear friend, respected business associate and executor—Thomas Griffith. As noted earlier, according to Christie’s archive, I was presented by JMW to Mr. Griffiths [sic]. “Turner sometimes, but seldom gave away pictures,” although Thornbury discovered that he seldom visited Mr. Griffith without bringing his touched proofs as a present. The generosity JMW felt towards his friendly agent was sincere.
JMW proposed to gift a picture to each of his executors including Thomas Griffith, presumably for their services in that official capacity. In one of the codicils to his will Turner proposed leaving each of his executors a picture. “They were to choose for themselves.” The Chancery court decided that Turner was not competent at the time he added this particular codicil to his will and thus cancelled it.
JMW had been known to trade artwork for services in the past and particularly with . One oft-quoted case took place in 1842, when sold nine watercolours for Turner. Ten were painted and Ruskin tells us that the tenth was offered to the dealer in lieu of commission. “ agreed, and Turner went home content.” It’s hard to remember exactly what happened back then, but if I was not entirely a gift, I may have been bartered to , and remained a somewhat private holding in memory of JMW. Ruskin wrote a friend that other artworks had often been exchanged for a Turner picture.
