title: Chapter 18: Muddled Intentions
content:
Chapter 18
Muddled Intentions Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
Muddled Intentions
Government regulation of historic artwork is one peripheral issue that concerns art experts and affects the value of artwork. It is easier to move, sell, export, and bequeath decorative art—that which does not fall into the restrictive category of national treasure. Once catalogued and traceable it becomes risky to do anything with it across borders without proper documentation. And by now I had slipped out of without being duly recognized. Who had been responsible for this faux pas?
The state of affairs for cultivated art lovers in is even more tenuous. The unfortunate French not only have counter-productive import and export regulations, but “they are taxed on their net worth, and as a result they are often reluctant to disclose ownership of valuable works of art.” It seems unjust that because some have a penchant for beautiful and rare objects, they are penalized by way of wealth taxes. “This type of taxation is certainly detrimental to genteel aspirations and indubitably fosters philistinism,” I’m quoting Bob here.
Competition between public institutions is one reason that certain important artwork is disparaged. As each competes to increase its holdings, monies by way of benefaction, attendance for their exhibitions, prestige, and government support; it becomes evident why a request for help in assessing a painting that comes from outside their own clique might go unheeded. It must be acknowledged that since the ‘competitive public museum’ has become politically and economically dominant in the art world, things have irreversibly changed. Disregard by curators for the private collector seems forever entrenched.
Irrationally, this is the same authority needed to bestow surnames to misunderstood “orphans,” those not yet rediscovered masterpieces. Such indifference or conflict of interest is disheartening when I contemplate how many of my brethren have likely been discounted. Soon it would become glaringly apparent to Graham and Bob that JMW’s oeuvre in particular has endured the cruel scrubbing of an unkind “guardian!”
Several years ago, Bob received a gracious letter from Alistair Smith. It came after an audience with him and the restorer, Anthony Reeve at the National Gallery in . In it, he categorically confirmed the exclusion of outsiders: “As a member of staff of the National Gallery, I am not allowed to authenticate paintings in private hands, and while I do not wish to be obstructive, I would be very grateful if you would remove my name from any printed materials which you are to issue.”
This attitude is prevalent within universities as well. Much of the present art establishment propagates the belief that all significant and relevant art should be public art, and in many cases public property. In his autobiography written over a quarter century ago, the former longtime curator of the National Gallery in Washington, John Walker, was already concerned over the shift in museum politics, but at the time defined it in terms of a clouded search for “relevance (a term its proponents never clearly define). The desire to be considered ‘relevant,’ vague as it is, seems to denote fewer funds for collecting and more for social purposes.” He still believed that museums should be places of enjoyment and enlightenment, and should not function in community relations, in solving racial problems, in propaganda for any cause.
Henry McBride, formerly America’s most prominent art critic, who started with The New York Sun in 1913 and finished with its demise in 1950, was incensed by what was happening around him. “In the 1930s he bore down heavily on propaganda in art which he felt was swamping the American scene.” Add the edict Berenson affixed to his post-doctoral study centre in : “I want this Institute to promote aesthetical and humanistic, rather than philosophical and antiquarian interests,” and a picture of the political climate that led up to the modern era begins to emerge.
In , the influential critic, Anthony Blunt, chose to see in Marxism, the cure for every aesthetic ill. He believed that “the New Realists, the Mexican painters Diego Rivera and José Orozco had demonstrated the way to paint the human condition without compromising their aesthetic responsibility…The great advantage of socialism was that it would selectively retain what was valuable in bourgeois culture and jettison the rest. The state, all controlling, enlightened and wise, would take over the role once played by the patron.” One does not have to think that Marxism was necessarily the root evil here. In 1930s Germany, with Mein Kampf, Hitler echoed the same manipulative sentiment when he insisted that theatre, art, literature, films, the press, posters, and window displays had to be “regulated to serve the state and civilization.”
This concept of institutional and government control was eventually also adopted by the British art establishment, but in their case it was embraced mainly to promote national and commercial interests. Even Kenneth Clark in the 1930s was a short-term proponent of this ostensibly unworkable ideology. There is no doubt that was then sympathetic to socialist aims. “It is evident,” he wrote to his mother early in the Second World War, “that socialism is the only solution. A centralized government would be bound to have flaws but to have profit as the only motive is even more dreadful.”
Little did he realize that eventually his youthful mutterings would in a paradoxical fashion become somewhat reality. Yes, overwhelming control would develop, but it would be positioned mostly in the hands of the aristocratically managed British government. The elite few in control were to benefit commercially to an even greater extent than the private collector had ever done. The further one moves away from the weaker the grip; but assuredly, the probing fingers of this gentrified class wander internationally.
With such eminent art historians and leaders reading from the same page, it is no wonder that the direction of attitudes has shifted away from the individual collector. An intellectual Marxist like Blunt needed little persuasion to believe that the best hope of preserving common European heritage from what Steiner terms “prostitution of the money market’ was by allying himself with the Communist system that not only promised but ostensibly did make public all private art collections.” It is interesting to note that Martin Butlin attended the Courtauld Institute during Anthony Blunt’s tenure.
Bob’s lack of success in dealing with Alistair Smith at the National Gallery was the first time he had experienced the art world running scared. For many years John Walker had encouraged the vetting of art at the National Gallery in , and “he always felt it was wrong that so many museums worried either by the possibilities of lawsuits or out of ineptitude and laziness, refused to help the public in what he considered an essential way.”
Institutional employees that refuse to give their fair, honest, and diligent opinion on privately owned artworks, and even for those traditionally accepted pieces, betray integrity as well as the public trust. Whatever the reason for their negligence, their espoused mandate to protect rare art treasures regardless of where they are found has been compromised. If something is not fully appreciated, it will be less protected. If a work of art is not given credit for being authentic by those in authority, chances are, by virtue of unintended consequences it will eventually be lost or destroyed.
The misanthropic ideal that holds political viewpoints above the search for truth, and the procrastination induced by fear of litigation are sure signs of decay within any system. It cannot be denied that some of those heavily endowed institutions are doing important things in the way of education and availing treasures to a broader public, but some of what is going on would certainly be a shock to those ‘late’ private collectors who made it all possible in the first place with their munificent bequests. They would certainly be disenchanted by the inharmonious insult to collectors orchestrated by their misguided progeny, and from within the same museums that carry their names.
The shift in trust toward institutional authority is largely misplaced according to author Huon Mallalieu. He wrote that “it is the private collector who often knows more than the seemingly grander and confident experts at museums and auction houses. The reason for this is obvious: he has to back his eye with his own money. The others may lay their reputations on the line, but not, in the short term, their livelihoods.”
Here, the person who comes to mind first is my former owner James Orrock. Not only did he take the necessary risks and buy as much art as he could afford, he did it fruitfully as a confident self-taught expert. The art critic for the Athenaeum wrote that “one is always glad, whatever the reason, when one provokes Mr. Orrock into epistolisation. He never takes pen in hand without advancing the cause of true art, the cause we all have at heart.”
How the early inquiries about me were handled by Christie’s was interesting. Apparently, after many miscommunications, Bob never did talk to the senior person in the department. This may have been a strategy, a way of distancing anyone with decision-making power from the issue. It was probably presumed that if need be, the whole matter could be more easily dropped or at least ignored. When the fingerprint evidence was introduced, the matter became serious again. This was likely for worries over potentially intrusive publicity. Fingerprint evidence found on a painting of such importance that had been rejected for so many years was sure to generate public and media interest.
Inherent in the art world are numerous prejudices which often express themselves in the

title: Chapter 19: The Stage Was Set
content:
Chapter 19
The Stage Was Set Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
The Stage Was Set
The stage was set. All the efforts of the last several years were to be decided worthy or worthless in one short moment, the length of time it took to grimace or grin. Martin Butlin had agreed to come to and assess me firsthand. The subject of much misfortune and dubious criticism for decades, I was now about to take my just place in history. Or was I? What if Butlin, one of the most renowned and politically influential British art experts condemned me?
Arrangements for the trip began with Mrs. Butlin answering the telephone in her flat in —her husband was out. According to Bob she had a very light British accent and the tone of her voice spoke of polite modesty. He hoped when phoning back in an hour, as she suggested, he would find Martin Butlin of the same temperament. He was not disappointed; his tension was relieved by Butlin’s overt friendliness. Bob described the Okanagan and as a great place to vacation, and once he was assured that Butlin was willing to make the journey, he invited his wife to join him. Bob suggested they treat the trip like a mini-vacation.
Bob was encouraged when, after seeing the photographs he gave the painting a fifty-fifty chance of being right. Nicholas White had used a figure of only ten percent. Two points that Butlin seemed to bristle over during the telephone conversation were: the provenance as it related to James Orrock, and the fingerprint evidence. Of course the latter was the strongest available proof that could dislodge a curatorial opinion. This inauspicious hint might have sparked a horrible premonition, but Bob was unwilling to fall prey to paranoia at this warm and fuzzy point in their early relationship.
Butlin also disputed whether all the pieces from the 1904 Orrock sale had been genuine. This disparagement seemed misplaced considering Orrock’s fame as a connoisseur. I can vouch that he was one of the main experts of his era. Many an influential visitor to his home, many of whom brushed me with their cloaks, were awed by what they saw—including John Ruskin, and Cosmo Monkhouse—both JMW experts.
Not only had Orrock emulated JMW throughout his own painting career, he had been responsible for recording for posterity some of JMW’s secret working methods. Martin’s incredulous declaration did not account for the unimpeachable provenances previous to Orrock—that of Griffith and Sir Charles Robinson. Of the many Turner pictures that Orrock owned in the late 19th century, there were some that shocked the auction world with their high prices. Walton Bridges, which sold as the ‘lot’ just before me, and Rockets and Blue Lights which brought an international record 25,000 pounds nearly a century ago. It is obvious that Orrock was not in the habit of buying dubious works.
Of Orrock’s oil paintings listed in the B & J Catalogue, all of them found their way to prestigious collections: Mountain Landscape with a Lake (B & J 38) to the Fitzwilliam Museum, Cambridge; Walton Bridges (B & J 63) to the National Gallery of Victoria, Melbourne; Newark Abbey on the Way (B & J 65) to the Yale Center for British Art; Rockets and Blue Lights (B & J 387) to the Sterling and Francine Clark Art Institute; and, Off the Nore: Wind and Water (B & J 476) to the Yale Center for British Art.
On reviewing his first telephone conversation with Butlin, Bob reasoned that my pedigree might not have been the clear target of malice; it was at the point when he mentioned Ehrenbrietstein that the rebuff occurred. He had mentioned that it had also come from the Orrock collection. Butlin then muttered the cruel snipe. But more than anything it was the attack on the fingerprint evidence that set Bob aback. Butlin seemed confident that generally speaking, fingerprints were being faked—a friend had told him so. The Internet did not reveal any information about this type of fraud so Bob decided to get a reaction from one who should know better than anyone—Peter Paul Biró. E-mail May 23 01: “Bonjour Paul, I hope this email finds you and yours happy and in good health. I’ve been told that fingerprints are now being faked. Is this possible? If so, how would this affect using them as evidence to support a painting’s authenticity?” The next day Paul replied. He assured Bob there was no worry, “the fakes are easy to catch by an expert examiner—they just look poor—a rubber stamp usually.”
I caught Bob mumbling about the overly tight collaboration that existed for many years among members of the Turner cult. Those connected to special interest groups as diverse as the Victoria and Albert Museum, National Gallery, British Museum, Tate Gallery, the twin firms of Christie’s and Sotheby’s, and a limited number of connected dealers. Some of these tight-knit relationships were soon to be revealed during casual conversations with Bob’s visitor from abroad. In the meantime, he remained troubled over how many of the people within Butlin’s circle had been acrimonious toward me for so many years. He worried, how could they possibly support me now and at the same time save face and maintain allegiances.
There was more that added to his discomfort. What about the hapless sting when I was embroiled in not only legal wrangling, but also criminal ones? How did this make Butlin feel? Certainly he knew many of the sordid details and as rumour might have it, probably more than what actually took place. Such a kafuffle would make any civil servant with a secure job reticent about getting involved.
It was easy to reason that there were a number of tentacles attached to the beast that bated Bob and Graham. They were only beginning to understand where these were anchored but were yet oblivious to the underlying ogre—the system of royal honours and patronage. Patrician support often relates to prestige, power, and money. This is not a brilliant revelation by any means; aspirations such as these have been rooted in British culture for many centuries. Was an agent of this same giant to arrive before long on Bob’s doorstep here in ?
E-mail from Martin Butlin (excerpt):
I could come on my own, flying out from on Saturday 26th May and returning on Thursday 31st. Unfortunately, my wife, who is a professional picture restorer, has a number of commitments that make it impossible for her to take time off at the moment.
We could both come for about a week, flying out anytime on or after 17th August. I imagine, however, that you would not want to put things off so long.
You very kindly said that you would cover my expenses, basically the fare plus accommodation. However, before you commit yourself I would like to repeat my opinion, based on a photograph of the picture, that the painting is more likely not to be by Turner than to be genuine—this just to make sure that there is no misunderstanding!
It was most kind of you to invite my wife as well but, unfortunately, it does not look as if this will be possible unless you are prepared to wait until August. My wife sends her greetings and warmest thanks for your generosity; she is only sorry that it does not look as though she will be able to take advantage of this.
Bob sent a quick reply: It would be disappointing if your wife were unable to accompany you, so I suggest we wait until August. I am pleased to hear that she is a picture restorer; …. Have you had the opportunity to read the multi-page document prepared about the painting? Mr. White has a copy. Please let me know your decision, and if you decide on the later date I will send you some information about the sunny Okanagan.
The dates were soon set, and the Butlins were coming for the third week in August. The itinerary was slowly taking shape and along with a package of information about British Columbia and the bank draft for their airfare, Bob took the opportunity to do a little more proselytizing over me—particularly about the 1844 letter to Griffith.
Bonding and Bantering
This is an excerpt. For the full chapter, read Art World’s Dirty Little Secret.

title: Chapter 20: The Arrival
content:
Chapter 20
The Arrival Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
The Butlin Visit
Assumptions made previously by Bob and Graham were based on events that occurred historically. Now it was time to meet one of the most prominent art world insiders in person. Was the clandestine nature of art world activity at the uppermost level about to be unveiled? To avoid forgetting any of the points that Martin might offer about me and the other paintings, Graham decided to wear a recording device—a wire. Hah, this was starting to sound quite James Bond.
With the microphone pinned to his chest, and ever ready to garner another piece of the art world puzzle, Graham persevered the full week. The attention that Martin gave to their questions and the general chatter during excursions and at mealtimes was enlightening. Hour after hour, during simple socializing, or testy disagreements, Graham discreetly hovered in the wings. Martin Butlin, the accepted authority on historic British art and in particular on JMW Turner and William Blake had been authorized as a senior consultant for Christie’s to inspect me. But more importantly, he provided the vital information needed to confirm Bob and Graham’s hypotheses that the clandestine intentions and actions of those in positions of influence have the art world running scared.
By the time Martin and his wife arrived, their visit had evolved far beyond what was first intended; a trip of pure business had become a vacation with a purpose. The tourist adventure provided for them was ample, and the careful planning paid off. Everything went like clockwork. The distance from home, casual social environment, and the “straight-forward and simple people” impression Martin had about Canadians, disarmed him. His banter over the next several days wrote the concluding few chapters of a book that would have otherwise been only speculation.
Bob assumed that one of his strategies during the visit would be to protect interests back home. As a Commander of the (C.B.E.), and thus “keeper” of British best interests, his tenacity was admirable and unshakable. Churchill would have been proud. It also became apparent during the many hours of intense discussion over art that the position he had as Keeper at the Tate was as much a personal philosophy to be upheld, as it was a job to be done. Was it also a charge that Butlin carried with him over to Christie’s as senior consultant after he retired and his administrative responsibilities at the Tate were divided?
It appeared that he had in the past ignored all inquiries about me or at best treated them lightly. Requests to the Tate for assistance had taken place over many years, frustrating the previous owners in the process, and it was not until Martin was tempted to Canada that he was finally obliged to defend his mandate and personal ideology and give a concrete opinion on me. Of the hard evidence gathered—that in most courts would conclusively support a JMW Turner authentication, Martin’s contradictory and at times befuddled attempts at denying it, were as much humorous as they were exasperating. His crafty arguments against the evidence might have seemed less futile if he had all his facts to begin with. If he had read carefully the information Bob sent him beforehand he would have been more prepared to ward off the onslaught of both scientific and stylistic arguments, or else accept them.
Regardless, he was the expert that everyone must defer to on questions of authenticity, because, in his field he effects a “monopoly” with the two most powerful auction firms. And if any other firm or dealer had the audacity to sell a newly discovered or rediscovered Turner outside this rarified circle, they would not only risk low prices, but also risk being sued for fraud. Without Martin’s support it was clear that a work could never be properly accepted, gain a fair and equitable status, or bring a fair and equitable price. Yes, with respect to Turner, Martin’s dominant position was synonymous with the two closely-knit auction firms, which in the 1990s controlled ninety percent of the four-billion-dollar worldwide auction trade—Christie’s and Sotheby’s.
Evening Aug 18 Arrival
Bob’s Journal entry, Aug 2001…
At the airport as people were trickling down the offloading ramp, without needing to ask, I recognized our guests immediately. They seemed melded: , a sturdy mother figure, handsome for her years and the shorter Martin who seemed tucked into her munificent bosom as they carried on a deep conversation. More than anything else, it was his tailored suit and keen eyes that gave them away.
Oddly, one of the first things said was about business and money, and how important they were to art. I thought this irrelevant at the time, interjected aimlessly within the first few moments of our meeting, and even by the time Martin and Frances left for home, I had still not resolved this puzzle, although it was repeated similarly on one other occasion. Was this a hint that a fee should be paid for Martin’s expertise? If this was true, surely I would have heard about it long before they arrived. The only overt agreement we had made about my financial commitment was the weeklong all-expense paid visit to Canada. There might have been something missed in the translation; but nonetheless, later I learned that Christie’s was paying a per diem fee.
According to James Henry Duveen, “by the early part of the twentieth century, presents and douceurs to such experts had gradually changed into very high fees. Even the redoubtable Dr. Wilhelm von Bode, head of the imperial museums, began to expect more than just a present to one of his museums. Such arrangements with the experts did not remain a trade secret, but for example, came out under cross-examination in the sensational lawsuit in about the portrait La Belle Ferronniere.” Might this still be the case! Surely, Martin would have been forthright about the issue if it was.
On our short journey from to , Martin talked about many of his adventures abroad, and was in the back seat with Graham getting a sense of her new surroundings. Her questions about local affairs, climate, the educational system, and of course Ogopogo, our legendary lake monster were answered by Graham with enthusiasm. That evening beside , Christine joined us for dinner at Salty’s, one of our favourite little ethnic restaurants. The taperecordings proved useless. The loud speakers that were meant to entertain with the likes of Bob Marley were positioned directly over head. The boisterous pedestrian traffic and the fact that Martin sat on the wrong side of Graham for the microphone to be effective were two other vexations.
Our new acquaintances were more than entertaining—they proved to be the lead characters in a bizarre drama that was to play itself out over the ensuing week. Not only did Martin command attention, he also demanded it with a continuum of rhetoric, including anecdotes that entertainingly came across as tall tales. His talent for dramatic inflection and his impeccable timing was that of a great comedian. His frequent roars of laughter, groans of despair, and cracking retorts, might even at times convince those present that they were at a Broadway production. Needless to say, his personality must have gone a long way in that potent world he was connected to for so many years as a bureaucrat. Could this same personality and cleverness have kept that politically volatile environment off guard, working in his direction, and easier for him to control? What gave special zest to his Spy versus Spy delivery for many of his reminiscences and accounts was certainly his great interest in detective stories and crime fiction, the literature he seemed to cherish most of all. …End journal entry. Yes Bob, but it is also vital to appreciate the contributions made by . She was helpful for the pauses she provided during discussions—they were somewhat like intermissions. At times, her comments hit the mark, but frequently her lingering expletives did nothing more than stop conversation. She always began with such charming nobility, but often the beginning was all she issued before a great long pause. Martin, who was ever anxious to tell a new story or repeat one, always filled the void. Expressions such as: “Hand A,” “yes-yes-yes,” “that’s marvelous but…” or just plain—“but”! used the singular “but” so frequently that everyone was constantly looking over their shoulder to see if she was calling out derogatorily. Discounting when she was defending Martin from attacks against his oftentimes-impossible logic, she was very sweet to have around. You must admit.
Complementary to ’ seeming vacuity you played a similar role. While searching for what was really going on in Martin’s mind, your questions often sounded uninformed, and occasionally even bigoted. You must have assumed that was doing the same with you—testing your knowledge, and possibly even your potential risk as a foe.
Graham was cool, mostly taciturn, but at times he shot from the hip with lucid remarks and pointed questions. His attempts to catch Martin contradicting himself were mostly veiled in easy conversation. Was I the only one that noticed that by the end of the week both Frances and Martin were increasingly careful answering questions… if they would answer them at all!
A.m. Aug 19 In the boudoir
“It’s a slow process,” was Martin’s first statement after seeing me for the first time. His privileged decision was not to be given immediately. For Martin, could seeing me for the first time have been like looking at an envelope in the post with his name on it, without knowing who sent it? His indecision was welcome; the visit was to last a week and there was certainly no need for a hasty verdict.
Christine and Frances carried on their visit talking about local affairs while Martin, Graham and Bob started up the stairs. My centralized placement on the wall in the living-room

title: Chapter 21: Portrait of a Soul
content:
Chapter 21
Portrait of a Soul Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
A.m. Aug 20—Portrait of a Soul
The dinner went well but the zealous entertainment that evening precluded any tape-recording. George Kayser had all the time needed to observe his subject in many of his varied poses. As always, Martin was alive to the moment.
George, a renowned expatriate Czech artist had been living in Canada since the late 1960s, when the Soviets took control of his homeland. It forced him and his young family out in search of greener pastures, and less politically charged ones. As a leading exponent of the Modern art movement, he gained significant stature and financial success before he was forced to flee. Once arriving in , he struggled to get his work understood, never mind marketed and sold. Now, as a vigorous old man his appreciation for life is beyond financial success. His grandchildren, his pipe, Czechoslovakian beer, and a swim in Okanagan Lake—this complex of simple pleasures is what fashions the personal Shangri-La of his twilight years.
He is a master at capturing personalities, but more importantly, he has an unusual ability to paint people while they move—skills he mastered while painting conductors and quartets during their performances. This was utterly essential for painting a dynamic person like Martin.
To get a good personality portrait Bob knew it was better if George had the opportunity to observe his subject at length beforehand, and this wanted doing while Martin was being as uninhibited as possible. It was best that Martin was unaware of the stealthy goal. At the Theo’s dinner party George sat directly across from Martin. By the end of the evening, Martin was told about the proposed portrait—he agreed whole heartedly. The next morning, before George arrived with his easel and paint box, Martin had already been dodging art questions for an hour. This was ideal. Martin was energized in a way he most likely felt familiar, the immediacy of the resulting visage said it all (see image No. 12).
Both he and Frances were astounded that it took so little time to complete—one and a half hours. They were also enthusiastic about the results. coddled Martin by disagreeing with him when he complained, “It makes me look much younger.” Discussions about Kayser and his expressive talent, particularly for doing portraits came up several times during their weeklong visit. With portraiture somewhat out of fashion it is rare to find an artist like Kayser. Jokingly, Graham commented that even the Queen of had difficulty finding an artist that could do the job right. He had apparently seen a recent attempt.
Evening Aug 20 Magnum Dinner
Bob’s Journal entry…
The winery visits that were planned for the afternoon went like clockwork. We visited Cellars as part of our wine tasting tour because it was on the way home for the Kaysers. George had created a large exterior mosaic for the winery several years earlier (see image No. 19). It proved a splendid photo opportunity. After a few more wine tastings in the Naramata area, the Butlins were dropped back to their hotel to rest. Before long, the five of us including Graham were seated for dinner at Magnums on the lake, located in the Butlin’s hotel.
Lighthearted chatter and well-primed imaginations led us on a few of Martin’s early travel adventures while he was installed at the Tate Gallery. Martin was certainly no stranger to North America, and his first visit described as a ‘grand tour’ lasted three and a half months. In the he was already seen as somewhat of a celebrity.
His experiences were varied: from being held up in a frigid little domicile in Louisiana where noses were turning blue from an overzealous air-conditioner, to his royal treatment in a chauffeur-driven limousine in New York. The opulence of the Vanderbilt mansion impressed him of course, but his excitement was most evident while discussing, for example, the swamps and alligators of the south, that mysterious setting for his favorite literature—detective thrillers.
There were apparently a lot of Turners in private collections in the at the time, at least until Gowing, Martin’s senior at the Tate organized an exhibition in with Martin acting as expert. It was early in Martin’s career, but he fulfilled his purpose as the incredulous art expert quite admirably. The cover for this operation was the exhibition, Turner: Imagination and Reality held at the in 1966. Martin made it clear that he dismissed all the Turner paintings brought in for assessment.
In support of this exigency, Gowing had a manifesto: “Our own frame of aesthetic reference is permissive to the point of chaos.” It is clear that permissive attributions were not to be tolerated. This harsh position must have fostered fear in the young Martin—forging a similar hard-line attitude. 1966 was a massacre.
Pardon me. There was one exception to the carte blanche dismissal of those formerly-accepted Turner paintings brought in for assessment at the exhibition. It was a “very fascinating” watercolour from the daughter of Mrs. Vanderbilt. Of the Americans, the Vanderbilts and Mellons seemed to bring the warmest memories for Martin. There was in fact, a strong link between the Vanderbilts and British aristocracy. Consuelo Vanderbilt became the Duchess of a generation before Martin’s own era.
The exhibition was also the first chance Martin had to condemn some of those “ Turners” that he had heard so much about. According to his recollection, the following is how one of those watershed days unfolded (an excerpt):
This was the famous exhibition arranged by the amazing man called Gowing. This was Turner, the modern. It absolutely established Turner in smart circles in . Most New Yorkers wouldn’t [normally] look at anything older than five years. Then suddenly they saw Turner… Then of course it meant that everyone brought in their famous Turners [to be assessed]. This is when I began to see these Turners, which I talked about, these amateur things. I’d never seen them before.
One day there was waiting for me in this office space I worked in—a very good watercolour. I said: “Well, this is really quite something, whom does it belong to?” “A member of staff,” was the answer. “Why isn’t she here?” and to Martin’s query came the telling reply, “She was frightened you’d dismiss it like you’d dismissed everything else!” “Well this is fascinating,” continued Martin, “where did she get it?” Reply: “Well her mother is a Vanderbilt.”
Martin certainly learned to appreciate this aristocratic American family early; they had chauffeured him around and taken him to their art laden “three hundred room mansion” to see the proverbial “Turner in the dining room.” This relates to a whimsical story Martin told about how the wealthy in would impress others of the same ilk during dinner parties by displaying their would-be Turners in the dining room.
Knowing the directorship makeup of the and the titled British interests involved with the exhibition, it was possible to make a rough correlation between money in and aristocratic privilege in . Certainly the names listed, like Rockefeller, Phillips, Whitney, and Ford would mesh comfortably with the names on the British side of the equation. Names such as: His Excellency Sir Patrick Dean, Sir Collin Anderson, The Right Honourable Viscount Radcliffe, and Sir Frank Francis, The Right Honourable Lord Robbins, and Sir Philip Hendy.
Our dinner party was the first opportunity for us to corner Martin about the legendary John Anderson Jr., the author of The Unknown Turner. Martin was the authority who had condemned the book to the fakes and copies section of the Oxford Companion, so presumably we were about to find out the true story.
This is an excerpt. For the full chapter, read Art World’s Dirty Little Secret.

title: Chapter 22: Disrespectful Tomato Indeed
content:
Chapter 22
Disrespectful Tomato Indeed Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
A Very Disrespectful Tomato Indeed
Journal entry continued (Magnum’s)…
Even Martin expressed certain doubt that anyone younger than the young Turner could have painted Shipwreck, the Rescue: “The thing is, people can always say, well, you know—if it’s not Turner, well, who else could do it? But in fact, there was, whatever, whoever… there was always somebody else who could perhaps do it. Not necessarily do it, but.”
Above this bit of doublespeak Martin remained unflinching; he maintained that by virtue of a lengthy study of Shipwreck at the , some “great admirer” had at the end of the day copied the heart from the great Shipwreck. Martin believed that his argument was strengthened further by comparing similarities between Shipwreck, and Shipwreck, the Rescue. Ah, but there was a fatal flaw to this reasoning! What was it?
Bob: Well, Shipwreck, the Rescue is very accomplished.
Martin with modest agreement: Uhum.
Bob: Especially the figures in the boat and the way they’ve been developed into that interwoven turmoil. It’s really quite exciting.
It has been noted by other students of his work that, “Turner frequently treated humanity rather as a mass, a force of life, than as individuals.”
Graham: Every time I look at that boat I see another limb somewhere, it’s just so complicated. Actually, what makes me think that it is directly related to the Shipwreck is looking at the costumes. I’ve been able to find almost the identical costumes, which makes me think that whoever did it, knew Shipwreck intimately.
Martin: Yeah. If it is not Turner—if it is not Turner, it’s somebody who saw the Shipwreck.
Graham: And studied it probably.
“Yeah, at the ,” Martin declared.
Bob: So, if it is not Turner, it is somebody that has seen Shipwreck?
Martin insisted: But there was no difficulty there, there was no difficulty there because it was exhibited at the !
Of course, Martin was wrong about Shipwreck being exhibited at the . This mistake left him in a difficult position for his follow-up report. Except for a short controversial stay elsewhere, Shipwreck had been held within the close confines of Turner’s own private gallery. Knowing what we know about Turner’s character, he certainly would not have allowed anyone to pull a chair up to Shipwreck in order to plagiarize.
Hamerton assures us that his need for privacy was so strong, “he would admit nobody into his painting room.” Turner’s guarded personality would have made it impossible to copy anything in his studio. “Turner would himself refuse young artists permission to make sketches of his works when they visited him.” “The difficulty with which his patrons; and even his relations obtained access to his presence was well known.” “He never would suffer me to see him draw, but concealed all that he did,” said one architect; when Mr. Britton entered the artist’s studio, “in an instant, Turner covered up his drawings.”
Nor would the intermediate owner of Shipwreck, Sir John Leicester have made the painting available for copying during its short stay with him. His wife wanted rid of the ‘sea-disaster’ because of its gloomy nature and because it was a painful reminder of a tragedy in her own family, “Lady Leicester having lost a favourite nephew at sea was unable to bear the association called up by the scene.” It certainly would not have been front and center at the ’s while they prepared to do a quick exchange with Turner for a less personal subject, Schaffhausen.
To make his theory come together, Martin reinforced his mistake by certifying the as the venue at which Shipwreck was copied. Calmly for the most part, except for the odd outburst of conciliatory laughter, he fought this point relentlessly as we all mumbled over dinner.
It was prophetic that Martin brought up Turner’s, The Wreck of a Transport Ship. This was because of its ostensibly one-of-a-kind pictorial device—the sailor in the back of the rescue boat who is seen trumpeting for help with a voice trumpet. This device is also found in Shipwreck, the Rescue. So if it were not the same hand that painted both paintings, the earlier painting would have had to inform the latter. There was no argument that Shipwreck, the Rescue was painted earlier, and therefore, would have informed Wreck of the Transport Ship. In fact the latter painting was not seen until fifty years later when it was exhibited at the British Institute in 1849, and thus could not have been used as a model for this trumpeting reference.
Shipwreck, the Rescue detail of figure using a voice trumpet This is an excerpt. For the full chapter, read Art World’s Dirty Little Secret.

title: Chapter 23: Fury and the Neglected Lover
content:
Chapter 23
Fury and the Neglected Lover Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
A.m. Aug 21— Fury and the Neglected Lover
The complaints regarding frivolous and untruthful expert opinions are all too justified. They have caused a reaction, so that timorous minds nowadays go to the extremes in judging negatively or with reserve. The people concerned say ‘no’ in order not, at all events, to be considered with the ‘yes-men.’ Now prudence is not only the mother of wisdom but also the daughter of ignorance. What must be done to steer the right course between the rocks of conciliator compliance on the one hand and a negative attitude, on principle, on the other?
Max J. Friedlander, On Art and Connoisseurship, 1942 According to the Butlins, when the Greeks sent a delegation to England to make inquiries about the Elgin Marbles, they were stonewalled, stewed in bureaucracy, and ultimately “they went away.” As Bob was to find out later, this ploy had been used on Paul Biró. Frances assured, “He just goes away, goes away.” Whether it was simple procrastination, a highbrow desire to sweep it under the rug or a quasi-conspiracy, one can only speculate—but Bob got the impression that some of their cast made a winning science of it.
The fallout had settled from the previous day’s critique, and most everyone had forgotten what was debated—everyone but Graham. While the rest were wine tasting, he stayed home with me. He fumbled through several tomes looking to refute Martin’s strongest stylistic argument against me: the argument dealing with my varying degrees of ‘finish’. For example, Martin claimed that my pier was more finished than the wrecked ship.
From his own B & J Catalogue, both visually and in the text, Graham found the exact opposite of Martin’s assertion. As the group stood in the living room, barely awake, Graham flipped from bookmark to bookmark to illustrate his discovery. A.J. Finberg left no doubt when he wrote that “Turner’s style differed in two opposite directions from the one then in vogue; it differed not only by deliberate vagueness or looseness of definition in appropriate places, but also, in other places, by an excessive vigour of realization.”
With feigned humility and mumbled concurrence Martin was able to brush off his previous verity as inconsequential by laughing, “So you’ve proven me wrong.” He then swiftly changed the topic by directing everyone to my backside (verso if you please). At Martin’s urging blithe connoisseurs had become prudent scientists in a flash.
Graham had won a critical stylistic argument, but disappointedly at this same juncture realized that he was up against something other than scholarship. He was starting to believe, as Bob had suspected for years, that there were other issues at stake in the art world beyond the truth. Was the control by experts predominantly grounded in the “looking, looking, looking, looking” principal as Martin insisted? It certainly left the door open for discretionary decision-making. Certainly, the necessary skills for making the best decision could be more faithfully gained by taking everything into account, including science and scholarship. Martin must have had himself convinced that little else had “weight”, that connoisseurship was the sole path to expertise. And what about his adherence to the more love-struck approach that required almost a romantic devotion to the subject painting before one could make a commitment? The ideal of Gowing, Martin’s admired predecessor at the Tate had implored, “When a letter from your girlfriend is dropped through the door, you don’t have to open it and read the name to know who it’s from.” Friedlander concisely assailed this theory when he wrote that “coldly analytical scholars make fewer mistakes.”
By exclusively using Gowing’s subjective approach for authenticating paintings (the “accidental impression” as Friedlander would call it); the door was left ajar for biased decision-making. Misattributions made on a subjective basis—although not always fraudulent—can result in skewed historical perspective—the history upon which an artist’s reputation including Turner’s depends. Naturally, the person positioned as the established expert on a particular artist would like to guard against challengers from beyond the reach of their own influence. What better way to do this than to restrict the basis for their authority to the mystical and difficult to challenge power of connoisseurship?
Friedlander wrote, “Since nobody can be called to account or produce proofs, since everything depends upon confidence and blind faith, it is authority which is demanded, claimed and striven for—at times even created artificially.” He added that, based on more strident activity in the marketplace, there is an increased demand for expertise, which contributes to the increased power of the expert, and to the danger of misusing this power.
Isolationist efforts with regard to ‘British Historic works of art’ apparently began in the 1960s with the creation of a keepership by the same name. At that time, Gowing was likely the main push behind taking control of this politically sensitive part of the Tate collection. Gowing had certainly been at the centre of ‘establishment’ politics—he had been Trustee of both the National Portrait Gallery and Tate Gallery for several years. In such an environment it would be desirable to pass the reins of authority along within one’s own circle of influence, thus keeping the decision-making authority in friendly hands.
As I mentioned the group had been drawn away from stylistic arguments to my verso, but as the physical evidence started to mount in support of Turner’s involvement, Martin shifted focus to his world of William Blake—almost as if Dr. Jekyll had become Mister Hyde everyone was treated to another of Martin’s charming anecdotes. Undeterred, Graham brought them back to Turner with a quote from Daphne Lawson. As previously mentioned, this professor from the (teaching with the ISC program at Herstmonceux) wrote a lengthy supplement to his Turner paper. Dating me based on the progression of Turner’s lozenge shaped composition, her conclusion was 1802-04. Martin immediately searched for an alternative argument, “I’m afraid it’s more than the lozenge, it’s the waves.” It appeared that my waves were Martin’s only other argument at the time, and somewhat frustrated, he sniped a quote from his master again, “You come into the room as Lawrence Gowing says, and recognize it as your lover or you don’t – and I’m afraid, I don’t!”
Graham sent Bob an interesting quote from his Anthropology of Art course subsequent to Martin’s visit. It destroyed the foundation for such affection:
The word 'connoisseurship' makes some people uncomfortable; it has gained connotations of mystery and elitism. The mystery aspect associated with connoisseurship is usually overdone. It is often relatively easy for a connoisseur to demonstrate the knowledge by producing different samples for the novice or student to compare and test. Connoisseurship is knowledge that is hard to describe, but the connoisseur who wants to pass on her or his knowledge can do so by pointing out differences in examples and providing a variety of samples. The charge of elitism that connoisseurship receives occurs when a craftsperson or connoisseur claims the right, by virtue of his or her experience, to be trusted to pronounce on matters that other people say are a matter of taste or opinion. Friedlander audaciously began his chapter on connoisseurship. “CHARLETANISM,” he wrote, “was the professional malady of experts and springs from the unstable nature of artistic judgment.”
The stylistic debate Graham and Bob had with Martin made them wonder if they were all talking about the same picture. When they suggested that an area of my waves was possibly added later at the time the picture was refurbished for , and at a time Turner’s brushwork was notoriously broad, Martin protested, “They are feeble little dabs!” And when Bob pleaded that some were sweeps extending nearly a foot long, he seemed about to boil so Bob changed the topic to James Orrock. The fire was not out though. When Bob asked if Orrock was a connoisseur, he blurted out, “He just didn’t know…he was a collector, and he was probably cheated by Agnew’s.” Immediately stopping himself, “Oh, shee!” as if drawing back from a precipice, he then gave a short half-hearted laugh.
As for Orrock being cheated: with the force of his artistic and collecting prowess, he likely advised Agnew’s, Christie’s, and that elite element of the British art trade, especially on works by Turner. He was certainly not in a position to be cheated. At the Christie’s “Highly Important” sale of his pictures in 1904 there were eighteen works by Turner alone. The Duveen family of art dealers and experts, at the time greatly respected Orrock’s connoisseurship, and as an old man his friendship and mentorship to James Henry Duveen seemed almost family-like.
The connection between Orrock and this charmed group was indubitably close, possibly too close. To appreciate this rather stealthy alliance, all one has to do is follow the trail of the record breaking Walton Bridges back and forth between Orrock and Agnew’s. This was in the same year that A.J. Finberg singled it out for high praise in his classic 1910 publication: “Mr. Orrock’s Walton Bridges…in all its essential beauty…”
This is an excerpt. For the full chapter, read Art World’s Dirty Little Secret.

title: Chapter 24: Jockeying or Joking
content:
Chapter 24
Jockeying or Joking Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
Jockeying or Joking?
Before long, to Martin’s aggravation, Graham introduced the fingerprint issue again, and the battle resumed with the defenseless Paul Biró on the other side of the country.
Graham: “Why would somebody put a false fingerprint…that’s fraudulent!”
Martin in agreement, “That much is fraudulent.”
Bob became somewhat animated: “That’s indictable; that’s criminal… people go to jail with fingerprint evidence – for life! Executed if they find a fingerprint at the scene where somebody was murdered. There’s no question about it!”
Martin: “Now they want DNA evidence.”
Bob: “No they don’t, because it’s less effective. The fingerprint is the foremost forensic evidence.”
Perhaps to calm Bob, Martin submitted, “Well, alright.”
Slightly defused, Bob continued, “There is a Turner fingerprint there, it’s been corroborated by a top Canadian official in the RCMP.”
Martin attempted to critique the fingerprint comparison. “Well look, if you compare the two, well, here there’s a great pit…”
Graham: “They are the experts.”
Bob: “I don’t even want to look at it, it was Turcotte. He’s studied fingerprints for thirty years, and he said, yes [it is right]!”
Martin: “It was Turcotte who did it for Biró, right – Turcotte who was employed by Biró, right?”
Graham: “He wasn’t employed.”
Martin with a chuckle that inferred privileged knowledge of what was going on behind the scenes, “Well he works with him.”
Bob: “Does he?”
Martin: “Yup!”
Attempting to uncover Martin’s meaning, Bob cooperated, “So, maybe it’s Turcotte?”
Martin was starting to enjoy this fanciful game and snickered, “Yeah, well, I read too many thrillers, I read too many thrillers of corrupt policemen and all the rest of it.”
It was not only the FBI and RCMP that Martin did not trust. He confirmed that even if Scotland Yard supported the fingerprint, he would still not believe it.
This is an excerpt. For the full chapter, read Art World’s Dirty Little Secret.

title: Chapter 25: The Only Painting Ever
content:
Chapter 25
The Only Painting Ever Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
A.m. Aug 21—The Only Painting Ever!
“J. C. Robinson, a brilliant connoisseur, whose purchases on behalf of the laid the foundations of its richness…” Sir Oliver Millar
Graham and Bob were fortunate to hear firsthand Martin’s hierarchal listing for his colleagues in the world of Turner expertise. His ranking was a response to their enquiry, and it seemed to work on down from himself, through the ranks at the Tate, culminating with those supplementary types on the outside—the more scholarly or sociological ones. Although those on the outside had some good points according to Martin, it was clear that their opinions were considered irrelevant when it came to authenticating artworks by JMW.
The main weakness of those on the fringe was apparent—they had not worked with the pictures at the Tate Gallery. This, regardless of the point made earlier that the sampling at the Tate was not fully representative of the artist’s work. Martin grudgingly agreed with this but insisted the Tate was a critical element for learning the ropes nonetheless. For obvious reasons, the isolated exception was Evelyn Joll from Agnew’s, co-author of the B & J Catalogue; he topped the list along with Martin but had recently passed away. There was no other name mentioned outside of the museum community. According to Martin, Evelyn Joll had been the diligent scholar for the catalogue and this along with his access to the all-important Agnew files made him indispensable at the time.
It is best to hear it in Martin’s own words. As a commander might do, he set his jaw firmly and started to recite: “There is myself, there is Andrew Wilton, there is David Blayney Brown, there is Ian Warrell, and all three of those work at the Tate.”
Graham brought up David Hill, and after the laughter over his connection to Biró had subsided, Martin admitted, “Although Hill is very good, he is very unmethodical.” Afterwards granting that, “He obviously has a pretty good idea what a Turner looks like.” Later, Martin was slightly less generous, “David Hill—I wouldn’t rule out entirely.” Of course this was at a point when Martin was being worried again by Graham over Biró. He had mentioned the news article involving Landscape with Rainbow, and how David Hill strongly factored the fingerprint evidence into his assessment of the painting. Martin argued that he himself would have approached it the other way round, saying, “It looks absolutely right, and by Jove, look, there’s a fingerprint!”
Martin had been very skeptical of the painting initially, but admitted to Graham, “[Although] I wouldn’t go to the stake for that one, I think it’s probably right.” Was it because Martin was unwilling to go to the stake that the painting did not sell at either Christie’s or Sotheby’s? As a result of the alternative sales venue, might we also suspect that this was the reason it sold cheaply compared to Kenneth Clark’s Folkestone, for example? It brought less than two-hundred thousand dollars versus the relative sum of twenty-eight million dollars for the painting (by today’s valuation)—less than one percent the amount. Is Martin that powerful?
Graham also asked about John Gage. Martin said, “He’s very good on the theory, imagination and things like that.” He then succumbed to telling another of his dramatized anecdotes. “Gage came back from somewhere waving a picture. He said it was Turner’s off cliffs, and everybody said, ‘Oh god!’ in strident disbelief. Turner did illustrate topographical work…that John Gage is fantastic at. Again, I think he slightly overdoes it. But he’s not the old-fashioned connoisseur. That’s now out of fashion, connoisseurship is rated as—down there. In some ways its fine until of course you have a problem like this [Shipwreck, the Rescue].” Martin felt empowered to use his latent skills as a connoisseur to their fullest for the present case. But was Martin saying that because John Gage was not an old-fashioned connoisseur, he would not be able to give a worthy opinion on me?
Martin: “He wouldn’t be the person I’d go to, to say that this painting is by any particular artist, but I would read John Gage because he writes much more interesting stuff about Turner.”
Marcia Pointon’s name came up several times and as Martin ‘rated’ her at this point he automatically retold the folkloric tale about the Peasant Girl and the Gold Tipped Hoe. Martin called it a classic. The trouble with Marcia Pointon, according to Martin was that, “She’s not a connoisseur, so she could build this great socialist interpretation of a picture.” added, “And not look at the picture.” Martin agreed, “And not look at the picture, and probably even get the artist wrong.”
The Peasant Girl and the Gold Tipped Hoe: this particular anecdotal gem seemed to defend ostensibly what might be considered Martin’s own bulwark, the status quo; that which condemned the wildly over-interpretive craze based on sociology. On this point he counseled Marcia Pointon during one of her exhibitions; it was over the same ‘gold tipped hoe’. As a benevolent mentor might be expected, he tolerantly wove this particular allegory for all present with patient articulation:
There was a little village street with a girl in roughish clothes leaning against a wall, with an older man talking to her. And, this was the rich old man trying to seduce the nice pure young peasant girl. And my question was, “Why do you think he’s a rich old man?” The reasoning was that he had a suit. Martin then instructed Ms. Pointon: “But then you know, in those days in and up until recently it didn’t matter how poor you were, you sat around all day in a dark suit in a rush cane chair.” “Because he’s holding a gold tipped cane,” came the return volley! So I looked at her and said, “You know—sorry—you want to go and do a little work in the garden. That’s a garden hoe!” This whole thing was built up on this wrong fact. It was in fact the light shining on the wooden handle; it was unmistakably a hoe that’s used to root out roots and things you know. I’m not a gardener in agriculture or anything, but I know what a garden hoe is! (Laughter ensued.)
What we are left with are two disparate points of view stemming from two disparate dogmatisms. As Martin suggested, perhaps Ms. Pointin had wrongly approached her interpretation from a feminist perspective, looking for faults with the old man by inferring a masterly dominance over the symbol of vulnerable womanhood—the young maiden. Martin’s antithetical argument may have been setting the stage for his own interpretive tale. In the end, neither one seemed interested in the picture artistically. The romantic JMW would have rejected both ideas.
Momentarily, after Martin’s critique and bellicose laughter, things became unusually silent. Bob apparently thought he could catch him off-guard, so he slid in a question about a watercolour Martin had already seen in the dining room. It was titled in pencil on the verso, Vale of Avoca… The vintage Agnew frame, label and original inscription on the frame all pointed to JMW. There was also a provenance letter from the dealer, Lister and Son, which took it back to the not-so-inconsequential Rev. William Kingsley. The Reverend was a friend of JMW’s
Vale of Avoca detail, goats… greatly enlarged Bob asked: “Do you think that’s a Turner, the watercolour with the goats?” The reason he focused on the goats, which were a minor part of the picture was that Martin had thought them outstanding the previous day. As described in Turner’s Liber Studiorum by W.G. Rawlinson, Turner’s deer and goats are rarely at fault, probably because they are generally associated rather with the wilder scenes of nature than with the surroundings of man.
Martin, after a long pause gave a curt reply “I’m not sure.” While clenching his teeth he gave a weak laugh and made a low growling noise. Trying to choose his words carefully: “I wouldn’t say: not my period, but I’d go to a watercolour specialist for that.” This was an odd thing for a person who had written a book titled Turner Watercolours back in 1962. The inside flap of the book states that “the practicing painter will be particularly interested in Butlin’s notes on the artist’s technique.”
For the Tate, Martin had also authored, Watercolours from the Turner Bequest in 1968. It was evident that for whatever reasons, Martin just did not have, in his “heart of hearts,” the confidence to make a decision and possibly “go to the stake for it.”
Martin confessed how stingy he had been in the past with Turner attributions. This dated back for more than thirty years of his tenure as a prominent Tate Gallery official. He appeared to express satisfaction that he only ever accepted one solitary painting. And when he reminisced about that particular picture, it was as though he was highlighting his prowess as a sleuth. His story started at Agnew’s in 1967 and dealt with a painting Martin made contentious in the first place—Neapolitan Fisher-Girls Surprised Bathing by Moonlight:
I remember going to the Agnew exhibition and the picture looked wrong. At that moment I didn’t know it had a provenance that goes right back to . So I said to Evelyn Joll: ‘You know Evelyn, this looks wrong.’ And, Evelyn said: ‘Well look.’ He shows me the provenance – it goes right back to Robert Vernon. And I said, ‘Well I’m sorry, it may have a verdict or a thing that goes right back to Robert Vernon. But to me it looks wrong, and I say, just to me.’ This is an excerpt. For the full chapter, read Art World’s Dirty Little Secret.

title: Chapter 26: Looking Blindly
content:
Chapter 26
Looking Blindly Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
Am Aug 21—Looking Blindly
It was already into the third hour of serious discussion that morning. Bob fell into conversation with , partly for her ability to carry on a two-way conversation, and possibly as an escape. Not so for Graham, his young blood was still well-charged as he tempted Martin upstairs and back to Ehrenbrietstein. He had found some comparable foliage in an “accepted” JMW painting to test Martin’s main argument against the oil sketch—the argument about the way Turner painted trees. On the way up the stairs the cadence of pounding footsteps was reinforced by Martin’s chant: “looked, and looked, and looked”; one “looked” for each ascending step. This was to remind Graham, without doubt of the importance of looking, looking, looking—the key to an expert’s magical powers.
After they reached the top of the stairs Graham got what he asked for—Martin’s connoisseurship. Comparing the trees in a small illustration from the B & J Catalogue, his low monotone whisper was hauntingly like that of a mystic. Was this a chestnut typically shared under such circumstances: not quite trite, not quite cliché? De-mystified! His invocation was carefully measured at first but ended with a bout of jubilant and whimsical laughter:
These [leaves] are more softly done and there is sort of unity about the whole of what grows off that branch. Which in a way, also, in fact, there’s much more variation here, in fact there’s two different-coloured trees. There’s a green one and a brown one, but here you go through the subtlety of… this is a green one, but you’re still seeing a bit of the orangey one through it, but there, there is more foliage and the modulation in the depth of green which is the green branches are in front of the green branches, front of the orange so they begin—there, as it were—the leaves are thicker. He wouldn’t have painted that from the trees, but his knowledge of having looked at trees when he went fishing.” Martin, then, with a burst of excitement and a lot more coherence shouted: ‘As I said he went fishing, and when you’re fishing you have an awful lot of time to look at trees. Ha, Ha, Ha!’
Was this simply an outburst of innocent joy or the cackle of a cunning oracle betraying a spoof? Martin was always full of good-natured fun, and after this entertaining ruse one could only feel charmed. He settled back temporarily to the task at hand, but soon Graham started asking short incisive questions. At first these were deflected indifferently, but as Graham persisted, Martin became slightly frustrated and started to make a more clear and hard appraisal. Frustration soon led to anger and eventually an outright savage condemnation of the poor little painting. Paradoxically, this attack led him to deceive his own “Hand” theory.
As Martin glared at the painted surface of the tiny “orphan,” he chastised: “The actual way the paint’s put on isn’t right and I’ve seen other things like this.”
Graham: What about the impasto?
Martin: “This blobby look, which doesn’t really—,” pointing to the centre of the painting he exclaimed, “What the hell’s that meant to be!” Pointing at another area, “What’s that meant to be?”
Orrock Ehrenbrietstein, detail of village Graham: It’s very sketchy.
It should be remembered that Martin had initially dubbed this little Orrock painting Hand C. Quoting from his Oxford Companion, page 100: “Hand C’ is distinguished by the small size of the paintings, in oils, and by an over delicate prettification of Turnerian style and motifs.” This description is far from being hellishly incoherent and “blobby.”
Martin continued his assault: “Even in the most sketchy Turner you can tell…” He abruptly stopped himself as he started to recognize some of the content, “Oh! That is a sail of a ship; I don’t know what these are.”
Graham answered enthusiastically, “Actually, those are houses.”
Martin: “So they are, yeah.”
At this point Graham might have called forth Turner himself, or at least Ruskin, who had borne witness to a similar muddling situation. Ruskin had explained on behalf of Turner, “In a proof impression of a plate lately submitted to me, the engraver had failed to discern the distant representation of a village at the base of a hill, and had substituted some unintelligible nothings. Turner had run a heavy pencil line into the margin of the paper to intimate that these were ‘houses;’ and the miniature village seemed to come into focus as if by magic.” Yes, at Graham’s prompting the miniature village at the base of the hill came into focus for Martin, as if by magic (see image on back cover).
Graham Reynolds expressed well the power and immediacy of Turner’s brushwork at the apex of his career when he wrote that “his graphic shorthand enabled him to simplify what he saw into a few swift mnemonic strokes.” As one JMW painting to another, Martin’s hellish blobs might better be described as swift mnemonic strokes. One has to stand up for his own!
After a couple of more-pointed questions from Graham, Martin decided to shut down any more challenges by unabashedly calling the little picture a late nineteenth-century fake! Martin added condescendingly, “It’s interesting but it’s not worth a lot of money I’m afraid,” then snickered.
Undeterred, Graham made an enquiry about the support the picture was painted on. It was a book board that had a marbled design on the back very much like the boards seen on other Turner sketchbooks. Martin commented that Turner was willing to use ‘found’ supports but he would not concede or perhaps did not understand that it would be unlikely that a faker would have used such a scrap. Graham asked, “How would a nineteenth century faker even know Turner used book boards?” Martin replied, “He may not have even thought that anybody would be bothered to look at the back of the picture.”
It was a standard for the economy-wise Turner to use every available scrap, including the book boards of his sketchbooks. While reinterring the neglected remains of several of the late artist’s drawings back in their archive, Ruskin made a special note that Turner had used even book boards for his sketches.
Graham continued pressing for solid stylistic reasoning: “Is the way he used the palette knife in the sky like Turner?”
This is an excerpt. For the full chapter, read Art World’s Dirty Little Secret.

title: Chapter 27: Loose Lips On Board
content:
Chapter 27
Loose Lips On Board Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
Eve Aug 21—Loose Lips On Board
The winery tour planned for late in the afternoon had gone well, and it proved somewhat revitalizing after the tenuous discussions that day. The Butlins were deposited back at their hotel for a rest. Graham and Bob were starting to show signs of overload though, Bob especially. Chris, who had wisely avoided the battleground in the living room earlier, was showing some exhilaration about the upcoming dinner cruise. Their trusty watercraft, which had plied for a decade, was the Casabella Princess. When everyone arrived at the small paddle wheeler, the Butlins admired its quaint charm. The only disappointment was that the evening was blustery and wet. Likely because of the weather, Ken Davis, our captain, had only one other couple on board.
The choppy lake was an ideal setting to discuss a sublime and stormy piece like myself. Regrettably, by now it was unlikely there’d be any budging those stoic Brits over my attribution. According to Bob’s journal the only meaningful discussion that took place that evening was shortly after dinner. Up until then, the regal demeanor of and the comic dramatizations of Martin entertained regardless of substance. Graham jested earlier about some obscure literary tale. How wine is an effective truth serum. Soon Martin had medicated himself with the heady stuff beyond safety. He talked relentlessly and the things he shared would have made many in his circle back home cringe. The fun character and humorous content of his banter was the code needed to unravel certain furtive activities being carried on by the auction trade, galleries, and museums. Martin had over the years dealt with all of them on the highest level. The churning, slapping, and grumbling of the vessel muffled many jewels of privileged insider knowledge from the recorder, but those that made it through were set in gold.
Apparently, while Keeper at the Tate, Martin would conspire with certain dealers (who he implicated by name). They were always doing deals. If a pair of pictures came up at auction, for example, they would fix it with the two pictures being split between the two parties afterwards.
Bob urged, “Although Christie’s had to sell at the highest price?”
“Yes, to a certain extent,” confirmed Martin. “They would make conciliatory deals and let us see the pictures much longer in advance.”
Martin again implicated, “X,” who apparently said at one point, “You shouldn’t be buying, Martin. We have to get the price down.” As it seems, competition met with a certain amount of disdain within this privileged core of the art trade. Albeit on a grander and more sophisticated scale, might a corollary be mapped out as follows?
The attorneys general of three states sued a group of seven stamp dealers for allegedly conspiring to rig auctions in several states, and . ‘These defendants’ collusive practices adversely affected sellers and undermined the marketplace.’ In 1996 and 1997, the ring allegedly fixed bids at most of the large stamp auction houses in the , including Sotheby’s, Christie’s… Over $150 million worth of stamps were sold at public auctions that year.
On behalf of the Tate, Martin had another interesting slant on acquisitions. “We’d go to the dealer who is most likely to want to buy the picture you wanted—for stock. Some dealers buy just stock and then resell. Then we’d get them to buy it for us, because that sort of knocked out the competition…we always got special terms, sometimes we would get it for no commission at all because they wanted prestige.”
This overriding need for prestige was highlighted when Martin exclaimed, “Of course” to the enquiry whether those involved in the art scene in support the public collections toward getting a knighthood. Martin discussed clear examples along this vein where the competition between dealers and colleagues was relatively fierce and wrought with jealousy. One allied dealer bidding on behalf of the Tate bid above the set limit. This was necessary to win the piece and thus be the gallery able to provide it for the Tate. As a drawback to this somewhat vain, somewhat business-wise exercise, the dealer purportedly absorbed the difference. One dealer apparently was reticent to compromise on his prices, even for the Tate, and would use an anonymous benefactor foil to disguise lowering his otherwise inflexible prices.
Indulging in the prestige-for-barter concept, whereby status is available for a price, Sir Joseph Joel Duveen “was offered a baronetcy in reward for his gift of the Turner Wing to the Tate.” In the tiny 1913 Tate Gallery publication, the National Gallery people wrote that “because of the munificent gift of the late Sir J. Duveen, the Turner Collection would now be adequately housed and exhibited.”
Support for public collections and the significance of such matters as they relate to “caste” was made evident in the listing of British supporters to the exhibition in 1966: all had auspicious titles. This was a propitious group of supporters, without doubt, and the contagious nature of this ideal was clearly being mimicked abroad in . As Martin had previously mentioned this was the exhibition that absolutely established Turner in “smart” circles.
It could be said that the far reaching fingers of the British Aristocracy dug deeply when it came to Turner, and that the exhibition was possibly staged not only to establish Turner in smart circles and to a degree educate the general public, but also to suppress the unruly nature of the vast accumulation of Turners outside England. Could it also have been organized to thwart undesirable competition? “Something in the spirit of the age…rivalries of nations,” Wheeler wrote this in the forward of the same exhibition catalogue, slightly out of context here, but nonetheless, prophetic.
Many Brits invariably feel anxiety over having so many of their historic treasures sold abroad the previous century, including many Turners. This was at a time when it was both legal and presumably ethical for great pictures to be shipped from without challenge. Seeing pictures such as Gainsborough’s, The Blue Boy; or Turner’s, The Fifth Plague of Egypt at home in a foreign land must tempt disdain, and for some, possibly even a counterproductive need to avenge the loss. For the resentful few, casting aspersions against the integrity of American collectors and their collections, as well as challenging authenticities would be a sound way to accomplish such a task.
Robert Cecil, a former British diplomat, admitted that there was a “latent anti-Americanism that infected so many of his generation.” For Anthony Blunt at the Courtauld:
The pollution of the language by Hollywood movies, the plundering of great works of art by transatlantic plutocrats, or any number of similar issues, the Americans had by the thirties become identified in the minds of many British intellectuals with an evil materialism that threatened their inheritance. The anti-Americanism that Blunt shared with many of his academic contemporaries was more than the traditional British snobbism toward the colonials…he was vehemently outspoken about the need to halt the flow of artworks to the United States…Blunt wrote The Times in 1971 to condemn the selling of artworks from major British collections as a ‘sign of cultural barbarism
It appears this warning came far too late. The hoard was permanently ensconced in American collections. And what about Martin Butlin, the graduate from the Courtauld during Blunt’s directorship, might it be true that he inherited a bit of the same animosity in accordance with Blunt’s socialist edict?
The 2004 upheaval at the caused by the predominantly British Beaverbrook Foundations, focused attention on similar sentiments. Although in this instance the kafuffle seemed propagated as much by animosity toward a perceived ‘colonial’ backwardness. The CBC reported that: “All but three members of the gallery’s board recently resigned in protest when the two foundations came forward and said they were considering removing several significant works from the gallery… [Sotheby’s] declared two of them too rare and valuable to remain in the gallery.”
At issue was whether the Beaverbrook Foundations gave or loaned the pictures to the Beaverbrook Gallery. Foundation vice-chairman Vincent Prager said, “However much one likes , is not the centre of the world and these are world-class paintings. It was felt by the foundation, as far as I understand it, that they should be taken back…, in exchange for a very large sum of money.”
Bob wrote (to several newspapers):
This is an excerpt. For the full chapter, read Art World’s Dirty Little Secret.
