title: Chapter 28: Backroom Whispers
content:
Chapter 28
Backroom Whispers Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
Backroom Whispers
The boat rocked and flapped its way towards shore. Bob was still silenced by what Martin had just said: “We’re all in each other’s pockets – it’s like incest in a way.” In his tome Peter Watson expounded on this point when he wrote that “Christie’s was run by a close-knit coterie of like-minded men – ‘in-bred’ in John Herbert’s apt term – who tend to assume that the firm would always have a monopoly on Old Masters and great furniture by virtue of its pedigree and its close links with the best families.”
Martin, now a Christie’s representative, esoterically whispered what amounted to clandestine implications of such control, “If something comes up, like a Blake, we try and steer it towards the Tate.” According to Martin, Christie’s would ask him for a valuation on a Blake or a Turner for example, and he would give them a figure.
“How much do you think it’s worth?” they would ask.
Martin: “I’d say a figure and they hated it.”
Dispirited (according to Martin) they would then concur, “Oh, all right then.”
Martin gave a whimsical example of one of these esoteric evaluations. “William Blake, two and a half thousand pounds,” he might offer as an appraised value.
With the reply from Christie’s being: “We were thinking of thirty thousand pounds.”
Martin’s retort: “Oh, pull the other one!”
Bob: Did you get it for your price?
Martin: Well, this was my recommendation to Christie’s.
Martin was not going to be unreservedly persuaded to admit that he was still buying for the Tate, and thus recanted his confession by waffling, stating that he tells Christie’s to “go away” when they ask for valuations. He tells them to go to their computers and look up the last ten years of sale records.
That same morning Bob had already been surprised. He learned that the declared rivals, Christie’s and Sotheby’s had a shared interest—Martin Butlin! Notwithstanding his employment as a senior expert for Christie’s, he revealed that if Sotheby’s got a Blake or Turner they would come to him.
Bob asked, “Because of your preeminence they wouldn’t go to anyone else but you?”
Officiously he replied, “Well, Sotheby’s have to, because I work for Christie’s…” Then he immediately added, “But in fact, Sotheby’s do come to me… That’s another story.”
Half an hour later Bob tried for further clarification, “With the sale of a painting like Shipwreck, the Rescue… the only place to sell it is through Christie’s…”
Martin gently interrupted, “Or Sotheby’s.”
Bob: “Yeah, but to get the proper money for it…”
Martin again interrupted, “Well, Christie’s or Sotheby’s.”
He then gave a humorous anecdote comparing Sotheby’s and Christie’s, “There’s a saying about Christie’s and Sotheby’s: sell your pictures at Sotheby’s. Buy your pictures at Christie’s… which is supposed to reflect on the honesty of the two firms.”
This is an excerpt. For the full chapter, read Art World’s Dirty Little Secret.

title: Chapter 29: The Astonishing Monogram
content:
Chapter 29
The Astonishing Monogram Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
A.m. Aug 22—The Astonishing Monogram
Bob picked up the Butlins from their hotel for the last time. The plan was to stop home momentarily before striking out for . When offered a coffee for the road, Martin spontaneously erupted with another Tate Gallery musing. Martin’s life was inextricably tied to his mother institution; one could not even offer coffee without being thrust back to the Tate.
“When the Tate was small, we all got together for a cup of coffee.” Although this appeared to elicit pleasant memories, Martin expressed his frustrations over how the staff would leave a project for coffee just as he felt they were getting something accomplished. He pondered over how the institution had grown so big; he was not certain who was who any more, or where they all belonged, “That’s the trouble everything gets bigger and bigger.” Perhaps Martin was too close to the matter to see or understand that the normal lifecycle of a bureaucracy is invariably tied up with “empire building.” If the funds and political will are present, growth is unbounded. The Tate being a focus for fervent National pride, it is unlikely that Martin will ever see a reversal of this trend.
As the last few things were being packed Martin was blindsided. “Sorry, to bring some business into the morning,” Graham interjected, “but can we go around one more time and write down a few points?” He led his compliant master back into the fray slowly. The result was splendid. The first whispers between Martin and Graham about the type of paper used for the Anderson Turner watercolour, ended with a rigorous battle over the inscription on my pier.
Things leaped off topic several times. Everyone was just about out the door and on their way, when Graham charged forth one last time. It was the inscription, J W T—it had not been discussed yet! Bob and Graham assumed that Martin had taken it into account already. The information about it was sent to Christie’s on compact disc in duplicate. One might have expected a short sensible discussion over the inscription, but what ensued was absolute pandemonium. Bob became disoriented when Martin said, “One would like to think it was put on later.” In effect this would make it fake. Apparently he was looking to Bob for support.
J W T His intermittent efforts over the next twenty minutes trying to gain an ally in this regard went from gentle hope to near blasphemy, and ended in a pithy plea for compliance. He wanted the initials forsaken for the sake of expedience. “Well, what one would like to think…” he instructed, “is that the letters were put on later.” Within minutes he went further. “What I’m trying to make clear; it would be a damn sight better from everybody’s point of view, if it didn’t have any signa… initials on it at all!” Finally, with his next to last breath, “As I say, it would make life much easier if they weren’t there.” This surreal episode ended with a whimper.
Frances commandeered Bob’s black light to prove whether or not the antique varnish was truly over top of the letters. They both agreed it was. She then pointed a spotlight at the little squares as Martin exclaimed, “Now I do in fact see the ‘T’ much better.” As his eyes wandered off the mark to the top of the pier, and away from the sequence of three paint blotches wherein the letters were found, he scoffed, “Look, I’ve found a little face there!” Was this meant to infer that imagination can play games with perception? Likely, but for Martin, only selectively!
When all four belligerents eventually made their way into the study to view the enlarged computer images of the paint blotches (as Francis had entreated they do) Martin admitted hesitantly, “That definitely is a W, I do accept that.”
The discussions degenerated as contended that the inscription had been scratched into the surface rather than inscribed into wet paint. Martin echoed, “That’s scratched.” In the end, Martin and Frances were stumped as to why the letters were there in the first place and eventually made all efforts to deny them completely. Soon they were calling them accidental markings. Yet Martin kept trying to answer his own question, ‘Why J W T and not J M W T’. §Just to remind everyone… In 1802, just after I was painted and at the time JMW was made an RA, he simultaneously made the addition to his name. He add the M for milord (AKA Mallord). It was partly a spoof and partly in deference to his mother’s family name. §
It was all but certain that the material Bob sent Martin about the inscription had been lost or neglected, and up until his visit, was unseen. He admitted, “The trouble is, I didn’t… probably, really look at them.” Without adequate lead time it was nearly impossible for him to formulate a successful off-hand confutation. In the process of attempting to do so, both he and Frances attacked the evidence and its purveyor—Bob—with surly shouts, akin to the way a master might control an unruly dog.
It was frightening how they contradicted and rebutted their own logic. First seeing, and then not seeing the inscription—agreeing that it was under the antique varnish; then swearing it was scratched deliberately into my surface, and finally declaring that the marks were merely accidental. The most disarming bit of faulty logic was the insistence that the initials were “done in two different ways,” scratched and inscribed. Things became more and more confusing.
With confidence fortified by her vocation as a restorer of pictures, was energetically willing to help Martin grapple his opponents. Before the team succumbed to crying, “accidental, accidental!” had used the word ‘scratch’ so many times that Christine was worried that their house was buggy. What she was inferring of course, was that the inscription was done into the dry paint, and thus should be considered a forged signature. The aging Martin had gnashed his teeth and gasped so many times that Bob was beginning to fear for his health.
The perceived differences between the highly magnified images of the J, and the W T, were caused by the slightly differing angle of light while being photographed, and the way in which it refracted through the antique varnish. It glistened more brightly within the hollows of the inscription (and other depressions in my paint surface also). The computer only enhanced the illusion. The underlying initials were all inscribed into the wet paint, all at the same time, and at the time the pier was painted. JMW took this narrow window of time before the paint dried, and into this all too convenient spot scribed his JWT. Whether the blotches themselves had been placed intentionally for the purpose, or whether he was inspired by their unintended, but tempting regularity, one will never know.
Why were Martin and Francis so tenacious? It seemed like a personal issue for them and an extremely critical issue at that, one that needed a commiserate battle as one might be obliged to endure for the sake of survival. Could it have been that this evidence was too powerful and too unyielding for Martin to plow under? Was he concerned about how he would deal with this point in his follow-up report for Christie’s?
Graham and Bob had been studying the inscription and its implications for a year, ever since Paul Biró found it while searching for fingerprints. They had analyzed it obsessively, and believed it was unshakable proof of a Turner connection. But the senseless shaking by Martin and Frances persisted until everyone gave up in frustration. Martin was already mumbling to Graham about how he would be able to dismiss the inscription in his report. His would-be acolyte stood entranced.
Would it not be expected, even out of curiosity, to try and reason why the initials are there in the first place? It was as though Martin and Frances had a vested interest in my status. There are undoubtedly issues that directly affect Martin, but the obvious ones should not have commanded such compulsive behavior.
They were not out the door yet! For the most part, Martin and Frances found their stay in , laid back—except for the cross-examinations. The morning of August 22 was one of those exceptions. It was necessary to give Martin one last trial before he escaped . Out came Bob’s book by John Anderson Jr., The Unknown Turner, the same publication and author that Martin was charged with reporting on for the Oxford Companion to J.M.W. Turner.
This is an excerpt. For the full chapter, read Art World’s Dirty Little Secret.

title: Chapter 30: Sticky Wicket
content:
Chapter 30
Sticky Wicket Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
Evening Aug 22—Sticky Wicket
From Bob’s Journal…Our journey to was solemn and soggy. I had promised the Butlins nice weather but our trip to the island via was anything but cheery. They loved the mountain vistas, at least the ones they could see through the cloud cover. The Romantic grandeur demanded a silence that was refreshing for all of us. The blustery two-hour ferry crossing to was made bearable by the vaporous beauty of the mist that cloaked the little islands along the way. Except for the week of the Butlin visit, it was otherwise a year of drought in BC.
Once in , we settled into our little hotel by the inner harbour. It was central to most of the tourist amenities. That evening we went for dinner at an upbeat place where our transformation from sophisticated diner to party-animal was a matter of ten steps.
Graham kept questioning Martin about his relationship with Christie’s and Sotheby’s and it came up again that Sotheby’s uses his expertise. He clarified his involvement more critically when he confirmed that he had an effective monopoly. This disclosure came when he demurely challenged us to take Shipwreck, the Rescue to Sotheby’s for a second opinion. “There’s nothing to stop you from going to Sotheby’s,” he said, “but my guess is that the moment they got the photograph, they would almost certainly ring me up—unofficially. There again, I wouldn’t be paid and they couldn’t use my name. In a way it sounds awful, one’s got a monopoly.”
The auction-room topic expanded and Martin revealed that it was often difficult tracking pictures down in order to catalogue them, and that existing records at the saleroom made it easier. Not only was he beholden to Christie’s archives for the history of many B & J Catalogue entries, but he also recanted previous mockery in the same breath, making it clear that Christie’s was not always “grasping at straws” in order to establish provenance.
Discussions led to my least favorite topic and a topic that even Graham got animated over—art being used to promote issues through narrow sociological interpretations. Martin ended up offering to wring the neck of one of Graham’s professors who had told her students during the first class of the year: “If you expect to look at art as something aesthetic, you should get up and leave the class right now.” We were abruptly freed from this unpleasant conversation; a table had just come available in the pub. We were off to join the inharmonious racket, perhaps a din of infidels. All we could do was assimilate.
Some of Martin’s biographical beginnings made it through the noise. His attendance at , was apparently granted through his father’s connection to the same college. On entering the interview room, there was this enormous cloud of smoke and a voice called from within, “Come in, come in.” It seems that the cigarette smoke was so heavy he could hardly make out who else was in the room. The first thing Martin remembered hearing was “post-war bulge.” This bulge was made up of the hordes wanting to attend the college. Martin believed it was mentioned to prepare him for rejection, but when he said that his father E. J. had the same problem when he applied years earlier, the interviewer appeared from out of the cloud. “Oh well, was your father at Trinity?” The barriers came down and shortly thereafter Martin was invested.
The way he was hired at the Tate was similar. His grandfather had known the Rothensteins as neighbours in the country. Sir William Rothenstein (1872-1945) was an eminent artist and director of the Tate Gallery from 1927-33. The grandfather had gone for tea at their home on numerous occasions. When Martin applied for the job he managed to mention the connection. Abracadabra, Martin had his first and in many respects, his final job. He worked there until retirement. With Sir John’s mandate, he and Martin co-authored a Turner book soon after his appointment. Thus, a new Turner expert was made. Pure serendipity, Martin’s attitude towards his work must have been as much a happy family relationship as it was a job.
Martin had read History at , a couple of years of art history at the Courtauld Institute, and much to his amazement he received his Master’s degree from his Alma Mater by virtue of having paid-up his “caution money.” Martin felt it was like a damage deposit. He believed that his honourary doctorate came as a result of his contributions to the Butlin and Joll, J.M.W. Turner Catalogue, albeit, he was also heavily involved as a cataloguer on William Blake and Samuel Palmer. Martin assured us that while he seemed to slip easily into his lot-in-life, it took more than just nepotism to accomplish one’s purpose and maintain a job once potted. He insisted there was still a chance for someone to “fall flat” if they were not fully prepared.
Martin also acknowledged that he owed part of his good fortune to timing. He came into the museum world at the leading edge of the great art history program-expansion. It was at a time before art history departments were well established, and competition for jobs was far less fervent. This could explain why Martin works more as an expert than a scholar. Things changed so quickly during his tenure; with the new emphasis on art scholarship, and advances in science and technology, he must have felt a bit awkward. There was also the reality that most young people graduating had much stronger academic credentials.
Was this why Martin and his predecessor, Gowing, felt that looking, looking, looking, and the “magic eye” were all-important? Must they, for the sake of sheer survival, have promoted the visceral approach when dealing with art expertise? They were certainly being trapped by progress, like the average seventy-five-year-old in today’s computer age. Some keep abreast—most do not! This seemed like a credible theory considering Martin’s evident animosity towards both academia and scientific involvement in authenticating works of art. Berenson’s portrayal in his dying year is perhaps poignant here, “We should live more with things as they feel and look, and not insist on what is their ‘scientific’ make-up. Blessed are the simple for they make the best of things.” Martin was certainly making the best of things.
It is not meant here to disregard the importance of having the “eye” or to disparage connoisseurship and the advantage derived from looking at many quality pictures. If an eye can be trained, that is the way to do it. In his prickly little book, The Charm of Turner, James Mason explains that “the human eye has remarkable limitations, and only those who have trained their sight by dint of hard and painful labour can realize how little we truly see.”
But students must be careful that there is not a false prophet in their midst. It is one thing to have “an eye” and another to only profess such a gift. Most curators can get away in their jobs with the ability to see the aesthetic in what has already been accepted as artistic, but it takes a connoisseur of true merit to see the artistic in what has not yet been discovered, or at least what is yet to be accepted as an important example of art. If it were not for independent connoisseurship, Turner might not have been such a successful landscape painter. “Turner’s development entered a new phase following his acquaintance in 1793 of a medical psychiatrist and art connoisseur, Dr. Monro, who gathered several artists around himself to do copies of drawings he possessed.” Considering the choice of the artists in his care, there is little doubt that Monro was the rare person who could choose successfully from unproven talent.
A good example of a connoisseur with a good eye is found in the person of a successful art and antique dealer or collector, who must continually enter into an overwhelming amount of miscellaneous barter, and out of the confusion, find the gem. Such success is dependent almost entirely on a good eye. A collector such as Orrock was one such person. He was willing to enter the fray, buying and selling things from galleries, auction rooms, and privately—risking his own money, but in the process successfully building a venerable reputation. Almost a century ago, a noteworthy furniture manufacturer, Mayhew of , developed a line of Thomas Chippendale revival furniture named after most respected personalities and connoisseurs. In their 1910 advertisement, “The James Orrock” chair was at the top of the list.
If the rarity of an item determines its desirability for a museum, and if these important things are often one-of-a-kind, then it should be agreed that a curator experienced exclusively with things that are fully recognized, would have difficulties. Might this also entail the search to rediscover and preserve forgotten treasures? Without a somewhat innate sense for recognizing “quality” first and foremost, it is unlikely that he or she could even aspire, let alone ascend to the highest levels of connoisseurship.
Consider the relative abilities of those who collect for a living, versus those who collect institutionally. It probably depends as much on the force of curiosity as it does anything else. Every fine object is significant for one reason or another. To determine how important it is amidst the jumble-at-large… that’s the rub. The collector tends to migrate towards the familiar, that which he or she recognizes and understands as having a value or importance. The difference between recognizing what is good and what is exceptional is surely a synthesis of at least three factors: visual, intellectual, and invention. Experience, which is also important, comes from looking, looking, and looking.
Yes, even imagination can help to place the importance of an item into context. It is unfortunate that the spontaneity and vigor often felt by a connoisseur just before making the ultimate decision—whether to purchase or

title: Chapter 31: Farewell or Freedom
content:
Chapter 31
Farewell or Freedom Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
Farewell or Freedom
Besides duty, the other operative word for Martin was Keeper, “a really old fashioned term” that he seemed to cherish beyond reason, a word that broadly speaking seemed to denote somebody proudly responsible for maintaining the best interests of Nation and Crown. If this is true, then Martin had supremely fulfilled his obligation. If not for his tenacious diligence and iron grip on his tiny part of the or rather the Turner kingdom of artworks, things would have been much different. Well, at least there would be a lot more Turners around to compete with Tate Gallery primacy.
It is also certain that his calling was based more on a cultural orientation than the law of some great conspiratorial society. But regardless of the will or fancy of it all, the repercussion of such an insular system is likely to be without the proper checks and balances to preserve Turner artworks for their own sake. Martin had been a dedicated soldier for the nation for forty-five years, and likely, without fully realizing the source of his motivation or ultimate goal. Frances had previously volunteered with confidence, “We are very middle class.” And when it was suggested to Martin that he might one day become Sir Martin, again volunteered that he had “done nothing for it.” Surely untrue, but likely said in the kindest sense, was possibly just egging him on.
Martin shared a little more Tate scuttlebutt about—as he put it—the somewhat “impatient and short-tempered” Andrew Wilton. According to Martin, had been frustrated recently by being passed over for an administrative position, and was relegated to the library stacks as a researcher of sorts. If Martin was correct about the distain that those in his circle felt for such activity, this was true banishment. Martin consoled him with stories about his own grand adventures to faraway places. One might presume that one day Andrew Wilton will be on the road fulfilling his own duty as Martin had been over the years, to be treated as a celebrity abroad.
Martin: “He’s been sort of side-tracked from being head of a department into being told, ‘You get on with scholarship and leave the administration to somebody else.’ So he’s a bit bitter about that, but it makes him rather a bit more interested in my next trip, to Australia or some far flung place, where one day he’ll also be treated as a celebrity rather than sitting at his desk dealing with the mail.”
Martin admitted being impatient at times, and particularly with certain colleagues from provincial museums. He had his own coy way of avoiding conversations with such people. He would get his poor “young innocent secretary” to lie for him and say that he was out of his office. Much to Martin’s sense of fun, she would coo with trepidation, “Martin, you’ve taught me to lie.”
Martin highlighted the inner workings and insular ideal of English museums, wherein private collectors were purposely excluded. He explained how the top auction houses willingly forsook their lifeblood—the general public. They circumvented fair-trade practices as would be expected from any other firm, by selling privately to museums pictures destined for sale to the public. Select museums such as the Tate apparently have free run to go in and pick and choose long before pieces are ever catalogued. In addition, the museums are able to offer tax incentives to the owners through government for such activities. The deal is called a “private treaty sale” whereby the owner is effectively tempted with tax advantages in order to capitulate. Likely, it should be assumed that those at the top of the museum community bully the auction firms into such practice. These museum officials are often the ones that authenticate or condemn pictures sold by the same auction houses.
Martin: We were always being criticized because we had a bad representation of Scottish pictures [at the Tate], and particularly of Allan Ramsay [1713-84], a very good sensitive portrait painter. This was mainly because whenever anything came up, the Scots always bought it. They must have hundreds stuffed away in the cellars.
Through one of my colleagues having seen one in the offices of Sotheby’s… [Before it came up for sale] we managed to do what we call a “private treaty sale.” Which means that the owner gets a tax advantage and we get it cheaper, and the only person that loses is the…
Again Martin seemed momentarily lost for words, he knew someone was the loser but was unable to articulate the full dimension of such activity. He carried on describing their coup d’état concluding with, “It never was publicly on sale because we got it before it came up for auction. It’s much easier to buy a picture with tax advantage than go to the auction where you get no tax advantage and you’re up against the world!”
It is probably going too far to say that the auction houses are bullied into such nefarious activity. It may have all begun in 1781, when King George III was passing a Christie’s sale near Marlow on the . James Christie hastened to set out some curious ivory chairs for His Majesty’s inspection. The furniture caught the King’s fancy. “Christie’s promptly withdrew the lots from the auction and sold them directly in a royal private-treaty sale. They are in the Royal Collection to this day.” Clearly this was the top of the slippery slope.
The evening ended on an amusing note. Martin’s job had obviously been very rewarding for a person who considered travel a hobby. So much so that he gauged the number of trips abroad by the size of his loose change bag. This great loot bag came out every time Martin was encouraged to go on an excursion. After dumping it on the floor he would go in search of the appropriate coinage to match the country of destination.
Events of the next couple of days in were the dressing on the weeklong adventure, thanks in large part to Dr. Gibson’s and my daughter Sarah’s help in planning activities. There was very little discussion about art. What time was not filled by pre-planned activities (whale watching, high tea at , the Royal BC Museum, antiquing) was filled with food, drink, and fun repartee.
Graham, Martin, Sarah, and sea-lions at Race Rock The whale watching was planned for and everything seemed all set until word came down that the boat was having engine problems. The initial concern was that everyone would have to suit up in waterproof gear and scramble into a high-powered rubber dinghy as an alternative. The group was convinced that, what appeared a bizarre way to track down the whales, might in-and-of-itself be exciting. Alas, the engine was repaired and before long, the captain, naturalist, and twenty or so family-styled adventurers boarded the luxury theatre—a vessel that lorded over the dinghies.
The captain/engine-repairman had done his job well. The powerful engine growled and thrust the smiling faces towards open water—destination—beyond Race Rock. This was where the pod of killer whales was apparently hidden. Frances and the captain were front and centre as the biting sea breeze flailed their hair and garments.
Frances Butlin and captain/engine repairman The captain had no time to change back into his uniform after his greasy ordeal with the engine; however, he made a fine comrade for the majestic Frances as they both perched side-by-side atop the cabin. The only point at which the vital craft seemed diminished, a veritable Raft of the Medusa, was when the dinghies sped by in a mist at break-neck speed, engines roaring behind the passengers who were bound-in like soggy dolls. It was uncertain whether trading blind excitement for comfort and luxury had been the right choice—as it turned out, the dinghies got closer to the whales.
The pretty on-board naturalist gave as much information about the whales and their environs as could be expected within the few hours of the excursion. Her wide-eyed enthusiasm was contagious. At one point she literally had everyone eating out of her hand. After dragging a length of seaweed from the water, she explained that this was the same slippery foil on which the sea lions fed. She entreated that all partake of this gourmet delight. From the salty depths, to the slippery deck and into reluctant mouths, yum! There were friendly barks and groans from the distance as those same sea lions watched the people-circus from atop their slithery Race Rocks.
On the way back to the inner harbour, the trusted craft churned in the mist of the flatulent dinghies. While watching Martin and Frances on the upper deck one could see that they were in their element. Graham and Sarah had been dragged below deck for coffee and to avoid hypothermia. The two limeys held fast, they had apparently found their bliss. reminded Bob of what his English grandmother expressed about his otherwise refined mother: “She’s made of toough stooff!”
This is an excerpt. For the full chapter, read Art World’s Dirty Little Secret.

title: Chapter 32: Portrait of a Hero
content:
Chapter 32
Portrait of a Hero Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
Portrait of a Hero
Great wooden vessel bears roughest of seas, Tossed by the beckoning deep;
While souls near free from mortal distress,
Still fight till their last precious breath. Lost in their toil, despair and exhaustion,
Sheer fright their last dying grip; Aye, an accomplice observer apart, Sits motionless out-but-within. Might that be JMW’s own self-portrait stabilizing the stern of my Rescue boat?
Shipwreck, the Rescue, enlarged detail If yes—why is he there? He is the immediate focus round which the scene is composed; sitting there in his red waistcoat with cravat loosely piled about his neck. At first, the authority and calm demeanour of the figure might bring to mind a courtier, or even a British military officer, observing from his makeshift command post. There he sits, clad in his red tunic, impotent, unable to control the upcoming events. Nature is fully in charge! As we look closer at the collar and lapel, they are not those of a soldier. Where are the obligatory hat and cape? Apparently, the red is only to capture our attention, and so it does. But this is somebody different and apart, somebody that observes us straight on—a fallacious participant wearing an elegant cravat knotted in the fashion of the dandies of his day.
Yes, JMW was an artist seldom seen without his cravat; even as a young man in his early twenties it seemed part of his identity. His good friend, Clara Wheeler, wrote that one day the servant opened the door to a great uproar. JMW was sitting on the ground and the children were winding his ridiculously long cravat round his neck. William Vaughan’s prophetic statement may help define who our eyes meet in the stern of the boat. He wrote that Turner entered fully into the events themselves. He recorded what he had witnessed, not so much by description as by the creation of the pictorial equivalent.
Besides the full white cravat round his neck, the red velvet waistcoat was also part of the artist’s often observed uniform. When Linnel did Turner’s portrait from memory, he was wearing a red velvet waistcoat—a dandy coat with velvet collar. Although private and often reclusive, it seems the artist set himself as a visual target throughout his life. His colleagues saw him, even in late life shine out at Academy meetings in a red velvet waistcoat.
Like a portrait, the quiet expressionless face is carefully composed and familiar. And comparing his body gestures with those around him—integration within the scene is lost. His activity level is in astonishing contrast to those other tormented tangled bodies fighting for survival, or fighting to save others. Those not fighting are near death from exhaustion or passed out from fear.
Wait! Seldom did an artist do a self-portrait frontally. This inconsistency is resolved when looking at JMW’s self-portrait from 1798. It is lightly earlier in date but the face and personal deportment are very similar. JMW disliked his own profile. His two other self-portraits were frontal. He was concerned about his nose.
Rothenstein and Butlin wrote: “the only portrayal of a human being that Turner could look at without embarrassment was himself as seen in his wonderfully sensitive and searching early Self-Portrait.” Jack Lindsay agreed that only once he succeeded in staring someone in the face and plainly putting down what he saw. The face was his own and the result was the early self-portrait out of which he insistently stares. Mayall the photographer, who knew him near the end, mentioned that in speaking, Turner kept his eyes on the ground and did not look his companion in the face.
What about the tangle of doll-like figures? Are they a recurring metaphor? The physiognomies of the individual cast-members in the boat are unmistakable. Instantly recognizable, JMW’s people are as distinctively Turner as Pieter Brueghel’s are distinctively Brueghel or Rubens’s are Rubens. Even Martin could not deny the familiarity of the figures rendered: “There is even a Madonna group as in The Shipwreck.” Madonna’s tragic moment is a metaphor. (detail see here)
The comparison with a Christian Madonna surely goes beyond a trite reference. It is not merely a mother-child bond that you are given to observe, but something much more profound. The contraposta positioning of the one woman’s head and upper torso is surely taken from a medieval or early Renaissance archetype. One example is the Pietà by E. Quarton. The Louvre, .
What about that lone black man in the rescue boat? He is dressed similarly to the other sailors and is teamed up with two others in an epic attempt to save those clinging to a spar. In 1802, this man would have been a slave not an equal as portrayed here, and certainly not a hero. “At the best of times Turner tended to portray his staffage as doltish or ‘low’ mankind. Man, in Turner’s works, is rarely given the opportunity to be heroic.” Why here?

title: Chapter 33: Steamy Chat with Christie's
content:
Chapter 33
Steamy Chat with Christie’s Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
Steamy Chat with Christie’s
Connoisseurship is the thread tying much of Bob’s story together, but there is a proviso—one must beware of the “magic eye.” It certainly serves its master well if left to its own volition, but if there is a political fog in the air one might just as well be blind. Lawrence Gowing, Martin’s admired predecessor at the Tate implored, “When a letter from your girlfriend is dropped through the door, you don’t have to open it and read the name to know who it’s from.”
The legendary Friedlander concisely assailed such a theory when he wrote that coldly analytical scholars make fewer mistakes. And as for Martin, in lieu of any concrete argument against me, he was forced to claim the superiority of his own connoisseurship, and suggest that this should be good enough. But what no one knew during the investigation and heated debate; James Orrock was a preeminent connoisseur when the nomenclature was widely used and an eye was the only defining element of expertise. He wrote: “Turner’s masculine oil-work, such as ‘The Shipwreck’ or ‘ Pier,’ stands forth like a giant on guard over his tender and gem-like drawings. Nothing was too mighty for him, nothing too delicate. As you can see, Orrock was a silver-tongued wizard as well. Photogravure by Walker E. Cockerell Orrock’s position within the art community is exemplified by his relationship to one of the most important private art collectors of his day—Lord Leverhulme. “One who was most influential over his buying tastes was James Orrock, a passionate advocate of British art. Lever bought Orrock’s own collection in its entirety three times…”
Christine was getting ready for her last day of teaching for the school year and Bob was out on the back deck serving (Piggy) her morning orange juice. Christine called Bob to the phone—“someone with an English accent,” she said.
It was Nicolas White from Christie’s! He immediately apologized for his delay in answering. It was , over three months since Bob’s registered letter to him. The telephone conversation was likely meant to be a short one, but by the time the hour was up, we were both exacerbated.
“At the end of the day,” as White implored, Christie’s’ hands were tied. If Martin and his friends down at the Tate agreed that I was not by Turner then Christie’s would not deal with me. He made a feeble attempt to endorse some of their own in-house staff, but this did not go far when Bob apprised him of Martin’s edict whereby, he directed Christie’s staff onto the floor and away from the more scholarly pursuits.
He asked if I had been shown to anyone else—if Sotheby’s had been contacted for example. After a confident “No,” Bob told him that recently and quite mysteriously, a brochure was sent to him from Sotheby’s office in . It listed someone to contact in reference to Turner’s work. Bob went on to assure White that according to Martin, Sotheby’s would certainly contact him over any Turner painting, notwithstanding his professional allegiance to Christie’s. In other words, whether talking to Sotheby’s or Christie’s, one would be speaking to the same pair of ears. At this hint of collusion, and with protective zeal White made an instinctive attempt to distance his firm.
Mr. White tried to condense the issue by asking, “Well, if you were asked: what are the strongest points that favour calling it a Turner, what would they be?” Bob started to list the stylistic ones dealing with my lozenge composition and brushwork—two things that within the first few years of the nineteenth century would point at JMW exclusively. Before he got to the premier scientific points White broke in and said, “Yes, but these are stylistic points and might come up against different opinions.” Martin might have rejoined, “Of course, people can always find reasons for not believing something.”
Mr. White’s incisive question begged the impossible. If fingerprints were not good enough, and reputable provenance was not good enough—in the end, all one is left with is an all-encompassing debate over style—a debate where all factors must be considered. On Bob’s part at least, all had been accounted for.
Again, Bob made an attempt to give the strongest arguments, but this time concentrating on material usage including pigments, and support materials. He appealed, “There are a hundred different supporting points, each of which has been supported over and over again by highly respected scholars. All are equally important to the whole, how could they be narrowed to just one or two points?” The raucous banter had now become hollow silence. After a pause Bob mentioned the support given to me by two art historians Alan Chong and Daphne Lawson. Lawson went so far as to date me by my lozenge composition!
Bob told White that Ruskin had been over to Orrock’s house and had certainly seen me previous to being artfully reproduced for Orrock’s biography. This powerful provenance is self-supporting without considering the other previous owners Sir Charles Robinson, and Thomas Griffith. Mr. White seemed casually interested that my original photogravure was found in the Orrock biography, but seemed more taken with the fact that the twin volumes dwarfed the B & J Catalogue.
Mr. White then advanced the argument that “surely the Tate would be pleased by the discovery of any new Turner.” “On the contrary,” Bob railed, “they have enough to occupy their time trying to unscramble the Turner Bequest.” It is likely perceived by the Tate team that there is a glut of the artist’s work. It would not be to their particular advantage whatsoever. From the time Ruskin first started to unpack those boxes of musty Turner drawings, right up until recent history, there has been concern over cataloguing, organizing and preserving the State’s share of Turner booty. In a petty competitive sort of way, it would be counterproductive to the Tate’s effort and ’s best interest to start finding another stockpile of the artist’s work in a foreign land.
The junior and somewhat naïve staff members at both Christie’s and the Tate Gallery were probably left to believe that merriment would ensue at the discovery of a new Turner. Certainly Christie’s would make a commission, but the British laws of precedence stretch far beyond ordinary parameters. It was Martin who indicated that Mr. White was junior; he was not even able to place him when his name was mentioned. Bob could only assume that he was left to communicate with a buffer employee. It was one step from archives and Jeremy, but was it a step in the right direction?
Bob mentioned that according to Martin’s own confession he had never authenticated a Turner painting brought to him at the Tate (in all the years he was there), and that he feared being sued. White argued that a Tate edict would be one based on unbiased scholarship and could be trusted. Bob protested, “They are bureaucrats first and foremost—out to keep their jobs.” He mumbled afterwards—far from White’s attention, “There are grave legal consequences attached to giving a wrong opinion.” At some point or possibly many times in the past the Tate had disparaged me and killed any chance of an equitable sale. Hypothetically, millions of pounds along with reputations had evaporated. If legal action ensued and the Tate Gallery officials were held to account things could certainly get ugly. Employees beneath such an ominous cloud might be obliged to team-up in order to watch for lightning—and to watch each other’s backs.
Again White suggested going to another firm. Because the provenance came through Christie’s Bob was adamant that there could be no other agent. If Christie’s would not stand behind their own provenance, who else was going to? If Bob and Graham were to “shop me around” (as the expression goes) it would only muddle the issue or at least diffuse Christie’s responsibility. Perhaps this was the wish.
He made one last attempt to give credence to Martin’s standing as a Turner authority by bringing up his “Hand” theory. White was disappointed to learn that it was a highly impeachable target. When Bob prodded whether or not Christie’s “grasped at straws” while gathering provenance (as Martin insisted) Bob was dumbfounded at the reply, “Not always.” He had expected an accusation of blaspheme, not a whisper of contrition. Alas, maybe Martin was right after all, possibly Christie’s provenances were not to be trusted; albeit, that is exactly where many had been gathered for the B & J Catalogue.
Ultimately, Mr. White concurred fully with the point that if I was proven genuine, I would be the most important Turner discovery of the last century. The conversation ended with a somewhat amicable stalemate. White assured Bob that after talking to Martin Butlin again, he would get back to him within the following few days. The extended conversation took place on June 27, 2002. It is now 2005 and Mr. White has not been heard from since.
Had Martin been riding his high horse, ducking confrontations for years already? How was he to be treated by Christie’s, now that his trusty steed had been tempted from its elegant stable in order to jump puddles in ? Could Martin stay on its back in perpetuity, or was he to be slapped to the ground by one of his very own “Hands.”
This is an excerpt. For the full chapter, read Art World’s Dirty Little Secret.

Chapter 34: Just Fears →

title: Chapter 34: Just Fears
content:
Chapter 34
Just Fears Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
Just Fears
Bob’s journal entry…
Private collecting by connoisseurs provides for scholarship outside the strictures of officialdom and encourages research driven by passion alone. Independent study should hold sway over dogmatic policies of a status quo that constricts the lust for knowledge and eventually conceals the truth. Policies and procedure that develop on the basis of self-interest and for political expedience can only undermine good judgment and distort reality. The desire to control one’s environment is natural, and is ever the case in the hierarchal world of governing bureaucracies; but when any authoritative body gains a monopoly in a certain area, that particular area is bound to decay.
In the short term, control can be propped up by force of influence, but eventually, if the regime is controlled entirely by self-interested officials not only will it eventually destroy itself, but also that which it stands to represent. At this point, the management of Turner interests has forced the art of their namesake to suffer immeasurably. The British art establishment have consistently ignored private connoisseurship and denied the rediscovery of lost paintings. Peter Landesman fears a “museum culture defined as much by commerce, politics and academic provincialism as by a commitment to accurate art history.” It is inarguable that authentic Turners have been condemned, shuffled around and possibly destroyed stemming from such contempt for the private collector, independent scholarship, and connoisseurship outside of officialdom. Sadly, this intellectual malady is not isolated to Turnerism, but is prevalent wherever the stakes are greatest, whenever a particular artist or group of artists is considered rare or precious, and of course—valuable. And also where there is the need to protect prices at an artificially high level.
Yet, in sympathy with officialdom, once they support or condemn a painting, they automatically have a legal attachment to it. People will undoubtedly come back for reckoning if the status of an artwork changes. In his ‘heart of hearts’ would Martin Butlin have the heart to make such a big commitment and support a multi-million dollar picture even if he believed it to be absolutely right? What if he had been in some way involved with condemning the picture in the past? He told us that he dreaded courtrooms, considering lawyers “a terror.” Could this be one reason for his adamancy even in the face of indisputable evidence? In light of new European laws, which place greater liability for misrepresenting works of art onto the art merchants, and the inevitable sharing of such liability by any who mistakenly support a wrong attribution, it is understandable that even a brave expert might cower. What if he or she was not even aware of the former status of the artwork under review?
Certainly, many are afraid to change the status of any potentially important work of art because of legal ramifications and getting tangled in lengthy and expensive ballyhoos. If a work was determined to be by Rembrandt and a multi-million-dollar transaction took place because of the attribution, then if at a later time the piece was denounced, somebody would likely be drawn into battle. If a work by Picasso was denounced and later accepted by a different expert, the former owner who had consequently sold it as a “corking dud” (do to the bad appraisal) would be out for blood. Fallout from this might drive the erring expert to cover up mistakes. This largely makes modern connoisseurship, art dealing, collecting—and for that matter, art scholarship, somewhat of an exercise in futility.
One should not understand this as an entirely modern phenomenon confined to an English-speaking world. As noted by J.H. Duveen, litigation was a pervasive worry for him and his Dutch cohorts nearly a century ago. He said he could not be cited as a witness in any notorious law case… “You know what the Dutch Courts are like when it comes to delays and enquiries: I shouldn’t be my own master for two years!”
“Who said what,” is clearly part of the problem. Not to know which authority has made what attribution to establish the status of a work creates a concern that can never be forgotten. Timid bureaucrats protecting their job first and foremost could be the biggest problem in this regard. Those willing to “go to the stake” or at least go to court in support of the truth seem few and far between. We must remember that some public institutions have been refusing to give opinions on works in private hands for over half a century now. If the only accepted authority has a monopoly position and at the same time is responsible to a mute institution, one must ponder the consequences. Give the collector a form letter of indifference, sweep the matter under the rug and hope that they will just go away.
There appears no respite for those that are being damaged by the fainthearted. The only recourse must be to take the problem out of the political realm and apply science with a vengeance. There must be a thrust toward developing more reliable tests and trials in order to mandate acceptance of all relevant evidence by those that maintain control through privileged appointments rather than honest scholarship. Would it work for the injured parties to play the role of martyr and subject themselves to the slow, expensive, and tormenting fuse of litigation in order to smoke the timid out of their ivory towers? The tenured must become more fact oriented than fiction obsessed, and more scientific at the expense of magic intuition. …end journal.
What followed was a report from Martin Butlin on behalf of Christie’s. It tried to refute the evidence supporting an attribution to JMW rather than presenting concrete reasons for rejecting me. Even if there were only stylistic anomalies that could be plied against me, surely these would have been carefully articulated first and foremost.
Martin must have realized the reciprocal risk involved in his present decision. He may have been in a precarious position regardless of whether he supported me or not. I wonder what Frosty (my former owner) would think if he saw his once-condemned picture sell to the Getty Museum for a fortune. How certain would Martin need to be before changing the status of a painting such as me, and particularly if he was implicated in condemning me in the first place?
A recent court case in underlines this concern—E.S.T, Inc. v. Christie’s. When Christie’s old master painting expert Crichton-Stuart misattributed The Entombment of Christ by Annibale Carracci, calling it a student work, the auction firm was sued for “negligence and breach of fiduciary duty on the grounds that Christie’s failed to recognize the painting’s true author and dramatically higher value.” The difference in value placed by the expert, $10,000-$15,000 versus the value of the painting as a Carracci, $300,000 made for litigious fodder. The court’s antagonism towards Christie’s defense caused the auction firm to settle the claim out of court.
Someone in this precarious circumstance might not be willing to jeopardize self, friend, or foe by supporting a piece he had once condemned—even if there was irrefutable evidence subsequently discovered supporting authenticity. It would make more sense for him to put up the battle of his life. If it is true that there was a conflict of interest, why had Martin come to in the first place? Alas, therein is the chafe, by his own confession, he is effectively the only Turner expert who could get such a major Turner painting into a Christie’s or Sotheby’s sale. Without this reference, the painting would always have a cloud of suspicion hanging over it.
The fundamental problem here is that the consumer of art services and the artwork itself are clearly at risk of being damaged, and especially if there is only one authority available to give a fully accepted authentication. This holds true also if there is only one sales venue to get top dollar, for example when auction houses collude effectively to create a single venue. This is a given if the two or three top firms will accept only one authoritative opinion before standing behind a work and promoting it.
How willing might any auction house be to stand behind an important work, or how willing would a consumer be to expend a huge sum without some equally large guarantee? This is a major problem. Imagine paying a million dollars at a good auction house in for a small Manet, only to find out later that the Wildensteins, the venerable art dealers with offices in , , and did not like it! If such a high-level authority questioned the authenticity of the Manet from , there would be turbulent times at the auction house.
In the face of art politics this problem can be capricious. Over a half-century ago, Friedlander was already talking about the impact of this sort of vacillation by the influential:
“Let us say that the expert recognized a picture as a work by Rembrandt. Out of confidence in him somebody acquires it at a high price. Later he arrives at the conclusion that he has made a mistake. Even if his love of truth now overcomes his vanity, he is yet reluctant to harm someone who has believed in him… Most people do not confess their mistake or they try and confuse the hard facts, more particularly as they know from experience that their clients never forget a financial loss.
Has Bob ended his subplot with an antihero of sorts? In the beginning, Martin Butlin was seen as a charming comedic celebrity. Had he become a slapstick villain? A villain, not only because he had condemned Me, but also because he discharged the brilliant connoisseurs: Griffith, Robinson, and Orrock in the process. Although gravely dishonored by Leviathan practices, “like incest in a way,” Friedlander comforts, “There is no choice but optimistically to rely upon the fact

title: Chapter 35: Tribal Instincts
content:
Chapter 35
Tribal Instincts Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
Tribal Instincts
The gentle Amiel, brooding upon his terrace at Geneva, came to more just conclusions about Wagner than did the professional litigants battling nearer Bayreuth, with louder but since forgotten words.
– Henry McBride: The Flow of Art It is fair to say that the sometimes subtle and sometimes not-so-subtle politics and conferred rites at the uppermost level of the art world, beg a moral inquisition. And that the vainglory inherent in what might be considered the most sophisticated of professional endeavors—art historical scholarship—has oftentimes resulted in the neglect of truth. In an effort to maintain their special post, and the status quo, to progress to a position of more influence, to escape litigation, or to control the market, several heavily endowed institutions, privileged individuals, connected dealers, auction houses, and museum officials appear to have been debased. Or they are at least running scared in front of the momentum of forces larger than themselves.
Nicholas Turner, a former curator at a top American museum is now displaced for speaking his mind over the authenticity of art within his own museum’s collection. “I see museums in a completely different light,” he says, “I find the employees caught up in institutional issues, like politics. They’re not studying the material as museum professionals, as independent academics. They don’t produce scholarship. Museum culture has become much more bureaucratic and caught up in entertainment issues and politics. Big exhibitions have become a public entertainment. Spectacle has overwhelmed serious study.” Rather than mentioning the name of the museum in this case, it might be assumed that most museums have skeletons in their closet, or fakes in their basement, and that the focus of art museums has shifted away from Nicholas Turner’s ideal of independent academic study and scholarship.
It seems apparent that officialdom perceives a need to protect their own special interests and promote their somewhat confused ideology before all else. In a voracious quest to control reputations and monopolize their own areas of interest, they often neglect what should be the heart of their purpose: scholarship, honest education, and art preservation. Unfortunately, those who are surely apt to benefit from and contribute to these more altruistic principals are perceived as a threat and discouraged from participation. This situation is of course unacceptable when one compares the contribution made by those motivated more by emotion and curiosity than by ambition, fear, and greed. According to Friedlander: “enthusiastic lovers of art – at the same time mere amateurs – have contributed most and in the best fashion towards artistic reconstruction. Coldly analytical scholars make fewer mistakes; they perform, however, less in the way of positive perception; they discover less, with weaker flair.”
At the command centre of the modern art world, , there is a triumvirate of self-serving groups working in concert. These include: politically powerful aristocrats and bureaucrats, the auction firm group, and a select number of dealers. In fact, some of the last words Bernard Berenson wrote in his lifelong diary broached with melancholy “the aristocratically pyramidal structure of society that Great art serves.”
Working parallel, yet antagonistically toward debasing this pyramidal group were Marxist intellectuals like Sir Anthony Blunt and comrades—those who had rejected the concept of private ownership, and specifically, private ownership of art. Steiner contended that the belief that “great art is not and cannot be private property played a crucial part in crystallizing Blunt’s contemptuous loathing for capitalism.”
Events went contrary to Blunt’s socialist dream of a personally controlled mother-institution full of the best works of art—works that were slated to be confiscated by him and his comrades. Latent sentiments derived from collectivist propaganda have, in the meantime, given ideological control over to the institutional sector. Ironically, the dim light that now emphasizes dubious interpretations via art criticism has enabled the triad—the “pyramid of capitalists” to move in and take command. Clearly, the left-leaning policies that have been spawned within the institutional and curatorial communities today have enabled the era of monopoly control by this triumvirate. The now interred Anthony Blunt should roll in despair.
Of the winners that moved into the void created by this quiet revolution of docile socialist educators, one might firstly envision elite members of the Invisible group, presided over by the Duke of . Has the egalitarian nature of allied brethren been co-opted to preserve the most elite of British society? The Duke and this club of the most influential British aristocrats, surly to this day, influence key institutions by means of concomitant allegiances and the laws of noble precedence. Near and dear to art interests, are the National Heritage Memorial Fund, National Art-Collections Fund, and the National Trust: which in turn coddle and hold sway over The National Gallery, British Museum, V&A, the National Portrait Gallery, and the Tate Gallery.
Other victors are the closely-related auction firms with their enduring grasp on the art market at the uppermost level. Finally, there is the limited group of influential dealers whose cooperation assists their overseers both financially and politically. This development is the only way that the market has been able to fly as high as it has to the benefit of the select few. One should consider the result an evolutionary one, a tyranny of precedence and dutiful capitulation as opposed to a conspiracy against guys like Bob. The intentions of the Invisibles are likely of the highest order, opposing “all forms of personal and religious injustice.” But alas, the impact on the art world and harm to the common collector might be the irreconcilable rub!
One must also credit stealthy business practices for the present state of affairs. Christie’s and Sotheby’s for example have undeniably gained much of their market position from a keen and effective marketing strategy: glitzy catalogues and annual reviews, exclusive direct mailers, presale tours of artwork sent between international venues, and public media campaigns orchestrated by staging auctions that predict and purport to deliver record multi-million-dollar sale prices. I’ve heard Bob complain that some of these records are likely illegitimate…. A great marketing strategy though!
With control set firmly with an elite few, one might only hope for a good underlying value-system…
Art for art’s sake is really awful rot,” declared Sotheby’s long-term chairman Peter Wilson, “he would go on to preach a little sermon on the utility of greed… Peter Wilson divided the world into those who wished to appear richer than they were, and those who knew it was smart to seem poorer, and he practiced the auction business as the exploitation of the former by the latter… It was a complex amorality which drove Peter Wilson to generate, almost single-handedly, a new sort of international business that was dominated by the English.
Ultimately it is the way in which the benefits of such domination are allocated within this milieu that needs unraveling.
It has long been accepted that Sotheby’s and Christie’s have been served by employees from the “polite” class: those who are more likely to have access to noble households and ancestral collections, and those that move more comfortably within privileged circles. British peers are often awarded top administrative positions in the museum world and certain dealers are patronized and operate according to ‘tribal’ customs in support of the influential. Put together, this creates an intangible support system that becomes exclusionary. When issues pertaining to death duties, inheritance tax, or treaty sales of important property are at stake (as they often are in aristocratic circles) it becomes imperative to have friends in the right places. A relationship in the spirit of this statement might be the one between David Westmoreland, Chairman of Sotheby’s, who was at the same time, Master of the Horse and Lord-in-Waiting to the Queen.
The tight controls needed to direct transactions according to precedence are well taken care of. Hugh Roberts of Christie’s gave a list of participants: “lawyers, accountants, politicians, heritage lobbyists, dealers, treasury officials, Christie’s, National Trust, and above all the Heritage Fund,” Accordingly, negotiated sales take place well beyond the circumstances of the middle class. When Roberts strung together the above list of individuals with their, “discreet behind the scenes lobbying,” to accommodate the sale of those greatest English country houses and their collections – Kedleston Hall, Nostell Priory and Weston Park, he was in effect waving a red flag at all but the elite.
His article on the subject was sprinkled with earls, viscounts and lords. In accordance with the new-age business model, which attempts to limit competition by increasing the size and scope of its operations, the leaders of the art world appear to be following suit by amalgamating the professionals, select dealers, and experts into structured compliance to support those with influence. At the same time, it is imperative that they exclude interlopers, those that might pose a threat including the independent collector, unconnected dealer, small auction firms, and minor museums. Although there are chants to the contrary, most decision making is not meant firstly to benefit art, culture, or humanity.
In the British antique trade, it is common knowledge that ‘blackballing’ competitors, dishonestly condemning their inventory, and conspiring at auctions to control prices has been going on for years.

title: Chapter 36: A Juggler's Paradise
content:
Chapter 36
A Juggler’s Paradise Excerpt from Art World’s Dirty Little Secret by R. Malcolm Setters / Graham Setters
A Juggler’s
In the case of second tier artworks—ones that are less core to the prestige of nations, quasi-monopolistic control is orchestrated by less influential groups. Seldom does one individual have the financial resources to promote and control the market of higher-priced artists independently. In the modern era, only one internationally-based dealer comes to mind, and that is Wildenstein and Co. But with the second tier and especially within niche markets, there can be both individuals and groups that affect the market as an oligopoly might. Most these participants are not graduates of renowned business schools or products of stock market and financial circles as the famous dealers Kahnweiler, and Rosenberg, or financiers such as William Weinberg, or investment bankers such as Charles Angrand (who would move from collector status to become financial director of Sotheby’s), but in their own awkward way the less powerful still affected their own small sector of the art market.
The collecting-cataloguing-marketing schema was no better exemplified than by the Sotheby’s sale of the American Weinberg collection. J.B. De la Faille had catalogued and effectively closed the book on van Gogh’s oeuvre (Weinberg was the first major collector under this cataloguer’s tutelage), In the meantime, Sotheby’s, by means of exuberant marketing had already started the upward spiral for works by modern masters. By the time they brought the Weinberg artworks back to from the , they were worth many-fold what they had been at time of purchase, several sold for record-breaking prices. The art market has not looked back or downward since. But this has little to do with the second tier. Producing a complete catalogue raisonné for an artist of the second tier is seldom cost-effective, unless of course, a bigger long-term scheme is planned.
The third class of art is often referred to as decorative. Although these artists are “listed” quality artists, they are subject to the vagaries of the free market system and are traded freely. It is this type of art that is open to just about anyone to trade, buy, and sell; and pieces usually change hands from a paltry few thousand dollars, to several thousand. There is rarely a dispute over authorship because the stakes are low. If one traces the life history of artists such as John Delisle Parker, Jiří Kaiser, Gerald Roach, and Zeljko Kujundzic it is easily seen how these third-tier artists are undervalued—in fact, unjustly neglected.
If a major work by an artist such as van Gogh, Picasso, Rembrandt, Pollock, Monet, or Turner were to sell for under 100,000 dollars, such a low price would shake the market. Conversely, if a painting by an artist sold for double what might be expected, then the overall price level for the artist’s work would rise. When this happens one might justifiably suspect market manipulation.
According to one newspaper report: in the mid-1920s sensational and also suspect bids, under the aegis of commissaires-priseur Alphonse Bellier, established several previously little-known artists. The winners here, of course, were the artists themselves and the dealers who had presumably stockpiled their works. There was a tradition attached to the Hotel Drouot where Bellier worked, concerning the sales of antiques. “It portrayed a mysterious gang of dealers rigging all the auctions (the bande noire). The notion was adapted to interpret sales of modern art—the dealers were accused of buying at inflated prices, paintings by artists whose work they had in stock.”
Could the idea of bidding up prices also be used to enhance the reputation of the auction house where such a record-breaking painting sold? Certainly! Has it happened? Yes—in a roundabout way at least. In 1956, the innovative chairman of Sotheby’s, Peter Wilson, fiercely fought to secure for sale at auction an important painting by Nicolas Poussin rather than having it sell through a dealer as it would normally have done at that time. In order to secure the sale, he promised a guaranteed sale price. The painting sold for less than the guarantee, but although Sotheby’s had to pay the difference, a record price was made for the year. This was revolutionary, and the ensuing publicity drew more artwork into the auction arena. Reports of high and record-breaking prices have been fuel for the twin auction firms’ marketing strategy ever since.
Had the world of fine art—around which cultural identity is formed, become little more than a “cash cow” for the influential? The two top European dealers, Kahnweiler and Rosenberg, had trained in commerce, in commodities in , , and . In , July 1915, Tony Tollett delivered a lecture at the , and Literature and Arts dealing with the corporate influence of a few dealers in French art. “He said that these dealers quickly prospered by cultivating wealthy collectors, by buying all the works of an artist in order to speculate with full control over the stock, and by falsely inflating the prices. He blamed them for having influenced and bribed critics and having started art magazines in order to better control the market.”
The modern business model and exploitation that can accompany such practice was being applied to the world of art and apparently with great pecuniary reward. With Kahnweiler, we are first led to assume that his venture into collecting art as a young man was altruistic and mainly driven by an innocent passion for his subject. It might be interesting to rationalize this in light of his introduction to the world of finance under the aegis of his maternal uncles, who had specialized in buying and selling precious metals for over a century, and enjoyed a considerable fortune. “They could best be described as bankers, speculators, entrepreneurs or brokers.” In they quickly acquired a position on the stock market and controlled gold and diamond mines in . After being sponsored by those same family members, Kahnweiler opened a gallery in . The way he was to apply his ancestral wit in controlling the earliest bits of his own art-world empire—Braque, Derain, Vlaminck, and van Dongen—was described by Assouline: “When he decided to buy he bought everything… Exclusivity was the basis of his actions and the only rule he would never forsake:”
To the delight of both Kahnweiler and Picasso, with a 1960 exhibition held at the Tate Gallery in , they were ostentatiously gathered into the fold of the omnipotent triumvirate. “Roland Penrose organized the most complete exhibition ever held on a living artist at the Tate Gallery, naturally Kahnweiler was in attendance and gave Picasso a detailed report as his special envoy: the number of rooms, the distance between paintings, the chronology of the hanging, the names of the dealers Sotheby’s had invited, the opening party held in a tent set up in front of the Tate with paella, flamenco dancers, and art critics singing his praises. Everything was there.”
As a result, there would be no looking back for Picasso, or at his prices—he was now a “made” artist. The pivotal influence of those aforementioned cognoscenti is somewhat veiled here, but it is fair to assume that the British auction firms, dealers and allied curators were collaborating to the utmost.
An even more worrisome unnatural pressure on art prices happened within the last couple of decades. There was an enormous price-spike for Impressionist and modern paintings, this time as unintended consequences. It is mentioned here in order to show how, no matter what side of the economics equation price pressures come from, the art establishment is in the wings to guide matters in an orderly direction. This major price-spike was driven by unmanageable demand as opposed to intentional limitations on supply. Thousands of expensive impressionist and modern paintings made their way to as a way to offer bribes during real estate dealings. Obviously the pressure of this abrupt increase in demand impacted prices internationally, “they not only rose sharply; they rose madly as well.” But this artificial price increase was not the worst of it. Works were sold, then immediately bought back for many times their value in order to secretly bribe people or at least surreptitiously transfer funds during land deals.
With the stock market downturn in 1987 and subsequent real estate market collapse in , these over-valued paintings were redirected into the hands of creditors who wanted rid of them. “When Michael Ainslie later analyzed the top twenty Japanese buyers at Sotheby’s in the years 1984-92, he discovered that seventeen of them were either bankrupt, in jail, or under serious investigation.” Christie’s and Sotheby’s have made it clear that if these paintings were dumped over a short period of time the art market would collapse. These paintings still sit in waiting. Surely the appropriate gatekeepers will ensure an orderly reintegration for some of those forsaken masterpieces notwithstanding the suspicious rumors that are already circulating over the authenticity of many of those works!
Maurice Rheims’ encounter with Peter Wilson of Sotheby’s in 1954 involved the sale of King Farouk’s collection. , a top commissaires-priseurs at the time, said that “ was no neutered pet, but a formidable tom. He threw a blockade round and made the turntable of the art market.” One effective part of the process was to open a office in the 1970s. The international positioning of the firm was further enhanced with the acquisition of . From that point onward offices were opened worldwide.
Most recently there has been a challenge to Wildenstein’s primacy in the world of Impressionist painting. This dealt with a Manet. In 1997, Sotheby’s refused to sell the painting, which had been fully authenticated and catalogued by Wildenstein. Suzanna Andrews joins the chorus about “the growing dominance of the auction

title: Albrecht Durer
content:
Durer, Albrecht Durer, art expert, Lucas Cranach the Elder, Albrecht Durer art expert, National Gallery London, Alistair Smith, Alistair…JMW Turner the source
St. Christopher circa 1497 (shown here subsequent to the 1988 assessment. (It was professionally conserved, with the removal of a substantial amount of over-painting). oil on panel/laid down on canvas, 33 x 23.7cm (this small size is consistent with the artist’s early work) from the Rudolph Hermani estate (Krupp/Benz notoriety) Vintage monogram A over D, lower left (in the style of Albrecht D rer) Attributed to Lucas Cranach the Elder (Alistair Smith, 1988)
presumable based on a print by this artist showing the child
surmounting the Saint similarly. Essay to follow showing many stylistic comparables
Three examples:
1-The comparison with D rer’s unusual face studies
In a unique effort D rer experimented with distinctive facial types 2 - the positioning of the infants legs (drawing 1495),
3 - Quarry study showing D rer’s interest in taking unusual landscape features from nature
watercolour on paper 1497 SAINT CHRISTOPHER MARTYR Feast: July 25 From the great mass of legendary material, often confused and contradictory, which is associated with the name of St. Christopher, there emerges one clear conception. It is that of a man who is strong, simple, kind, and completely dedicated to one thing: serving the Lord by serving his fellow men. Christopher, according to the ancient and very popular tradition, lived in the province of Lydia, Asia Minor, during the reign of the Emperor Decius. He was a man of enormous size and strength, who had been converted to Christianity by a holy hermit. Having no gift for preaching, fasting, or prayer, the customary practices of the good Christian, he searched for some other way of showing his love of God. An inspiration came to him. He went to a certain stream whose current was so dangerous that travelers were often swept away while trying to ford it. Here Christopher built a hut for shelter, then stationed himself on the bank, and carried across all who came, a sort of human ferry. After he had been laboring in this way for some time, a little child appeared one day, and asked to be carried. Christopher lifted the child in his great arms, placed him on his shoulder, and started across, staff in hand. At every step the load grew more burdensome, and Christopher came near losing his balance in the rushing water. On reaching the other bank, he put the boy down, saying, “Child, thou hast put me in dire peril, and hast weighed so heavily on me that if I had borne the whole weight of the world upon my shoulders it could not have burdened me more heavily.” The boy answered, “Wonder not, Christopher, for not only hast thou borne the whole world on thy shoulders, but Him who created the world”’ “for the Christ Child, bearing in His own arms the great world, had been Christopher’s burden. To prove that this was true, He told Christopher to recross the river and plant his staff in the ground beside his hut and soon it would burst into bloom. Obeying, Christopher was amazed to see this occur, and then he knew how wonderfully he had been favored. This miracle brought about the conversion of many in those parts, but it aroused the wrath of the pagan king, who had Christopher imprisoned, tortured, and beheaded. In the Golden Legend are to be found other stories of the saint which were current in medieval times. Christopher is loved and honored in the churches both of the East and the West. In addition to being the patron and protector of all travelers, he is also invoked against storms and sudden death. In art Christopher is usually shown with his emblems, the Christ Child, a tree in bloom, a torrent; the great artists Durer and Pollaiuolo are among those who have portrayed him in the act for which he is best known. This was taken from “Lives of Saints”, Published by John J. Crawley & Co., Inc.
Art World’s Dirty Little Secret
